sections.label: Irrational Numbers (IX-1.2)
sections.html:
A number 's' is called an irrational number if it cannot be written in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0{/tex}.

The decimal expansion of an irrational number is non-terminating non-recurring. Moreover, a number whose decimal expansion is non-terminating non-recurring is known as irrational.

A number 's' is called an irrational number if it cannot be written in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0{/tex}.

एक अपरिमेय संख्या एक प्रकार की वास्तविक संख्या होती है जिसे एक साधारण भिन्न के रूप में प्रदर्शित नहीं किया जा सकता है। इसे अनुपात के रूप में व्यक्त नहीं किया जा सकता है। यदि N अपरिमेय है, तो N, p/q के बराबर नहीं है जहाँ p और q पूर्णांक हैं और q 0 के बराबर नहीं है।

एक अपरिमेय संख्या एक प्रकार की वास्तविक संख्या होती है जिसे एक साधारण भिन्न के रूप में प्रदर्शित नहीं किया जा सकता है। इसे अनुपात के रूप में व्यक्त नहीं किया जा सकता है। यदि N अपरिमेय है, तो N, p/q के बराबर नहीं है जहाँ p और q पूर्णांक हैं और q 0 के बराबर नहीं है।

Definition
An irrational number is a number that cannot be expressed in the form {tex} \frac{p}{q} {/tex} where {tex}{p}{/tex} and {tex}{q}{/tex} are integers and {tex}{q} \neq 0{/tex}. It has non-terminating and non-repeating decimal expansion. Examples: {tex} \sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \pi, e, 0.1010010001 \ldots, \text { etc. } {/tex}

Examples of Irrational Numbers - {tex}\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}{/tex}, etc. (since they can't be expressed as {tex}p / q{/tex} ) - {tex}\pi=3.1415926 \ldots{/tex} - {tex}{e}=2.7182818 \ldots{/tex} - Non-terminating, non-repeating decimals like 0.1010010001 ...

Proof of Irrationality (Example) To prove {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational:
1. Assume {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is rational {tex}\rightarrow \sqrt{2}=p / q{/tex}, where {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} have no common factor.
2. Squaring both sides: {tex}2=p^2 / q^2 \rightarrow p^2=2 q^2{/tex}. {tex}\Rightarrow p^2{/tex} is even {tex}\rightarrow p{/tex} is even {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} let {tex}p=2 r{/tex}.
3. Substitute: {tex}(2 r)^2=2 q^2 \rightarrow 4 r^2=2 q^2 \rightarrow q^2=2 r^2 \rightarrow q{/tex} is also even. But both {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} even {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} contradiction. Hence, {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational.

Operations with Irrational Numbers - Sum or difference of two irrational numbers may be rational or irrational. e.g., {tex}(\sqrt{2}+(-\sqrt{2}))=0{/tex} (rational) - Product of two irrational numbers may be rational or irrational. e.g., {tex}(\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2})=2{/tex} (rational)

Representation on Number Line To represent {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} :
1. Draw a right triangle with both sides {tex}=1{/tex} unit.
2. Hypotenuse {tex}=\sqrt{ }\left(1^2+1^2\right)=\sqrt{2}{/tex}. 3. Use compass to mark that length on the number line starting from 0 . (Similarly, {tex}\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}{/tex}, etc. can be represented.)

Key Points to Remember -
{tex}\sqrt{ } {n}{/tex} is irrational if {tex}{n}{/tex} is not a perfect square. - Sum or product of a rational and irrational number is always irrational (except special cases like {tex}0 \times \sqrt{2}=0{/tex} ). - The set of real numbers (R) includes both rational and irrational numbers.

sections.label: Real Numbers and Their Decimal Expansions (IX-1.3)
sections.html:
All the rational and irrational numbers make up the collection of real numbers.

The decimal expansion of a rational number is either terminating or non-terminating recurring. Moreover, a number whose decimal expansion is terminating or non-terminating recurring is a rational.

sections.label: Representing Real Numbers on the Number Line (IX)
sections.html: There is a unique real number corresponding to every point on the number line. Also, corresponding to each real number, there is a unique point on the number line.

sections.label: Operations on Real Numbers (IX-1.4)
sections.html:
To rationalise the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}+b}{/tex}, we multiply and divide this by {tex}{\sqrt{a}-b}{/tex}, where a and b are integers.

If r is a rational and s is an irrational, then r + s and r – s are irrational numbers, and rs and {tex}\frac{r}{s}{/tex} are irrational numbers, {tex}r \neq 0{/tex}.

sections.label: Laws of Exponents for Real Numbers (IX-1.5)
sections.html:
Let a, b > 0 be a real number and p be rational numbers. Then
apbp = (ab)p

Let a > 0 be a real number and p and q be rational numbers and p > q. Then
{tex}\frac{a^{p}}{a^{q}} = a^{p-q}{/tex}

Let a > 0 be a real number and p and q be rational numbers. Then
(ap)q = apq

Let a > 0 be a real number and p and q be rational numbers. Then
ap{tex}\cdot{/tex} aq = ap + q

For positive real numbers a and b, the identity hold:
{tex}(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^{2}{/tex} = {tex}a+2 \sqrt{a b}+b{/tex}

For positive real numbers a and b, the identity hold:
{tex}(a+\sqrt{b})(a-\sqrt{b}){/tex} = a2 - b

For positive real numbers a and b, the identity hold:
{tex}(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b}){/tex} = a - b

For positive real numbers a and b, the identity hold:
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{a}{b}}=\frac{\sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{b}}{/tex}

For positive real numbers a and b, the identity hold:
{tex}\sqrt{a b}=\sqrt{a} \sqrt{b}{/tex}

sections.label: Polynomials in One Variable
sections.html:
A quadratic polynomial can have at most 2 zeroes and a cubic polynomial can have at most 3 zeroes.

Polynomials of degrees 1, 2 and 3 are called linear, quadratic and cubic polynomials respectively.

A polynomial of degree three is called a cubic polynomial.

A polynomial of degree two is called a quadratic polynomial.

A polynomial of degree one is called a linear polynomial.

A polynomial of three terms is called a trinomial.

Examples : 2x^2 + 2x + 4  etc

A polynomial of two terms is called a binomial.

Examples : 2x+1 , x^2 - 4 , 3 +2y etc

A polynomial of one term is called a monomial.

Examples: 2x, 3, y, 3x^2 etc

sections.label: Zeros of A Polynomial
sections.html:
Every linear polynomial in one variable has a unique zero, a non-zero constant polynomial has no zero, and every real number is a zero of the zero polynomial.

A real number ‘a’ is a zero of a polynomial p(x) if p(a) = 0. In this case, a is also called a root of the equation p(x) = 0.

sections.label: Remainder Theorem (IX)
sections.html: Remainder Theorem : If p(x) is any polynomial of degree greater than or equal to 1 and p(x) is divided by the linear polynomial x – a, then the remainder is p(a).

sections.label: Factorisation of Polynomials (IX-2.3)
sections.html: Factor Theorem : x – a is a factor of the polynomial p(x), if p(a) = 0. Also, if x – a is a factor of p(x), then p(a) = 0.

sections.label: Algebraic Identities (IX-2.4)
sections.html:
x3 + y3 + z3 – 3xyz = (x + y + z) (x2 + y2 + z2 – xy – yz – zx)

(x - y)3 = x3 - y3 - 3xy(x - y)

(x + y)3 = x3 + y3 + 3xy(x + y)

(x + y + z)2 = x2 + y2 + z2 + 2xy +2yz + 2zx

sections.label: Revision Notes
sections.html:
The coordinates of a point

  • The coordinates of a point on the x-axis are of the form (x, 0)
  • The coordinate of a point on the y-axis are of the form (0, y).

The distance of a point

  • The distance of a point from the y-axis is called its x-coordinate, or abscissa.
  • The distance of a point from the x-axis is called its y-coordinate, or ordinate.

Mirror image of point

Point About axis Mirror Image
(a, b) X (a, -b)
(a, b) Y (-a, b)
(a, b) origin (-a, -b)
(-a, b) X (-a, -b)
(-a, b) Y (a, b)
(-a, b) origin (a, -b)
(-a, -b) X (-a, b)
(-a, -b) Y (a, -b)
(-a, -b) origin (a, b)
(a, -b) X (a, b)
(a, -b) Y (-a, -b)
(a, -b) origin (-a, b)

Plotting of points in the Cartesian plane can be compared to some extent with drawing of graphs in different situations such as Time-Distance Graph, Side-Perimeter Graph, etc.

  • If x ≠ y, then (x, y) ≠ (y, x)
  • If x = y, then (x, y) = (y, x)

If point lie on X-axis or Y-axis, it does not lie in any quadrant. Coordinates of A (2,3),  B(-3.5,-1.5)

OX′ and OY′ are called the negative directions of the x - axis and the y - axis, respectively.

Since the positive numbers lie on the directions OX and OY, OX and OY are called the positive directions of the x - axis and the y - axis, respectively.

If a point is in the 4th quadrant, then the point will be in the form (+, –), since the 4th quadrant is enclosed by the positive x - axis and the negative y – axis.

If a point is in the 3rd quadrant, then the point will be in the form (–, –), since the 3rd quadrant is enclosed by the negative x - axis and the negative y - axis.

If a point is in the 2nd quadrant, then the point will be in the form (–, +), since the 2nd quadrant is enclosed by the negative x - axis and the positive y - axis.

If a point is in the 1st quadrant, then the point will be in the form (+, +), since the 1st quadrant is enclosed by the positive x - axis and the positive y - axis.

If the abscissa of a point is x and the ordinate is y, then (x, y) are called the coordinates of the point. The coordinates of a point on the x-axis are of the form (x, 0)

  • The coordinates of a point on the y-axis are (0, y).
  • The coordinates of the origin are (0, 0).

The coordinate axes divide the plane into four parts called quadrants.

  • The point of intersection of the axes is called the origin.
  • The distance of a point from the y - axis is called its x-coordinate, or abscissa
  • The distance of the point from the x-axis is called its y-coordinate, or ordinate.

The horizontal line is called the x -axis, and the vertical line is called the y - axis.

Coordinate geometry is the branch of Mathematics in which we study the position of any object lying in a plane, with the help of two mutually perpendicular lines in the same plane.

If x {tex}\neq{/tex} y, then (x, y) {tex}\neq{/tex} (y, x), and (x, y) = (y, x), if x = y.

The coordinates of a point are of the form (+, +) in the first quadrant, (-, +) in the second quadrant, (-, -) in the third quadrant and (+, -) in the fourth quadrant, where + denotes a positive real number and - denotes a negative real number.

The coordinates of the origin are (0, 0).

The coordinates of a point on the x-axis are of the form (x, 0) and that of the point on the y-axis are (0, y).

If the abscissa of a point is x and the ordinate is y, then (x, y) are called the coordinates of the point.

The distance of a point from the y-axis is called its x-coordinate, or abscissa, and the distance of the point from the x-axis is called its y-coordinate, or ordinate.

The point of intersection of the axes is called the origin.

The coordinate axes divide the plane into four parts are known as quadrants.

The horizontal line is also called the x-axis, and the vertical line is also called the y-axis.

The plane is called the Cartesian, or coordinate plane and the lines are called the coordinate axes.

To locate the position of an object or a point in a plane, we require two perpendicular lines. One of them is horizontal, and the other is vertical.

sections.label: Linear Equations (IX-4.1)
sections.html:
An equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b and c are real numbers, such that a and b are not both zero, is called a linear equation in two variables.

For Example: 2x +3y-5=0

sections.label: Solution of a Linear Equation (IX-4.2)
sections.html: A linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions.

sections.label: Graph of a Linear Equation in Two Variables (IX)
sections.html:
Each solution (x, y) of a linear equation in two variables, ax + by + c = 0, corresponds to a point on the line representing the equation, and vice versa.

An equation of the type y = mx represents a line passing through the origin.

The graph of y = a is a straight line parallel to the x-axis.

The graph of x = a is a straight line parallel to the y-axis.

x = 0 is the equation of the y-axis and y = 0 is the equation of the x-axis.

The graph of every linear equation in two variables is a straight line.

sections.label: Euclid's Definitions, Axioms and Postulates (IX-5.1)
sections.html:
Euclid’s postulate were:
All right angles are equal to one another.

Euclid’s postulate were:
A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius.

Euclid’s postulate were:
A terminated line can be produced indefinitely.

Euclid’s postulate were:
A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.

Euclid’s axiom were:
Things which are halves of the same things are equal to one another.

Euclid’s axiom were:
Things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.

Euclid’s axiom were:
The whole is greater than the part.

Euclid’s axiom were:
Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.

Euclid’s axiom were:
If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.

Euclid’s axiom were:
If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.

Euclid’s axiom were:
Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.

Theorems are statements which are proved, using definitions, axioms, previously proved statements and deductive reasoning.

Axioms or postulates are the assumptions which are obvious universal truths. They are not proved.

Though Euclid defined a point, a line, and a plane, the definitions are not accepted by mathematicians. Therefore, these terms are now taken as undefined.

sections.label: Equivalent Versions of Euclid's Fifth Postulate (IX) FA ONLY
sections.html:
All the attempts to prove Euclid’s fifth postulate using the first 4 postulates failed. But they led to the discovery of several other geometries, called non-Euclidean geometries.

Two equivalent versions of Euclid’s fifth postulate are:

  1. 'For every line l and for every point P not lying on l, there exists a unique line m passing through P and parallel to l '.
  2. Two distinct intersecting lines cannot be parallel to the same line.

Euclid’s postulate were:
If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side of it taken together less than two right angles, then the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which the sum of angles is less than two right angles.

sections.label: Intersecting Lines and Non-Intersecting Lines (IX-6.1)
sections.html:
If two lines intersect each other, then the vertically opposite angles are equal.

 Nazi-Propoganda:

sections.label: Pairs of Angles (IX-6.1)
sections.html: If a ray stands on a line, then the sum of the two adjacent angles so formed is 180o and vice-versa. This property is called as the Linear pair axiom.

sections.label: Parallel Lines and a Transversal (IX) FA ONLY
sections.html:
If a transversal intersects two lines such that, either any one pair of interior angles on the same side of the transversal is supplementary, then the lines are parallel.

If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of interior angles on the same side of the transversal is supplementary.

If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of alternate interior angles is equal.

If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of corresponding angles is equal.

sections.label: Lines Parallel to the Same Line (IX-6.2)
sections.html: Lines which are parallel to a given line are parallel to each other.

sections.label: Angle Sum Property of a Triangle (IX) FA ONLY
sections.html:
If a side of a triangle is produced, the exterior angle so formed is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles.

The sum of the three angles of a triangle is 180o.

sections.label: Congruence of Triangles (IX-7.1)
sections.html:
If two triangles ABC and PQR are congruent under the correspondence A {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} P, B {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} Q and C {tex}\leftrightarrow{/tex} R, then symbolically, it is expressed as {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQR.

Two squares of the same sides are congruent.

Two circles of the same radii are congruent.

Two figures are congruent, if they are of the same shape and of the same size.

sections.label: Criteria for Congruence of Triangles (IX-7.1)
sections.html:
If three sides of one triangle are equal to three sides of the other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent (SSS Congruence Rule).

If two angles and one side of one triangle are equal to two angles and the corresponding side of the other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent (AAS Congruence Rule).

If two angles and the included side of one triangle are equal to two angles and the included side of the other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent (ASA Congruence Rule).

If two sides and the included angle of one triangle are equal to two sides and the included angle of the other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent (SAS Congruence Rule).

sections.label: Some Properties of a Triangle (IX-7.2)
sections.html:
Sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.

In a triangle, side opposite to the larger (greater) angle is longer.

In a triangle, angle opposite to the longer side is larger (greater).

Each angle of an equilateral triangle is of 60o.

Sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are equal.

Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal.

sections.label: Some More Criteria for Congruence of Triangles (IX-7.3)
sections.html: If in two right triangles, hypotenuse and one side of a triangle are equal to the hypotenuse and one side of other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent (RHS Congruence Rule).

sections.label: Properties of a Parallelogram (IX-8.1)
sections.html:
A quadrilateral is a parallelogram, if

  1. opposite sides are equal and parallel
  2. opposite angles are equal
  3. diagonals bisect each other

In a parallelogram,

  1. opposite sides are equal
  2. opposite angles are equal
  3. diagonals bisect each other

A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles.

sections.label: The Mid-Point Theorem (IX-8.2)
sections.html:
A line through the mid-point of a side of a triangle parallel to another side bisects the third side.

The line-segment joining the mid-points of any two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and is half of it.

revision.chapter: Areas of Parallelograms and Triangles (FA Only)

sections.label: Figures on the Same Base and Between the Same Parallels (IX-9.1)
sections.html:
Two figures are said to be on the same base and between the same parallels, if they have a common base (side) and the vertices, (or the vertex) opposite to the common base of each figure lie on a line parallel to the base.

If a planar region formed by a figure T is made up of two non-overlapping planar regions formed by figures P and Q, then ar (T) = ar (P) + ar (Q), where ar (X) denotes the area of figure X.

Two congruent figures have equal areas but the converse need not be true.

The area can be defined as the space occupied by a flat shape or the surface of an object. The area of a figure is the number of unit squares that cover the surface of a closed figure. The area is measured in square units such as square centimeters, square feet, square inches, etc.

sections.label: Parallelograms on the Same Base and Between the Same Parallels (IX-9.2)
sections.html:
Parallelograms on the same base (or equal bases) and having equal areas lie between the same parallels.

Area of a parallelogram is the product of its base and the corresponding altitude.

Parallelograms on the same base (or equal bases) and between the same parallels are equal in area.

sections.label: Triangles on the Same Base and Between the Same Parallels (IX-9.3)
sections.html:
A median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal areas.

Triangles on the same base (or equal bases) and having equal areas lie between the same parallels.

Area of a triangle is half the product of its base and the corresponding altitude.

Triangles on the same base (or equal bases) and between the same parallels are equal in area.

If a parallelogram and a triangle are on the same base and between the same parallels, then area of the triangle is half the area of the parallelogram.

sections.label: Circles and Its Related Terms: a Review (IX)
sections.html: A circle is the collection of all points in a plane, which are equidistant from a fixed point in the plane.

sections.label: Angle Subtended by a Chord at a Point (IX-9.1)
sections.html:
If the angles subtended by two chords of a circle (or of congruent circles) at the centre (corresponding centres) are equal, the chords are equal.

Equal chords of a circle (or of congruent circles) subtend equal angles at the centre.

sections.label: Perpendicular from the Centre to a Chord (IX-9.2)
sections.html:
The line drawn through the centre of a circle to bisect a chord is perpendicular to the chord.

The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.

sections.label: Circle Through Three Points (IX) FA ONLY
sections.html: There is one and only one circle passing through three non-collinear points.

sections.label: Equal Chords and their Distances from the Centre (IX-9.2)
sections.html:
Chords equidistant from the centre (or corresponding centres) of a circle (or of congruent circles) are equal.

Equal chords of a circle (or of congruent circles) are equidistant from the centre (or corresponding centres).

sections.label: Angle Subtended by an Arc of a Circle (IX-9.3)
sections.html:
Angle in a semicircle is a right angle.

Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal.

The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle.

Congruent arcs of a circle subtend equal angles at the centre.

If two arcs of a circle are congruent, then their corresponding chords are equal and conversely if two chords of a circle are equal, then their corresponding arcs (minor, major) are congruent.

sections.label: Cyclic Quadrilaterals (IX-9.3)
sections.html:
If sum of a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is 180o, the quadrilateral is cyclic.

The sum of either pair of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180o.

If a line segment joining two points subtends equal angles at two other points lying on the same side of the line containing the line segment, the four points lie on a circle.

sections.label: Basic Constructions (IX-11.1)
sections.html:
To bisect a given angle.

Example: Given an angle ABC, we want to construct its bisector.

1. Taking B as centre and any radius, draw an arc to intersect the rays BA and BC, say at E and D respectively

 2. Next, taking D and E as centres and with the radius more than half DE, draw arcs to intersect each other, say at F.

3. Draw the ray BF . This ray BF is the required bisector of the angle ABC.

To draw the perpendicular bisector of a given line segment.

Example: Given a line segment AB,  construct its perpendicular bisector. 

1. Taking A and B as centres and radius more than half of AB, draw arcs on both sides of the line segment AB (to intersect each other).

2. Let these arcs intersect each other at P and Q. Join PQ .

3. Let PQ intersect AB at the point M. Then line PMQ is the required perpendicular bisector of AB.

To construct an angle of 60o etc.

1. Taking A as centre and some radius, draw an arc of a circle, which intersects AB, say at a point D.

2. Taking D as centre and with the same radius as before, draw an arc intersecting the previously drawn arc, say at a point E.

3. Draw the ray AC passing through E . Then ∠ CAB =600

sections.label: Some Constructions of Triangles (IX-11.2)
sections.html:
To construct a triangle given its base, a base angle and the sum of the other two sides.

1. Draw the base BC and at the point B make an angle, say XBC equal to the given angle.

2. Cut a line segment BD equal to AB + AC from the ray BX.

3. Join DC and make an angle DCY equal to ∠BDC.

4. Let CY intersect BX at A . Then, ABC is the required triangle.

To construct a triangle given its base, a base angle and the difference of the other two sides.

1. Draw the base BC and at point B make an angle say XBC equal to the given angle.

2. Cut the line segment BD equal to AB – AC from ray BX.

3. Join DC and draw the perpendicular bisector, say PQ of DC.

4. Let it intersect BX at a point A. Join AC. Then ABC is the required triangle.

To construct a triangle given its perimeter and its two base angles.

1. Draw a line segment, say XY equal to BC + CA + AB.

2. Make angles LXY equal to ∠B and MYX equal to ∠C.

3. Bisect ∠ LXY and ∠ MYX. Let these bisectors intersect at a point A 

4. Draw perpendicular bisectors PQ of AX and RS of AY. Let PQ intersect XY at B and RS intersect XY at C. Join AB and AC .

sections.label: Area of a Triangle - by Heron's Formula (IX-10.1)
sections.html:
Area of a triangle with its sides as a, b and c is calculated by using Heron’s formula, stated as
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
where {tex}s =\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}

sections.label: Application of Heron's Formula in Finding Areas of Quadrilaterals (IX)
sections.html: Area of a quadrilateral whose sides and one diagonal are given, can be calculated by dividing the quadrilateral into two triangles and using the Heron’s formula.

sections.label: Surface Area of a Cuboid and a Cube (IX) FA ONLY
sections.html:
Surface area of a cube = 6a2
where a is the edge of the cube.

Surface area of a cuboid = 2(lb + bh + hl)
where l, b and h are respectively the three edges of the cuboid.

sections.label: Surface Area of a Right Circular Cone (IX-11.1)
sections.html:
Total surface area of a right circular cone = {tex}\pi {/tex}rl + {tex}\pi {/tex}r2, i.e., {tex}\pi {/tex}r(l + r)

Curved surface area of a cone = {tex}\pi{/tex}rl
where r is its base radius and l its slant height.

sections.label: Surface Area of a Sphere (IX-11.2)
sections.html:
Total surface area of a hemisphere = 3{tex}\pi{/tex}r2

Curved surface area of a hemisphere = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
where r is the radius of the sphere of which the hemisphere is a part.

Surface area of a sphere of radius r is ,  A= 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
where r is the radius of the sphere.

sections.label: Volume of a Right Circular Cone (IX-11.3)
sections.html:
Volume of a cone = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex}
where r is the base radius and h is the height of the cone.

sections.label: Volume of a Sphere (IX-11.4)
sections.html:
Volume of a hemisphere = {tex}\frac{2}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}
where r is the radius of the hemisphere.

Volume of a sphere of radius r = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}
where r is the radius of the sphere.

sections.label: Revision Notes
sections.html: How data can be presented graphically in the form of bar graphs, histograms and frequency polygons.

sections.label: Probability – An Experimental Approach (IX-15.1)
sections.html:
The probability of an event E is a number P(E) such that
0 {tex}\leq{/tex} P(E) {tex}\leq{/tex} 1

The probability of an impossible event is 0.

The probability of a sure event (or certain event) is 1.

The Probability of an event lies between 0 and 1 (0 and 1 inclusive).

The empirical (or experimental) probability P(E) of an event E is given by
{tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{E})=\frac{\text { Number of possible outcomes } \mathrm{} \text { }}{\text { Total number of outcomes }}{/tex}

sections.label: Probability — A Theoretical Approach (X-14.1)
sections.html:
For any event E, P(E) + ({tex}\bar{E}{/tex}) = 1, where {tex}\bar{E}{/tex} stands for 'not E'. E and {tex}\bar{E}{/tex} are called complementary events.

An event having only one outcome is called an elementary event. The sum of the probabilities of all the elementary events of an experiment is 1.

The theoretical (classical) probability of an event E, written as P(E), is defined
{tex}P(E)=\frac{\text { Number of outcomes favourable to } E}{\text { Number of all possible outcomes of the experiment }}{/tex}
where we assume that the outcomes of the experiment are equally likely.

An event for an experiment is the collection of some outcomes of the experiment.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

Every whole number is a natural number.

Q 2

Every integer is a rational number.

Q 3

Every rational number is an integer.

Q 4

Find five rational numbers between 1 and 2.

Solution

Recall that to find a rational number between r and s, you can add r and s and divide the sum by 2, that is {tex}\frac{r+s}{2}{/tex} lies between r and s. So, {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} is a number between 1 and 2. You can proceed in this manner to find four more rational numbers between 1 and 2. These four numbers are {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{11}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{13}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7}{4}{/tex}. 
Thus five rational numbers between 1 and 2 are {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{11}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{13}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7}{4}{/tex}. 

Q 5

Locate {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} on the number line.

Solution

Consider a square OABC, with each side 1 unit in length (see Fig.).

Then you can see by the Pythagoras theorem that OB = {tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}.
Transfer Fig. onto the number line making sure that the vertex O coincides with zero (see Fig.)

We have just seen that OB = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}.
Using a compass with centre O and radius OB, draw an arc intersecting the number line at the point P.
Then P corresponds to {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} on the number line.

Q 6

Locate {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}  on the number line.

Solution

Let point A represents 1 as shown in Figure.
Clearly, {tex}OA = 1 \ unit.{/tex}
Now, draw a right triangle OAB in which {tex}AB = OA = 1\ unit.{/tex}
By Using Pythagoras theorem, we have
{tex}OB^2 = OA^2 + AB^2 \\= 1^2 + 1^2{/tex}
{tex}= 2 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow O B=\sqrt{2}{/tex}

Taking O as centre and OB as a radius draw an arc intersecting the number line at point P.
Then p corresponds to {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} on the number line. Now draw DB of unit length perpendicular to OB.
By using Pythagoras theorem, we have
{tex}OD^2 = OB^2 + DB^2{/tex}
OD2 = {tex}(\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex} + 12
{tex}= 2 + 1 = 3{/tex}
OD = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
Taking O as centre and OD as a radius draw an arc which intersects the number line at the point Q.
Clearly, Q corresponds to {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}.

Q 7

Find the decimal expansions of {tex}\frac{10}{3},\frac{7}{8}{/tex} and {tex} \frac{1}{7}{/tex}.

Solution


{tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex}, we found that the remainder becomes zero and the decimal expansion of {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex} = 0.875.
We call the decimal expansion of such numbers terminating.
{tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex} , we notice that the remainders repeat after a certain stage forcing the decimal expansion to go on for ever. In other words, we have a repeating block of digits in the quotient. We say that this expansion is non-terminating recurring. 

Q 8

Show that 3.142678 is a rational number. In other words, express 3.142678 in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} , where p and q are integers and q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.

Solution

We can write given number as,

3.142678 = {tex}\frac{3142678}{1000000}{/tex}, and hence is a rational number.
Now, let us consider the case when the decimal expansion is non-terminating recurring.

Q 9

Show that 0.3333... = {tex}0.\overline 3{/tex} can be expressed in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.

Solution

We have to expressed 0.3333... = {tex}0.\overline 3{/tex}  in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.
Let x = 0.3333... ----(i)
Multiplying eq (i) by 10, we get
10 x = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.333...) = 3.333...----(ii)
Subtracting eq (i) from (ii)
10x - x = 3.333... - .333...
9x = 3, i.e., x = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

Q 10

Show that 1.272727 = {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex} can be expressed in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.

Solution

The given number= 1.272727....= {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex}
Let x = {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex}. Then,
x = 1.272727.......(i)
Multiplying both sides by 100 we get
100 x = 127.272727......(ii)
On subtracting (i) from (ii), we getLet x = {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex}. Then,
99 x = (127.272727...) - (1.272727....) =127-1
99 x = 126
 {tex}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm x\;=\frac{126}{99}=\frac{14}{11}\\\end{array}{/tex}
Therefore,  {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex} = 1.272727..={tex}\frac {14}{11} = \frac pq{/tex}
Hence {tex}1. \overline {27}{/tex} can be expressed in {tex}\frac pq{/tex} form where p=14 and q=11

Q 11

Show that 0.2353535... = {tex}0. 2\overline{35}{/tex} can be expressed in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and q {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.

Solution

Let x = 0.235... ---- (i)
Multiplying both sides by 10
10x = 2.35....-----(ii)
Multiplying both sides by 100
1000x = 235.35....----(iii)
Subtracting (ii) from (iii)
1000x - 10x = 235.35...- 2.35...
990x = 233
x = {tex}\frac{233}{990}{/tex}  

Q 12

Find an irrational number between {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{7}.{/tex}

Solution

Given numbers are {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex}and {tex}\frac{2}{7}.{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{7}=0.142857142857 \ldots{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{7}=0 . \overline{142857}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2}{7}=2 \times \frac{1}{7}=0.285714285714 \ldots{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{7}=0 . \overline{285714}{/tex}
To find irrational numbers between {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex}and {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}, we find numbers which are non-terminating and non-repeating.
There are infinitely many such numbers lying between {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex}and {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}. 
Examples: 

  1. 0.150150015000150000 ...
  2. 0.250250025000250000 ... 
    etc..
Q 13

Check whether {tex}7 \sqrt{5}, \frac{7}{\sqrt{5}}, \sqrt{2}+21, \pi-2{/tex} are irrational numbers or not.

Solution

Recall that

  1.  {tex}\sqrt{p}{/tex} is always an irrational number where p is prime number.
  2. Sum of a rational and irrational number is always irrational.

Thus
{tex}7 \sqrt{5} , \frac{7}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} + 21, {tex}\pi{/tex} – 2 
So, all these are irrational numbers.

Q 14

Add {tex}2 \sqrt{2}+5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{2}-3 \sqrt{3}{/tex}.

Solution

We have {tex}(2 \sqrt{2}+5 \sqrt{3})+(\sqrt{2}-3 \sqrt{3})=(2 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2})+(5 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3}){/tex} = {tex}(2+1) \sqrt{2}+(5-3) \sqrt{3}=3 \sqrt{2}+2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

Q 15

Multiply {tex}6 \sqrt{5}{/tex} by {tex}2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}.

Solution

we have..{tex}6 \sqrt{5} \times 2 \sqrt{5}=6 \times 2 \times \sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{5}=12 \times 5=60{/tex}

Q 16

Divide {tex}8 \sqrt{15} {/tex} by {tex} 2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}.

Solution

{tex}8 \sqrt{15} \div 2 \sqrt{3}=\frac{8}{2} \sqrt{\frac{15}{3}}=4 \sqrt{5}{/tex}.

Q 17

Simplify the expression: (5 + {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex}) ( 2 + {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex})

Solution

It is given that, 
(5 + {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex}) (2 + {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}) [opening both the brackets using distributive property] 
= (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + (5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}) + ( {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + ( {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex})  
= 10 + 5{tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} + 2{tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{7 \times 5}{/tex} 
= 10 + 5{tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} + 2{tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{35}{/tex}

Q 18

Simplify the expression: (5 + {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}) (5 - {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex})

Solution

{tex}(5-\sqrt{5})(5+\sqrt{5}){/tex}
According to the formula a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b) = (5)2 - ({tex}\sqrt{5})^{2}{/tex}
So we get
= 25 - 5
= 20
Hence (5-{tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}) (5+{tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}) is rational.

Q 19

Simplify the expression: ({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex})2

Solution

The given expression is:
({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex})2 
= ({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex})2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} + ({tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex})2  [{tex}\because{/tex} (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]
= 3 + 2{tex}\sqrt{21}{/tex} + 7
{tex}=10+2\sqrt{21}{/tex}

Q 20

Simplify the expression: {tex}(\sqrt{11}-\sqrt{7})(\sqrt{11}+\sqrt{7}){/tex}

Solution

we have.

{tex}(\sqrt{11}-\sqrt{7})(\sqrt{11}+\sqrt{7}){/tex} = {tex}(\sqrt{11})^{2}-(\sqrt{7})^{2}{/tex} = 11 - 7 = 4

Q 21

Rationalise the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

Solution


{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} (Rationalize the denominator)

{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} {/tex}     (since {tex}\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 2)
In this form, it is easy to locate {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} on the number line. It is halfway between 0 and {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

Q 22

Rationalise the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{3}}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{2-\sqrt{3}}{/tex} (Rationalise the denominator)

       {tex}=\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{4-3}{/tex} [using a2 - b2 = (a+b)(a-b)]

      {tex}=2-\sqrt{3}{/tex}

Q 23

Rationalise the denominator of {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{5}}{/tex}

Solution

Let {tex}y=\frac{5}{\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{5}}{/tex} and its denominator = {tex}\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{5}{/tex}
Here, the conjugate of denominator {tex}(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{5}){/tex} is {tex}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5}).{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{5}{\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{5}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5}}{/tex} [by rationalising)
{tex}=\frac{5(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5})}{(\sqrt{3})^{2}-(\sqrt{5})^{2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} (a-b)(a+b)=a2 - b2]
{tex}=\frac{5(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5})}{3-5}=-\frac{5}{2}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{5}){/tex}

Q 24

Rationalise the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{7+3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

Solution

we have..

{tex}\frac{1}{7+3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{7+3 \sqrt{2}} \times\left(\frac{7-3 \sqrt{2}}{7-3 \sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{7-3 \sqrt{2}}{49-18}=\frac{7-3 \sqrt{2}}{31}{/tex}

Q 25

Simplify: {tex}2 ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \cdot 2 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}2 ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \cdot 2 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex}

We know that  {tex}a ^ { m } \cdot a ^ { n } = a ^ { ( m +n) }{/tex}.

So, {tex}2 ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \cdot 2 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } = ( 2 ) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex}

{tex}= ( 2 ) ^ { \frac { 2+1} { 3} }\\ = ( 2 ) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 3 } }\\=(2)^1\\=2{/tex}

Therefore, the value of  {tex}2 ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \cdot 2 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }{/tex} will be  {tex}2{/tex}.

Q 26

Simplify : {tex}\left(3^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)^{4}{/tex}

Solution

we have

{tex}\left(3^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)^{4}=3^{\frac{4}{5}}{/tex}

Q 27

Simplify : {tex}\frac{7^{\frac{1}{5}}}{7^{\frac{1}{3}}}{/tex}

Solution

We have,

{tex}\frac{7^{\frac{1}{5}}}{7^{\frac{1}{3}}}=7^{\left(\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{3}\right)}=7^{\frac{3-5}{15}}=7^{\frac{-2}{15}}{/tex}

Q 28

Simplify : {tex}13^{\frac{1}{5}} \cdot 17^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex}

Solution

We have,

{tex}13^{\frac{1}{5}} \cdot 17^{\frac{1}{5}}=(13 \times 17)^{\frac{1}{5}}=221^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex}

sections.label: Exercise 1.1
sections.html:
Q 1

Is zero a rational number? Can you write it in the form {tex}\frac { p } { q }{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0{/tex}?

Solution

Consider the definition of a rational number.
A rational number is the one that can be written in the form of {tex}\frac { p } { q }{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0{/tex}.
Zero can be written as {tex}\frac { 0 } { 1 } , \frac { 0 } { 2 } , \frac { 0 } { 3 } , \frac { 0 } { 4 } , \frac { 0 } { 5 }.....{/tex}
So, we arrive at the conclusion that 0 can be written in form of {tex}\frac { p } { q }{/tex}, where q is any integer. Therefore, zero is a rational number.

Q 2

Find six rational numbers between 3 and 4.

Solution

We know that there are infinite rational numbers between any two numbers. A rational number is the one that can be written in the form of {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \ne 0{/tex} We know that the numbers all lie between 3 and 4. We need to rewrite the numbers in {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} form to get the rational numbers between 3 and 4. So, we cover it in{tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}

{tex}\eqalign{ & {3 \over 1} = {3 \over 1} \times {{10} \over {10}} = {{30} \over {10}} \cr & {4 \over 1} = {4 \over 1} \times {{10} \over {10}} = {{40} \over {10}} \cr} {/tex}

So any six number between {tex}{{30} \over {10}},{{40} \over {10}}{/tex}will be the answer example {tex}{{31} \over {10}},{{33} \over {10}},{{34} \over {10}},{{35} \over {10}},{{37} \over {10}},{{38} \over {10}}{/tex}

Q 3

Find five rational numbers between {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}.

Solution

We know that there are infinite rational numbers between any two numbers. A rational number is the one that can be written in the form of {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} where p and q are Integers and {tex}q \ne 0{/tex} We know that the numbers {tex}\frac{3}{5}{\text{ and }}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} can also be written as.0.6 and 0.8 or 

{tex}\eqalign{ & {3 \over 5} = {3 \over 5} \times {{20} \over {20}} = {{60} \over {100}} \cr & {4 \over 5} = {4 \over 5} \times {{20} \over {20}} = {{80} \over {100}} \cr}{/tex}

We can conclude that the numbers all lie between We need to rewrite the numbers {tex}0.61,0.62,0.63,0.64{\text{ and }}0.65{/tex} in {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} form to get the rational numbers between {tex}\frac{3}{5}{\text{ and }}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} . So, after converting, we get {tex}\frac{{61}}{{100}},\frac{{62}}{{100}},\frac{{63}}{{100}},\frac{{64}}{{100}}{\text{ and }}\frac{{65}}{{100}}{/tex} We can further convert the rational numbers {tex}\frac{{62}}{{100}},\frac{{64}}{{100}}{\text{ and }}\frac{{65}}{{100}}{/tex} into lowest fractions.On converting the fractions, we get {tex}\frac{{31}}{{50}},\frac{{16}}{{25}}{\text{ and }}\frac{{13}}{{20}}{/tex} Therefore, five rational numbers between {tex}\frac{3}{5}{\text{ and }}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}are {tex}\frac{{61}}{{100}},\frac{{31}}{{50}},\frac{{63}}{{100}},\frac{{16}}{{25}}{\text{ and }}\frac{{13}}{{50}}{/tex}

Q 4

Every natural number is a whole number.

Q 5

Every integer is a whole number.

Q 6

Every rational number is a whole number.

sections.label: Exercise 1.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Every irrational number is a real number.

Q 2

Every point on the number line is of the form {tex}\sqrt m{/tex}, where m is a natural number.

Q 3

Every real number is an irrational number.

Q 4

Are the square roots of all positive integers irrational? If not, give an example of the square root of a number that is a rational number.

Solution

We know that square root of every positive integer will not yield an integer.We know that {tex}\sqrt 4 {/tex} is 2, which is an integer. But, {tex}\sqrt 7 {\text{ or }}\sqrt {10} {/tex} will give an irrational number. Therefore, we conclude that square root of every positive integer is not an irrational number.

Q 5

Show how {tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex} can be represented on the number line.

Solution

According to the Pythagoras theorem, we can conclude that {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 } \right)^2} = {\left( 2 \right)^2} + {\left( 1 \right)^2}{/tex} We need to draw a line segment AB of 1 unit on the number line. Then draw a straight line segment BC of 2 units. Then join the points C and A, to form a line segment BC. Then draw the arc ACD, to get the number {tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex} on the number line.

Q 6

Classroom activity (Constructing the ‘square root spiral’) : Take a large sheet of paper and construct the ‘square root spiral’ in the following fashion. Start with a point O and draw a line segment OP1 of unit length. Draw a line segment P1 P2 perpendicular to OP1 of unit length (see Fig.). Now draw a line segment P2 P3 perpendicular to OP2. Then draw a line segment P3 P4 perpendicular to OP3. Continuing in this manner, you can get the line segment Pn - 1 Pn by drawing a line segment of unit length perpendicular to OPn–1. In this manner, you will have created the points P2, P3,...., Pn,... ., and joined them to create a beautiful spiral depicting {tex}\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{4}{/tex}, ...

Fig. : Constructing square root spiral

Solution

For the square root spiral follow the given steps:

  1. Draw a line AB of length 1 unit.
  2. Draw another line BC {tex}\perp{/tex} AB of length 1 unit.
  3. Now, Join point AC . Here, AC represents a line of length {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} units. (This can be easily found using Pythagoras Theorem right {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}
  4. Now, Draw a perpendicular CD of length 1 unit at point C and join points A and D. AD here represents length {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
  5. Similarly proceeding further we get Square Root Spiral.

sections.label: Exercise 1.3
sections.html:
Q 1

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: {tex}\frac{{36}}{{100}}{/tex}

Solution

On dividing 36 by 100, we get
 
So, the answer is 0.36,  which is a terminating decimal.

Q 2

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: {tex}\frac{1}{11}{/tex}

Solution


{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac {1} {11}{/tex} = 0.090909 ... = {tex}0 . \overline{09}{/tex}
The decimal expansion is non-terminating recurring.

Q 3

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: 4{tex}\frac {1} {8}{/tex}

Solution

4{tex}\frac {1} {8}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times 8+1}{8}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{32+1}{8}=\frac{33}{8}{/tex}
 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4{tex}\frac {1} {8}{/tex} = 4.125
The decimal expansion is terminating.

Q 4

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: {tex}\frac{3}{{13}}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{3}{{13}}{/tex} On dividing 3 by 13, we get

We can observe that while dividing 3 by 13 we got the remainder as 3, which will continue to be 3 after carrying out 6 continuous divisions.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}\frac{3}{{13}} = 0.230769.....{\text{ or }}\frac{3}{{13}} = 0.\overline {230769} {/tex}, which is a non-terminating decimal and recurring decimal.

Q 5

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: {tex}\frac{2}{{11}}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{2}{{11}}{/tex} On dividing 2 by 11, we get

We can observe that while dividing 2 by 11, first we got the remainder as 2 and then 9, which will continue to be 2 and 9 alternately.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}\frac{2}{{11}} = 0.1818.....{\text{ or }}\frac{2}{{11}} = 0.\overline {18}{/tex} , which is a non-terminating decimal and recurring decimal.

Q 6

Write in decimal form and say what kind of decimal expansion: {tex}\frac{329}{400}{/tex}

Solution


{tex}\frac{329}{400}{/tex} = 0.8225
The decimal expansion is terminating.

Q 7

You know that {tex}\frac{1}{7}=0 . \overline{142857}{/tex}. Can you predict what the decimal expansions of  {tex}\frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 3 } { 7 } , \frac { 4 } { 7 } , \frac { 5 } { 7 } , \frac { 6 } { 7 }{/tex} are, without actually doing the long division? If so, how?

Solution

Yes, We can predict the decimal expansions of {tex}\frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 3 } { 7 } , \frac { 4 } { 7 } , \frac { 5 } { 7 } , \frac { 6 } { 7 }{/tex}, without actually doing the long division as follows :
{tex}\frac { 2 } { 7 } = 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 7 } = 2 \times 0 . \overline { 142857 } = 0 . \overline { 285714 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 3 } { 7 } = 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 7 } = 3 \times 0 . \overline { 142857 } = 0 . \overline { 428571 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 4 } { 7 } = 4 \times \frac { 1 } { 7 } = 4 \times 0 . \overline { 142857 } = 0 . \overline { 571428 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 5 } { 7 } = 5 \times \frac { 1 } { 7 } = 5 \times 0 . \overline { 142857 } = \overline{0.714285}{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 6 } { 7 } = 6 \times \frac { 1 } { 7 } = 6 \times 0 . \overline { 142857 } = 0 . \overline { 857142 }{/tex}

Q 8

Express {tex}0 . \overline{6}{/tex} in the form {tex}\frac {P} {q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and q {tex}\neq{/tex} 0.

Solution

Let x = {tex}0 . \overline{6}{/tex} = 0.6666 ...
Multiplying both sides by 10 (since one digit is repeating), we get
10x = 0.666 ...
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x = 6 + 0.6666
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x = 6 + x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x  x = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}0 . \overline{6}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Here p = 2
q = 3({tex}\neq{/tex}0)

Q 9

Express {tex} 0.4 \overline{7}{/tex} in the form {tex}\frac {p} {q}{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0 \;{/tex}

Solution

Let x = {tex}0.4 \overline{7}{/tex} = 0.47777 ...
Multiplying both sides by 10 (since one digit is repeating), we get
10x = 4.7777 ...
⇒ 10x = 4.3 + 0.47777 ...
⇒ 10x = 4.3 + x
⇒ 10x - x = 4.3
⇒ 9x = 4.3
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{4.3}{9}=\frac{43}{90}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}4 . \overline{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{43}{90}{/tex}
Here   p = 43
q = 90({tex}\neq{/tex}0)

Q 10

Express {tex}0. \overline { 001 }{/tex} in the form {tex}\frac { p } { q }{/tex}, where p and q are integers and {tex}q \neq 0 {/tex}

Solution

Let x = {tex}0. \overline { 001 }{/tex} = 0.001001001 . . . .
Multiplying both sides by 1000 (since three digits are repeating), we get
1000x = 1.001001 . . . .
⇒ 1000x =  1+ 0.001001001 . . . .
⇒ 1000x = 1 + x
⇒ 1000x – x = 1
⇒ 999x = 1
⇒ {tex}x = \frac { 1 } { 999 }{/tex}
Thus {tex}0 . \overline { 001 } = \frac { 1 } { 999 }{/tex}
Here  p = 1
q = {tex}999\left( { \ne 0} \right){/tex}

Q 11

Express 0.99999.... in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex}. Are you surprised by your answer? With your teacher and classmates discuss why the answer makes sense.

Solution

Let x = 0.99999.... (a) 
We need to multiply both sides by 10 to get 10x = 9.9999.... (b)
We need to subtract (a) from (b), to get {tex}\begin{gathered} {\text{10}}x = 9.99999..... \hfill \\ \underline {\, - x = 0.99999....} \hfill \\ \,\,9x = 9 \hfill \\ \end{gathered}{/tex}
We can also write 9x = 9 as {tex}x = \frac{9}{9}{/tex} or x = 1
Therefore, on converting {tex}0.99999...{/tex} in the {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} form ,we get 1.
Hence the answer is 1 and it make sense because 0.999… goes on forever so there is no gap between 1 and 0.9999… and hence they are equal .

Q 12

What can the maximum number of digits be in the repeating block of digits in the decimal expansion of {tex}\frac{1}{{17}}{/tex}? Perform the division to check your answer.

Solution

We need to find the number of digits in the recurring block of {tex}\frac{1}{{17}}{/tex}
Let us perform the long division to get the recurring block of {tex}\frac{1}{{17}}{/tex} We need to divide 1 by 17, to get

We can observe that while dividing 1 by 17 we got the remainder as 1, which will continue to be 1 after carrying out 16 continuous divisions. Therefore, we conclude that
{tex}\frac{1}{{17}} = 0.{\text{0588235294117647}}.....{\text{ or }}{/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{{17}} = 0.\overline {{\text{0588235294117647}}}{/tex}, which is a non-terminating decimal and recurring decimal.

Q 13

Look at several examples of rational numbers in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}(q \neq 0){/tex}, where p and q are integers with no common factors other than 1 and having terminating decimal representations (expansions). Can you guess what property q must satisfy?

Solution

Let {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex},{tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex},{tex}\frac{2}{125}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{13}{20}{/tex} etc which has terminating decimal expansion . For example:
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}={tex}\frac{1}{2^1}{/tex}=0.5 , denominator q = 21
{tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}={tex}\frac{1}{2^2}{/tex} = 0.25  denominator q = 22
{tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} ={tex}\frac{5}{2^3}{/tex} = 0.625 , denominator q = 23
{tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{17}{5^2}{/tex} = 0.68 , denominator q = 52
{tex}\frac{2}{125}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{2}{5^3}{/tex} = 0.016, denominator q = 53
{tex}\frac{13}{20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{13}{2^2\times 5}{/tex} = 0.65, denominator q = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
It can be observed that the terminating decimal can be obtained in a condition where prime factorization of the denominator (i.e. q) of the given fractions has 2 or the power of 2 or 5 or power of 5 or both.

Q 14

Write three numbers whose decimal expansions are non-terminating non-recurring.

Solution

0.01001 0001 00001 . . . .,
0.20 2002 20003 200002 . . . .,
0.003000300003 . . . .,

Q 15

Find three different irrational numbers between the rational numbers {tex}\frac {5} {7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac {9} {11}{/tex}.

Solution

To find irrational numbers, first we will divide 5 by 7 and 9 by 11.
Thus, {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex} = 0.714285... = {tex}0 . \overline{714285}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{9}{11}{/tex} = 0.8181.... = {tex}0 . \overline{81}{/tex}
That means the required irrational numbers will lie between {tex}0 . \overline{714285}{/tex} and {tex}0 . \overline{81}.{/tex}
Also, the irrational numbers have non-terminating non-repeating decimals.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required three numbers are:
0.73073007300073000073...,
0.7650765007650007650000...
and 0.80800800080000...

Q 16

Classify the number {tex}\sqrt { 23 }{/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution


Thus, {tex}\sqrt { 23 }{/tex} = 4.795831523......
{tex}\because{/tex} The decimal expansion is non-terminating non-recurring.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\sqrt { 23 }{/tex} is an irrational number.

Q 17

Classify the number {tex}\sqrt {225} {/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution


{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\sqrt {225}{/tex} = 15 = {tex}\frac {15} {1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\sqrt {225}{/tex} is a rational number.
Here p = 15
q = {tex}1( \neq 0){/tex}

Q 18

Classify the number 0.3796 as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}\therefore{/tex} The decimal expansion is terminating.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.3796 is a rational number.

Q 19

Classify the number 7.478478... as rational or irrational.

Solution

7.478478... =  {tex}7.\overline{478}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} The decimal expansion is non-terminating recurring.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 7.478478... is a rational number.

Q 20

Classify the number 1.101001000100001... as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} The decimal expansion is non-terminating non-recurring.
1.101001000100001... is an irrational number.

sections.label: Exercise 1.4
sections.html:
Q 1

Classify the number 2 - {tex}\sqrt {5}{/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution

2 - {tex}\sqrt {5}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} 2 is a rational number and{tex}\sqrt {5}{/tex} is an irrational number.
({tex}\because{/tex} The difference of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational.)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}2 - {tex}\sqrt {5}{/tex} is an irrational number.

Q 2

Classify the number {tex}(3+\sqrt{23})-\sqrt{23}{/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}(3+\sqrt{23})-\sqrt{23}{/tex}= {tex}3+\sqrt{23}-\sqrt{23}{/tex} = 3 is a rational number.

Q 3

Classify the number {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{7}}{7 \sqrt{7}} {/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{7}}{7 \sqrt{7}}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex} is a rational number

Q 4

Classify the number {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} 1({tex}\neq{/tex} 0) is a rational number and{tex}\sqrt{2}( \neq 0){/tex} is an irrational number.
({tex}\because{/tex} The quotient of a non-zero rational number with an irrational number is irrational.)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is an irrational number.

Q 5

Classify the number 2{tex}\pi{/tex} as rational or irrational.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} 2 is a rational number and {tex}\pi{/tex}  is an irrational number.
({tex}\because{/tex} The product of a non-zero rational number with an irrational number is irrational)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}  is an irrational number.

Q 6

Simplify the expression:
{tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex}

Solution

{tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex}
We need to apply distributive law to find value of {tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right) = 3\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right) + \sqrt 3 \left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex} = 6 + 3\sqrt 2 + 2\sqrt 3 + \sqrt 6 {/tex}
Therefore, on simplifying{tex} \left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {2 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex},we get {tex}6 + 3\sqrt 2 + 2\sqrt 3 + \sqrt 6 {/tex}

Q 7

Simplify the expression:
{tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}

Solution

{tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}
We need to apply distributive law to find value of {tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex} {tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right) = 3\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right) + \sqrt 3 \left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 9 - 3\sqrt 3 + 3\sqrt 3 - 3{/tex}
= 6
Therefore, on simplifying {tex}\left( {3 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\left( {3 - \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}, we get 6.

Q 8

Simplify the expression:
{tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}
We need to apply the formula {tex}{\left( {a + b} \right)^2} = {a^2} + 2ab + {b^2} \ to \ find\ value\ of \ {\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}
{tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2} = {\left( {\sqrt 5 } \right)^2} + 2 \times \sqrt 5 \times \sqrt 2 + {\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}
{tex} = 5 + 2\sqrt {10} + 2{/tex}
 {tex}= 7 + 2\sqrt {10}{/tex}
Therefore, on simplifying {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex} , we get {tex}7 + 2\sqrt {10} {/tex} Therefore, on simplifying {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}, we get {tex}7 + 2\sqrt {10}{/tex}

Q 9

Simplify the expression: {tex}(\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}){/tex}

Solution

{tex}\left( {\sqrt 5 - \sqrt 2 } \right)\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex} We need to apply the formula {tex}\left( {a - b} \right)\left( {a + b} \right) = {a^2} - {b^2}{/tex} to find value of {tex}{\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}

{tex} (\sqrt 5 - \sqrt 2)(\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2){/tex}

= {tex} [(\sqrt 5)^2 - (\sqrt 2)^2]{/tex}

= 5 - 2 = 3
Therefore, on simplifying {tex}\left( {\sqrt 5 - \sqrt 2 } \right)\left( {\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 } \right){/tex},we get 3.

Q 10

Recall, {tex}\pi{/tex} is defined as the ratio of the circumference (say c) of a circle to its diameter (say d). That is, {tex}\pi = \frac{c}{d}{/tex}. This seems to contradict the fact that {tex}\pi{/tex} is irrational. How will you resolve this contradiction?

Solution

We know that when we measure the length of a line or a figure by using a scale or any device, we do not get an exact measurement. In fact, we get an approximate rational value. So, we are not able to realize that either circumference (c) or diameter(d) of a circle is irrational. Therefore, we can conclude that as such there is not any contradiction regarding the value {tex}\pi{/tex} of and we realize that the value of {tex}\pi{/tex} is irrational.

Q 11

Represent {tex}\sqrt{9.3}{/tex}  on the number line.

Solution


The distance 9.3 from a fixed point A on a given line to obtain a point B such that AB = 9.3 units. From B mark a distance of 1 unit and mark the new point as C. Find the mid-point of AC and mark that point as O. Draw a semi-circle with centre O and radius OC. Draw a line perpendicular to AC passing through B and interesting the semi-circle at D. Then BD = {tex}\sqrt{9.3}{/tex}. 

Q 12

Rationalize the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}
We need to multiply the numerator and denominator {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} by {tex}\sqrt 7,to get \frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 }} \times \frac{{\sqrt 7 }}{{\sqrt 7 }} = \frac{{\sqrt 7 }}{7}{/tex}
Therefore, we conclude that on rationalizing the denominator {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} we get {tex}\frac{{\sqrt 7 }}{7}{/tex}

Q 13

Rationalize the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }}{/tex}
We need to multiply the numerator and denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }}{/tex} by {tex}\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6,to get \frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }} \times \frac{{\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 }}{{\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 }} = \frac{{\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 }}{{\left( {\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 } \right)\left( {\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 } \right)}}{/tex}
We need to apply the formula {tex}\left( {a - b} \right)\left( {a + b} \right) = {a^2} - {b^2}{/tex} in the denominator to get
{tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }} = \frac{{\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 }}{{{{\left( {\sqrt 7 } \right)}^2} - {{\left( {\sqrt 6 } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
 {tex}= \frac{{\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 }}{{7 - 6}}{/tex}
 {tex}= \sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6 .{/tex}
Therefore, we conclude that on rationalizing the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - \sqrt 6 }}{/tex} we get {tex}\sqrt 7 + \sqrt 6{/tex} .

Q 14

Rationalize the denominator of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 }}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 + \sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
multiply denominator and numerator by {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 - \sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{(\sqrt{5})^{2}-(\sqrt{2})^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{5-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}

Q 15

Rationalize the denominators of {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 7 - 2}}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\eqalign{ & {1 \over {\sqrt 7 - 2}} \cr & = {1 \over {\sqrt 7 - 2}} \times {{\sqrt 7 + 2} \over {\sqrt 7 + 2}} \cr & = {{\sqrt 7 + 2} \over {{{(\sqrt 7 )}^2} - {{(2)}^2}}} = {{\sqrt 7 + 2} \over {7 - 4}} \cr & = {{\sqrt 7 + 2} \over 3} \cr}{/tex}

sections.label: Exercise 1.5
sections.html:
Q 1

Find: {tex}{64^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}

Solution

We know that {tex}{a^{\frac{1}{n}}} = \sqrt[n]{a},{\text{ where }}a > 0.{/tex}
We conclude that {tex}{64^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} can also be written as {tex}\sqrt[2]{{64}} = \sqrt[2]{{8 \times 8}} = 8.{/tex}
Therefore, the value of {tex}{64^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} will be 8.

Q 2

Find: {tex}32^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex}

Solution

321/5 = (25) 1/5
= 25 × 1/5 = 21 = 2

Q 3

Find: {tex}125^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}

Solution

1251/3 = (53)1/3
= 53 × 1/3 = 51 = 5

Q 4

Find: {tex}9^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}

Solution

93/2 = (91/2)3 = 33 = 27

Q 5

Find: {tex}32^{\frac{2}{5}}{/tex}

Solution

322/5 = (25)2/5 = 25 × 2/5 = 22 =  4.

Q 6

Find: {tex}16^{\frac{3}{4}}{/tex}

Solution

163/4 = (24)3/4 = 24 × 3/4 = 23 = 8

Q 7

Find: {tex}125^{\frac{-1}{3}}{/tex}

Solution

125-1/3 = (53)–1/3 = 53 {tex}\times{/tex} (–1/3) = 5–1 = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}

Q 8

Simplify : {tex}2^{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot 2^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex}

Solution

We have.

{tex}2^{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot 2^{\frac{1}{5}}=2^{\frac{2}{3}+\frac{1}{5}}{/tex} {by {tex}a^{m} \cdot a^{n}=a^{(m+n)}{/tex}} 
{tex}2^{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot 2^{\frac{1}{5}}=2^{\left(\frac{2 \times 5+1 \times 3}{15}\right)}{/tex}{tex}=2^{\frac{13}{15}}{/tex}

Q 9

Simplify: {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{3}}\right)^{7}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{3}}\right)^{7}=\frac{1^{7}}{\left(3^{3}\right)^{7}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{3^{21}}=3^{-21}{/tex}

Q 10

Simplify: {tex}\frac{11^{\frac{1}{2}}}{11^{\frac{1}{4}}}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{11^{1 / 2}}{11^{1 / 4}}{/tex} = 111/2-1/4 = 111/4

Q 11

Simplify: {tex}7 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \cdot 8 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}

Solution

{tex}7 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \cdot 8 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}

We know that {tex}a ^ { m } \cdot b ^ { m } = ( a \times b ) ^ { m }{/tex}.

So, {tex}7 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \cdot 8 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = ( 7 \times 8 ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}= ( 56 ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}

Therefore, the value of {tex}7 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \cdot 8 ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}will be {tex}( 56 ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }{/tex}.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the degree of the polynomial : x5 – x4 + 3

Solution

The highest power of the variable is 5. Therefore, the degree of the polynomial is 5.

Q 2

Find the degree of the polynomial: 2 – y2 – y3 + 2y8

Solution

The highest power of the variable is 8. Therefore, the degree of the polynomial is 8.

Q 3

Find the degree of the polynomial: 2

Solution

The only term here is 2 which can be written as 2x0. So the exponent of x is 0. Hence, the degree of the polynomial is 0.

Q 4

Find the value of p(x) = 5x2 – 3x + 7 at x = 1

Solution

The given polynomial is,

p(x) = 5x2 – 3x + 7
The value of the polynomial p(x) at x = 1 is given by p(1) = 5(1)2 – 3(1) + 7 = 5 – 3 + 7 = 9

Q 5

Find the value of q(y) = 3y3 - 4y +{tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}at y = 2.

Solution

We have, q(y) = 3y3 - 4y + {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}
On put y = 2 in q(y), we get
q(2) = 3(2)3 - 4(2)+{tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex}8 - 8 + {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}
= 24 - 8 + {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}
= 16 + {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}

Q 6

Find the value of p(t) = 4t4 + 5t3 - t2 + 6 at t = a.

Solution

We have, p(t) = 4t4+ 5t3 - t2 + 6
On putting t = a in p(t), we get,
p(a) = 4 (a)4 + 5 (a)3 - (a)2 + 6
= 4a4 + 5a3 - a2 + 6

Q 7

Check whether –2 and 2 are zeroes of the polynomial x + 2

Solution

We have, p(x) = x + 2
Then p(2) = 2 + 2 = 4, p(–2) = –2 + 2 = 0
Therefore, –2 is a zero of the polynomial x + 2, but 2 is not the zero of the polynomial.

Q 8

Find a zero of the polynomial p(x) = 2x + 1

Solution

Finding a zero of p(x), is the same as solving the equation p(x) = 0
Now, 2x + 1 = 0 gives us {tex}x=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
So, {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} is a zero of the polynomial 2x + 1   

Q 9

Verify whether 2 and 0 are zeroes of the polynomial x2 – 2x

Solution

Let p(x) = x2 – 2x
Then p(2) = 22 – 4 = 4 – 4 = 0
and p(0) = 0 – 0 = 0
Hence, 2 and 0 are both zeroes of the polynomial x2 – 2x.

Q 10

Examine whether x + 2 is a factor of x3 + 3x2 + 5x + 6 and of 2x + 4.

Solution

The zero of x + 2 is –2.
Let p(x) = x3 + 3x2 + 5x + 6 and s(x) = 2x + 4
Then, p(–2) = (–2)3 + 3(–2)2 + 5(–2) + 6
= –8 + 12 – 10 + 6
= 0
So, by the Factor Theorem, x + 2 is a factor of x3 + 3x2 + 5x + 6.
Again, s(–2) = 2(–2) + 4 = 0
So, x + 2 is a factor of 2x + 4. 

Q 11

Find the value of k, if x – 1 is a factor of 4x3 + 3x2 – 4x + k.

Solution

As x – 1 is a factor of p(x) = 4x3 + 3x2 – 4x + k, therefore,

p(1) = 0
Now, p(1) = 4(1)3 + 3(1)2 – 4(1) + k
So, 4 + 3 – 4 + k = 0
i.e., k = –3

Q 12

Factorise 6x2 + 17x + 5 by splitting the middle term, and by using the Factor Theorem.

Solution

If we can find two numbers p and q such that p + q = 17 and pq = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 30, then we can get the factors
So, let us look for the pairs of factors of 30. Some are 1 and 30, 2 and 15, 3 and 10, 5 and 6. Of these pairs, 2 and 15 will give us p + q = 17.
So, 6x2 + 17x + 5
= 6x2 + (2 + 15)x + 5
= 6x2 + 2x + 15x + 5
= 2x(3x + 1) + 5(3x + 1)
= (3x + 1) (2x + 5)
This is the required factorisation.

Q 13

Factorise y2 – 5y + 6 by using the Factor Theorem.

Solution

Let p(y) = y2 – 5y + 6. 
The factors of 6 are 1, 2 and 3.
Now, p(2) = 22 – (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) + 6 = 0
So, y – 2 is a factor of p(y).
Also, p(3) = 32 – (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) + 6 = 0
So, y – 3 is also a factor of y2 – 5y + 6
Therefore, y2 – 5y + 6 = (y – 2)(y – 3)

This is the required factorisation.

Q 14

Factorise : x3 – 23x2 + 142x – 120

Solution

Let p(x) = x3 – 23x2 + 142x – 120
We shall now look for all the factors of –120. Some of these are {tex}\pm{/tex}1, {tex}\pm{/tex}2, {tex}\pm{/tex}3, {tex}\pm{/tex}4, {tex}\pm{/tex}5, {tex}\pm{/tex}6, {tex}\pm{/tex}8, {tex}\pm{/tex}10, {tex}\pm{/tex}12, {tex}\pm{/tex}15, {tex}\pm{/tex}20, {tex}\pm{/tex}24, {tex}\pm{/tex}30, {tex}\pm{/tex}60.
By trial, we find that p(1) = 0. Therefore, x – 1 is a factor of p(x).
Now we see that x3 – 23x2 + 142x – 120 = x3 – x2 – 22x2 + 22x + 120x – 120
= x2 (x –1) – 22x(x – 1) + 120(x – 1)
= (x – 1) (x2 – 22x + 120) [Taking (x – 1) common]
Now x2 – 22x + 120 can be factorised either by splitting the middle term or by using the Factor theorem. By splitting the middle term, we have: x2 – 22x + 120 = x2 – 12x – 10x + 120
= x(x – 12) – 10(x – 12)
= (x – 12) (x – 10)
Therefore, x3 – 23x2 – 142x – 120 = (x – 1)(x – 10)(x – 12)

Q 15

Find the products using appropriate identities: (x + 3) (x + 3)

Solution

We have the Identity: (x + y)2 = x2 + 2xy + y2.
Put y = 3 in it,
we get (x + 3) (x + 3) = (x + 3)2 = x2 + 2(x)(3) + (3)2 = x2 + 6x + 9

Q 16

Find the products using appropriate identities: (x - 3) (x + 5)

Solution

We know the Identity i.e., (x + a) (x + b) = x2 + (a + b)x + ab,
We have (x – 3) (x + 5) = x2 + (–3 + 5)x + (–3)(5) = x2 + 2x – 15

Q 17

Evaluate 105 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 without multiplying directly.

Solution

Firstly, write 105 as 100 + 5 and 106 as100 + 6.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  105 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 = (100 + 5) {tex}\times{/tex} (100 + 6)
= (100)2 + (5 + 6)100 + (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6)
[{tex}\because{/tex} (x + a)(x + b) = x2 + (a + b)x + ab]
= 10000+ 11{tex}\times{/tex}100 + 30
= 10000 + 1100 + 30 = 11130

Q 18

Factorise: 49a2 + 70ab + 25b2

Solution

Here you can see that 49a2 = (7a)2, 25b2 = (5b)2, 70ab = 2(7a) (5b)
Comparing with x2 + 2xy + y2, we observe that x = 7a and y = 5b.
Using Identity (x + y)2 = x2 + 2xy + y, we get
49a2 + 70ab + 25b2 = (7a + 5b)2 = (7a + 5b) (7a + 5b)
This is the required factorisation.

Q 19

Factorise: {tex}\frac{25}{4} x^{2}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}.{/tex}

Solution

a2-b2 = (a+b)(a-b)

{tex}\frac{25 x^{2}}{4}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}=\left(\frac{5}{2} x\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{y}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex}

{tex}=\left(\frac{5}{2} x+\frac{y}{3}\right)\left(\frac{5}{2} x-\frac{y}{3}\right){/tex}

Q 20

Write (3a + 4b + 5c)2 in expanded form.

Solution

Comparing the given expression with (x + y + z)2, we find that x = 3a, y = 4b and z = 5c.
Therefore, using Identity (x + y + z)2 = x2 + y2 + z2 + 2xy + 2yz + 2zx,
we have (3a + 4b + 5c)2 = (3a)2 + (4b)2 + (5c)2 + 2(3a)(4b) + 2(4b)(5c) + 2(5c)(3a)
= 9a2 + 16b2 + 25c2 + 24ab + 40bc + 30ac

Q 21

Expand (4a – 2b – 3c)2

Solution

Using Identity (x + y + z)2 = x2 + y2 + z2 + 2xy + 2yz + 2zx, we have
(4a – 2b – 3c)2 = [4a + (–2b) + (–3c)]2
= (4a)2 + (–2b)2 + (–3c)2 + 2(4a)(–2b) + 2(–2b)(–3c) + 2(–3c)(4a)
= 16a2 + 4b2 + 9c2 – 16ab + 12bc – 24ac
This is the required expansion.

Q 22

Factorise : 4x2 + y2 + z2 – 4xy – 2yz + 4xz

Solution

We have,
4x2 + y2 + z2 – 4xy – 2yz + 4xz = (2x)2 + (–y)2 + (z)2 + 2(2x)(–y) + 2(–y)(z) + 2(2x)(z)
= [2x + (–y) + z]2
= (2x – y + z)2
= (2x – y + z)(2x – y + z)
This is the required factorisation.

Q 23

Write (3a + 4b)3 in the expanded form.

Solution

Comparing the given expression with (x + y)3 ,
we find that x = 3a and y = 4b.
So, using Identity (x + y)3 = x3 + y3 + 3xy (x + y),
we have: (3a + 4b)3 = (3a)3 + (4b)3 + 3(3a)(4b)(3a + 4b)
= 27a3 + 64b3 + 108a2b + 144ab2
This is the required expansion.

Q 24

Write (5p – 3q)3 in the expanded form.

Solution

Comparing the given expression with (x – y)3,
we find that x = 5p, y = 3q.
So, using Identity (x - y)3 = x3 – 3x2y + 3xy2 – y3,
we have: (5p – 3q)3 = (5p)3 – (3q)3 – 3(5p)(3q)(5p – 3q)
= 125p3 – 27q3 – 225p2q + 135pq2
This is the required expansion.

Q 25

Evaluate (104)3 using suitable identity.

Solution

(104)3 = (100 + 4)3
Using identity (x + y)3 = x3 + 3xy(x + y) + y3
We get,
(100 + 4)3 = (100)3 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 (100 + 4) + 43
= 10,00 000 + 1,200 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 + 64
= 10,00,000 + 1,24,800 + 64
= 11,24,864

Q 26

Evaluate (999)3 using suitable identity.

Solution

We have, (999)3 = (1000 – 1)3
= (1000)3 – (1)3 – 3(1000)(1)(1000 – 1)
= 1000000000 – 1 – 2997000
= 997002999

Q 27

Factorise: 8x3 + 27y3 + 36x2y + 54xy2

Solution

The given expression can be written as
(2x)3 + (3y)3 + 3(4x2)(3y) + 3(2x)(9y2)
= (2x)3 + (3y)3 + 3(2x)2(3y) + 3(2x)(3y)2
= (2x + 3y)3 (Using Identity (x + y)3 = x3 + y3 + 3xy (x + y))
= (2x + 3y)(2x + 3y)(2x + 3y)
This is the required factorisation.

Q 28

Factorise: 8x3 + y3 + 27z3 – 18xyz

Solution

Here, we have
8x3 + y3 + 27z3 - 18xyz
= (2x)3 + y3 + (3z)3 – 3(2x)(y)(3z)
= (2x + y + 3z)[(2x)2 + y2 + (3z)2 – (2x)(y) – (y)(3z) – (2x)(3z)]
= (2x + y + 3z) (4x2 + y2 + 9z2 - 2xy - 3yz - 6xz)
This is the required factorisation.

Q 1

Is the expression {tex}4{x^2} - 3x + 7{\text{ }}{/tex}, polynomial in one variable or not? State the reason for your answer.

Solution

{tex}4{x^2} - 3x + 7{\text{ }}{/tex}
We can observe that in the polynomial {tex}4{x^2} - 3x + 7{\text{ }}{/tex}
we have x as the only variable and the powers of x in each term are a whole number.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}4{x^2} - 3x + 7{\text{ }}{/tex} is a polynomial in one variable.

Q 2

Is the expression {tex}{y^2} + \sqrt 2{/tex}, polynomial in one variable or not? State the reason for your answer.

Solution

{tex}{y^2} + \sqrt 2{/tex}
We can observe that in the polynomial {tex}{y^2} + \sqrt 2 {/tex}, we have y as the only variable and the powers of y in each term are a whole number.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}{y^2} + \sqrt 2{/tex}  is a polynomial in one variable.

Q 3

Is the expression {tex}3\sqrt t + t\sqrt 2{/tex}, polynomial in one variable or not? State the reason for your answer.

Solution

{tex}3\sqrt t + t\sqrt 2{/tex}
We can observe that in the polynomial {tex}3\sqrt t + t\sqrt 2 {/tex} we have t as the only variable and the
powers of t in each term are not a whole number.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}3\sqrt t + t\sqrt 2{/tex}  is not a polynomial in one variable.

Q 4

Is the expression {tex}y + \frac{2}{y}{/tex}, polynomial in one variable or not? State the reason for your answer.

Solution

{tex}y + \frac{2}{y}{/tex}
We can observe that in the polynomial {tex}y + \frac{2}{y}{/tex} ,we have y as the only variable and the powers of y in each term are not a whole number.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}y + \frac{2}{y}{/tex} is not a polynomial in one variable.

Q 5

Is the expression {tex}{x^{10}} + {y^3} + {t^{50}}{/tex}, polynomial in one variable or not? State the reason for your answer.

Solution

{tex}{x^{10}} + {y^3} + {t^{50}}{/tex}
We can observe that in the polynomial {tex}{x^{10}} + {y^3} + {t^{50}}{/tex}, we have x, y and t as the variables and the powers of x, y and t in each term is a whole number.
Therefore, we conclude that {tex}{x^{10}} + {y^3} + {t^{50}}{/tex} is a polynomial but not a polynomial in one variable.

Q 6

Write the coefficient of {tex}{x^2}{/tex} in 2 + x2 +x

Solution

{tex}2 + {x^2} + x{/tex}
The coefficient of {tex}{x^2}{/tex} in the polynomial {tex}2 + {x^2} + x{/tex} is 1.

Q 7

Write the coefficient of x2 in 2 – x2 + x3

Solution

We have,

Coefficient of x2 = -1

Q 8

Write the coefficient of {tex}{x^2}{/tex} in {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{x^2} + x{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{x^2} + x{/tex}
The coefficient of {tex}{x^2}{/tex} in the polynomial {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{x^2} + x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}.

Q 9

Write the coefficient of x2 in {tex}\sqrt{2} x-1{/tex}

Solution

Since {tex}x^2{/tex} is absent in given expression, therefore,
Coefficient of x2 = 0

Q 10

Give one example each of a binomial of degree 35, and of a monomial of degree 100.

Solution

The binomial of degree 35 can be x35 + 9
The monomial of degree 100 can be t100

Q 11

Write the degree of the polynomial: 5x3 + 4x2 + 7x

Solution

In the given polynomial, Term with the highest power of x is 5x3
Exponent of x in this term = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Degree of the given  polynomial is 3.

Q 12

Write the degree of the polynomial: 4 – y2

Solution

Term with the highest power of y = – y2
Exponent of y in this term = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Degree of this polynomial = 2.

Q 13

Write the degree of the polynomial: 5t - {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} 

Solution

Term with the highest power of t = 5t
Exponent of t in this term = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Degree of this polynomial = 1

Q 14

Write the degree of the polynomial: 3

Solution

It is a non-zero constant. So the degree of this polynomial is zero.

Q 15

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial:
{tex}{x^2} + x{\text{ }}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}{x^2} + x{\text{ }}{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}{x^2} + x{\text{ }}{/tex} is 2.
Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial {tex}{x^2} + x{\text{ }}{/tex} is a quadratic polynomial.

Q 16

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial: {tex}x - {x^3}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}x - {x^3}{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}x - {x^3}{/tex} is 3.
Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial {tex}x - {x^3}{/tex} is a cubic polynomial.

Q 17

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial:
{tex}y + {y^2} + 4{/tex}

Solution

{tex}y + {y^2} + 4{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}y + {y^2} + 4{/tex} is 2.
Therefore, the polynomial {tex}y + {y^2} + 4{/tex} is a quadratic polynomial.

Q 18

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial: {tex}1 + x{\text{ }}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}1 + x{\text{ }}{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}\left( {1 + x{\text{ }}} \right){/tex} is 1.
Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial {tex}1 + x{\text{ }}{/tex} is a linear polynomial.

Q 19

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial: 3t

Solution

3t
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}\left( {3t} \right){/tex}is 1. Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial 3t is a linear polynomial.

Q 20

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial: {tex}{r^2}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}{r^2}{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}{r^2}{/tex} is 2.
Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial {tex}{r^2}{/tex} is a quadratic polynomial.

Q 21

Classify as linear, quadratic and cubic polynomial: {tex}7{x^3}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}7{x^3}{/tex}
We can observe that the degree of the polynomial {tex}7{x^3}{/tex} is 3.
Therefore, we can conclude that the polynomial {tex}7{x^3}{/tex} is a cubic polynomial.
