sections.label: Exercise 5.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Euclidean geometry is valid only for curved surfaces. Justify your answer.

Solution

False
Because Euclidean geometry is valid only for the figures in the plane but on the curved surfaces, it fails.

Q 2

The boundaries of the solids are curves. Justify your answer.

Solution

False
Because the boundaries of the solids are surfaces.

Q 3

The edges of a surface are curves. Justify your answer.

Solution

False
Because the edges of surfaces are lines.

Q 4

The things which are double of the same thing are equal to one another. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
It is one of the Euclid's axioms.
 Example: if 2x = 2y, then x=y.

Q 5

If a quantity B is a part of another quantity A, then A can be written as the sum of B and some third quantity C. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
Let B be a quantity which is a part of another quantity A.
Then by Euclid's axiom which states that, "The whole is greater than the part"
we can say that:
The quantity A is greater than the quantity B, which means that there exists a third quantity, say C by which quantity A is greater than the quantity B.
Symbolically, A > B which means that there is some C such that A = B + C. 

Q 6

The statements that are proved are called axioms. Justify your answer.

Solution

False, Because the statements that are proved are called theorems.

Q 7

For every line l and for every point P not lying on a given line l, there exists a unique line m passing through P and parallel to l is known as Playfair’s axiom. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
Since, it is an equivalent version of Euclid’s fifth postulate and it is known as Playfair’s axiom.

Q 8

Two distinct intersecting lines cannot be parallel to the same line. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
Since it is an equivalent version of Euclid's fifth postulate.

Q 9

Attempts to prove Euclid’s fifth postulate using the other postulates and axioms led to the discovery of several other geometries. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
All attempts to prove the fifth postulate as a theorem led to a great achievement in the creation of several other geometries. These geometries are quite different from Euclidean geometry and called non-Euclidean geometry.

sections.label: Exercise 5.3
sections.html:
Q 1

Two salesmen make equal sales during the month of August. In September, each salesman doubles his sale of the month of August. Compare their sales in September.

Solution

Let the equal sale of each salesmen in August be x.
In September each salesman doubles his sale of August.
Therefore, sale of first salesman is 2x and sale of second salesman is 2x.
According to Euclid’s axioms, things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.
Therefore, in September their sales are again equal.

Q 2

It is known that x + y = 10 and that x = z. Show that z + y = 10?

Solution

We are given that,
x + y = 10 ...(i)
and x = z ...(ii)
According to Euclid's axioms, if equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.
Therefore, From Eq.(ii),
x + y = z + y ...(iii)
From Equations (i) and (iii)
z + y = 10.

Q 3

Look at the Fig. Show that length AH > sum of lengths of AB + BC + CD.

Solution

From the given figure, we have
AB + BC + CD = AD [AB, SC and CD are the parts of AD] Here, AD is also the parts of AH.
By Euclid's axiom, the whole is greater than the part. i.e., AH > AD.
Therefore, length AH > sum of lengths of AB + BC + CD.

Q 4

In the given figure, we have AB = BC, BX = BY. Show that AX = CY.

Solution

We have AB = BC …(1) [Given]
And BX = BY …(2) [Given]
Subtracting (2) from (1), we get
AB - BX = BC - BY
Now, By Euclid’s axiom 3, we have
If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainder are equal.
Hence, AX = CY.

Q 5

In the given figure, we have X and Y are the mid-points of AC and BC and AX = CY. Show that AC = BC.

Solution

We have AX = CY [Given]
Now, by Euclid’s axiom 6, we have things which are double of the same thing are equal to one another,
so 2AX = 2CY
Hence, AC = BC. [{tex}\because{/tex} X and Y are the mid- points of AC and BC]

Q 6

In the given figure, we have {tex}{\text{BX = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}{\text{BY = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{BC}}{/tex} and AB = BC. Show that BX = BY.

Solution

We have AB = BC [Given]
Now, by Euclid’s axiom 7, we have things which are halves of the same things are equal to one another.
{tex}\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{2}{\text{AB = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{BC}}{/tex}
Hence, BX = BY. [{tex}\because {\text{BX = }}\,\,\frac{1}{2}{\text{AB}}{/tex} and {tex}{\text{BY = }}\,\,\frac{1}{2}{\text{BC}}{/tex} (Given)]

Q 7

In the given figure, we have {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2,\angle 2 = \angle 3{/tex}. Show that {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex}.

Solution

We have
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Given]
{tex}\angle 2 = \angle 3{/tex} [Given]
Now, by Euclid’s axiom 1, things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one other.
Hence, {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex}.

Q 8

In the given figure, we have {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex} and {tex}\angle 2 = \angle 4{/tex}. Show that {tex}\angle {\text{A}} = \angle {\text{C}}{/tex}.

Solution

We have {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex}  …(1) [Given]
And {tex}\angle 2 = \angle 4{/tex} …(2) [Given]
Now, by Euclid’s axiom 2, we have if equal are added to equals, the whole are equal.
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 = \angle 3 + \angle 4{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\angle {\text{A = }}\angle {\text{C}}{/tex}.

Q 9

In the given figure, we have {tex}\angle {\text{ABC = }}\angle {\text{ACB,}}\angle 4 = \angle 3{/tex}. Show that {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}.

Solution

We have
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle {\text{ABC = }}\angle {\text{ACB}}{/tex} …(1) [(Given)]
And {tex}\angle 4{\text{ = }}\angle 3{/tex} …(2) [(Given)]
Now, subtracting (2) from (1), we get
Now, by Euclid’s axiom 3, if equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.
{tex}\angle {\text{ABC}} - \angle 4 = \angle {\text{ACB}} - \angle 3{/tex}
Hence,  {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}.

Q 10

In the given figure, we have AC = DC, CB = CE. Show that AB = DE.

Solution

We have AC = DC …(1) [Given]
And CB = CE…(2) [Given]
Now, by axiom 2, if equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.
Adding (1) and (2), we get
AC + CB = DC + CE
Hence, AB = DE.

Q 11

In the given figure, if {tex}{\text{OX = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{XY,}}\;{\text{PX = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{XZ}}{/tex} and OX = PX, show that XY = XZ.

Solution

We have OX = PX [Given]
Now
{tex}\because \;{\text{OX = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{XY}}{/tex}(Given) and {tex}{\text{PX = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{XZ}}{/tex} [Given]
{tex}\therefore \;\frac{1}{2}{\text{XY = }}\frac{1}{2}{\text{XZ}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Things which are halves of the same thing are equal to one another (Euclid’s axiom 7)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} XY = XZ [{tex}\because{/tex} Things which are double of the same thing are equal to one another (Euclid’s axiom 6)]

Q 12
  1. AB = BC, M is the mid-point of AB and N is the mid-point of BC. Show that AM = NC.
  2. BM = BN, M is the mid-point of AB and N is the mid-point of BC. Show that AB = BC.
Solution
  1. From the above figure, We have AB = BC…(1) [Given]
    Now, A, M, B are the three points on a line, and M lies between A and B such that M is the mid point of AB [Given], then
    AM + MB = AB …(2) Also B, N, C are three points on a line such that N is the mid point of BC [Given]
    Similarly, BN + NC = BC....(3)
    So, we get AM + MB = BN + NC
    From (1), (2), (3) and Euclid’s first axiom 
    Since M is the mid-point of AB and N is the mid-point of BC, therefore
    2AM = 2NC i.e. AM = NC
    Hence, AM = NC. Proved
    Using axiom 6, things which are double of the same thing are equal to one another.
  2. From the above figure, We have BM = BN …(1) [ Given ]
    As M is the mid-point of AB [Given] , so that
    BM = AM…(2)
    And N is the mid-point of BC [Given]
    BN = NC…(3)
    From (1), (2) and (3) and Euclid’s first axiom, we get
    AM = NC…(4)
    Adding (4) and (1), we get
    AM + BM = NC + BN
    Hence, AB = BC Proved
    [By axiom 2 if equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal]

sections.label: Exercise 5.4
sections.html:
Q 1

Read the following statement:
An equilateral triangle is a polygon made up of three line segment out of which two line segments are equal to the third one and all its angles are 60° each.
Define the terms used in this definition which you feel necessary. Are there any undefined terms in this? Can you justify that all sides and all angles are equal in an equilateral triangle.

Solution

The terms need to be defined are

  1. Polygon A closed figure bounded by three or more line segments.
  2. Ling segments Part of a line with two ends points.
  3. Line Undefined term.
  4. Point Undefined term.
  5. Angle A figure formed by two rays with one common initial points.
  6. Acute angle Angle whose measure is between 0° to 90°.

Here undefined terms are line and point.
All the angles of equilateral triangle are 60° each (given)
Two line segments are equal to the third-one (given).
Therefore, all three sides of an equilateral triangle are equal, because, according to Euclid's axiom, things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.

Q 2

Study the following statement:
Two intersecting lines cannot be perpendicular to the same line
Check whether it is an equivalent version to the Euclid’s fifth postulate.
[Hint: Identify the two intersecting lines l and m and the line n in the above statement.]

Solution

The equivalent versions of Euclid's fifth postulate are mentioned as follows:

  1. For every line l and for every point P not lying on Z, there exists a unique line m passing through P and parallel to Z.
  2. Two distinct intersecting lines cannot be parallel to the same line.

From above two statements it follows given statements is not an equivalent version to the Euclid's fifth postulate.
 

Q 3

Read the following statements which are taken as axioms:

  1. If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then corresponding angles are not necessarily equal.
  2. If a transversal intersect two parallel lines, then alternate interior angles are equal.

Is this system of axioms consistent? Justify your answer.

Solution
  1. A system of axiom is called consistent if there is no statement which can be deduced from these axioms such that it contradicts any axiom. It is known that, if a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of corresponding angles are equal, which is a theorem. Therefore, Statement I is false and it is not an axiom.
  2. It is known that, if a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of alternate interior angles are equal. It is also a theorem. So, Statement parallel is true and an axiom. Therefore, in the given statement, first is false and second is an axiom.
    Therefore, given system of axioms is not consistent.
Q 4

Read the following two statements which are taken as axioms:

  1. If two lines intersect each other, then the vertically opposite angles are not equal.
  2. If a ray stands on a line, then the sum of two adjacent angles so formed is equal to 180°.

Is this system of axioms consistent? Justify your answer.

Solution

It is known that, if two lines intersect each other, then the vertically opposite angles are equal. It is a theorem, therefore,given Statement I is false and not an axiom.
Also, we know that, if a ray stands on a line, then the sum of two adjacent angles so formed is equal to 180°. It is an axiom. Therefore, given statement parallel is true and an axiom.
Thus, in given statements, first is false and second is an axiom. Therefore, given system of axioms is not consistent.

Q 5

Read the following axioms:

  1. Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.
  2. If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.
  3. Things which are double of the same thing are equal to one another.

Check whether the given system of axioms is consistent or inconsistent.

Solution

Some of Euclid’s axioms are

  1. Things which are equal to the same thing are equal one another.
  2. If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.
  3. Things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.

Therefore, given three axioms are Euclid’s axioms. So, here we cannot deduce any statement from these axioms which contradicts any axiom. Therefore, given system of axioms is consistent.

Q 5

John is of the same age as Mohan. Ram is also of the same age as Mohan. State the Euclid’s axiom that illustrates the relative ages of John and Ram.

Q 6

If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side of it, whose sum is 120°, then the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on the side on which the sum of angles is

Q 7

Pyramid is a solid figure, the base of which is a triangle or square or some other polygon and its side faces are equilateral triangles that converges to a point at the top. Justify your answer.

Solution

False
The side faces of a pyramid are triangles not necessarily equilateral triangles as a pyramid is a structure whose outer surfaces are triangular and converge to a single point at the top. The base of a pyramid can be trilateral, quadrilateral, or of any polygon shape. 

Q 8

In Vedic period, squares and circular shaped altars were used for household rituals, while altars whose shapes were combination of rectangles, triangles and trapeziums were used for public worship. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
The geometry of Vedic period originated with the construction of vedis and fireplaces for performing vedic rites. The location of the sacred fires had to be in accordance to the clearly laid down instructions about their shapes and area.

Q 9

In geometry, we take a point, a line and a plane as undefined terms. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
To define a point, a line and plane in geometry we need to define many other things that give a long chain of definition without an end. For such reasons mathematicians agree to leave these geometric terms undefined.

Q 10

If the area of a triangle equals the area of a rectangle and the area of the rectangle equals that of a square then the area of the triangle also equals the area of the square. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
Things equal to the same thing are equal.

Q 11

Euclid's fourth axiom says that everything equals itself. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
It is the justification of the principle of superposition.

Q 12

The Euclidean geometry is valid only for figures in the plane. Justify your answer.

Solution

True
It fails on the curved surfaces. For example on curved surfaces, the sum of angles of a triangle may be more than 180°.

Q 13

Ram and Ravi have the same weight. If they each gain weight by 2 kg, how will their new weights be compared?

Solution

Let x kg be the weight each of Ram and Ravi. On gaining 2 kg, the weight of Ram and Ravi will be (x + 2) each. By Euclid’s second axiom, when equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal. Therefore, the weight of Ram and Ravi are again equal.

Q 14

Solve the equation a - 15 = 25 and state which axiom do you use here.

Solution

We have,
a - 15 = 25.
On adding 15 to both sides, we have
a - 15 + 15 = 25 + 15 = 40 (using Euclid’s second axiom).or a = 40

Q 15

In Fig., if {tex}\angle 1=\angle 3{/tex}, {tex}\angle 2=\angle 4{/tex} and {tex}\angle 3=\angle 4,{/tex} write the relation between {tex}\angle 1{/tex} and  {tex}\angle 2{/tex}  using Euclid's axiom.

Solution

From the above Fig. we have

 {tex}\angle 3=\angle 4, \quad \angle 1=\angle 3{/tex} and {tex}\angle 2=\angle 4{/tex}  [ Given ]  ..(1)
According to Euclid's first axiom, the things which are equal to equal things are equal to one another.
Therefore, {tex}\angle 1=\angle 2{/tex}  [ from (1) ]

Q 16

In fig. AC = XD, C is the mid-point of AB and D is the mid-point of XY. Using a Euclid's axiom, show that AB = XY.

Solution

 In the above figure, we have

AB = AC + BC = AC + AC = 2AC  (Since, C is the mid-point of AB) ..(1)
XY = XD + DY = XD + XD = 2XD (Since, D is the mid-point of XY) ..(2)
Also, AC = XD (Given) ..(3)
From (1),(2)and(3), we get 
AB = XY, According to Euclid, things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.

Q 17

Read the following statement:
A square is a polygon made up of four line segments, out of which, length of three line segments are equal to the length of fourth one and all its angles are right angles.
Define the terms used in this definition which you feel necessary. Are there any undefined terms in this? Can you justify that all angles and sides of a square are equal?

Solution

The terms need to be defined are:
Polygon: A simple closed figure made up of three or more line segments.
Line segment: Part of a line with two end points.
Line: Undefined term.
Point: Undefined term
Angle: A figure formed by two rays with a common initial point.
Ray: Part of a line with one end point.
Right angle: Angle whose measure is 90°.
Undefined terms used are: line, point.
Euclid's fourth postulate says that "all right angles are equal to  one another."
In a square, all angles are right angles, therefore, all angles are equal (From Euclid's fourth postulate).
Three line segments are equal to fourth line segment (Given).
Therefore, all the four sides of a square are equal. (by Euclid's first axiom "things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.")

sections.label: Exercise 6.2
sections.html:
Q 1

For what value of x + y in Fig., will ABC be a line? Justify your answer.

Solution

For ABC to be a line, the sum of two adjacent angles must be {tex}180°{/tex} i.e., x + y must be equal to {tex} 180°{/tex}.

Q 2

Can a triangle have all angles less than 60°? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

A triangle cannot have all angle less than 60°. Then, sum of all the angles will be less than 180° whereas sum of all the angles of a triangle is always 180°.

Q 3

Can a triangle have two obtuse angles? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

An angle whose measure is more than 90° but less than 180° is called an obtuse angle.
A triangle cannot have two obtuse angles because the sum of all the angles of it cannot be more than 180°. It is always equal to 180°.

Q 4

How many triangles can be drawn having its angles as 45°, 64° and 72°? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

We cannot draw any triangle having its angles 45°, 64° and 72° because the sum of the angles (45° + 64° + 72° = 181°) cannot be 181°.

Q 5

How many triangles can be drawn having is angles as 53°, 64° and 63°? Give reason for your answer.

Solution
Sum of these angles = 53° + 64° + 63° = 180°. So, we can draw infinitely many triangles, sum of the angles of every triangle having its angles as 53°, 64° and 63° is 180°.
Q 6

In the given figure, find the value of x for which the lines l and m are parallel.

Solution

If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of consecutive interior angles are supplementary.
Here, the two given lines l and m are parallel.
Angles x and 44°, are consecutive interior angles on the same side of the transversal.
Therefore, x + 44° = 180°
Hence, x = 180° - 44° = 136°.

Q 7

Two adjacent angles are equal. Is it necessary that each of these angles will be a right angle? Justify your answer.

Solution
No, each of these angles will be a right angle only when they form a linear pair, i.e., when the non-common arms of the given two adjacent angles are two opposite rays.
Q 8

If one of the angles formed by two intersecting lines is a right angle, what can you say about the other three angles? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

If two intersect each other at a point, then four angles are formed.
If one of these four angles is a right angle, then each of the other three angles will also be a right by linear pair axiom.

Q 9

In a figure, which of the two lines are parallel and why?
 

Solution


In a figure, sum of two interior angles 132o + 48o = 180o  [{tex}\therefore{/tex} equal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 180o]
Here, we see that the sum of two interior angles on the same side of n is 180o, then l and m are the parallel lines.
 
In the above figure, the sum of two interior angles 73o + 106o = 179o {tex}\ne{/tex} 180o.
Here, we see that the sum of two interior angles on the same side of r is not equal to 180o, then p and q are not parallel lines.

Q 10

Two lines l and m are perpendicular to the same line n, Are l and m perpendicular to each other? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

No, they are parallel.

sections.label: Exercise 6.3
sections.html:
Q 1

In the below fig. If OD is the bisector of ∠AOC, OE is the bisector of ∠BOC and OD ⊥ OE. Show that the points A, O and B are collinear.

Solution

Given: In figure, OD ⊥ OE (i.e. {tex}\angle D O E = 90 ^ { 0 }{/tex}), OD and OE are the bisectors of ∠AOC and ∠BOC.

To prove: points A, O and B are collinear i.e., AOB is a straight line.

Proof: From the fig. we have ∠AOB comprising ∠AOC and ∠BOC such that OD and OE are the bisectors of these two angles

∠AOB = ∠AOC + ∠BOC

Since, OD and OE bisect angles ∠AOC and ∠BOC respectively.

{tex}\therefore \angle A O C = 2 \angle D O C{/tex} ...(1)

And  {tex}\angle C O B = 2 \angle C O E{/tex} ...(2)

On adding equations (1) and (2), we get

{tex}\angle A O C + \angle C O B = 2 \angle D O C + 2 \angle C O E{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C + \angle C O B = 2 ( \angle D O C + \angle C O E ){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C + \angle C O B = 2 \angle D O E{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C + \angle C O B = 2 \times 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}{tex}[ \because O D \perp O E ]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C + \angle C O B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \angle A O B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

So, {tex}\angle A O C \space and \space \angle C O B{/tex} are forming linear pair or AOB is a straight line. Hence, points A, O and B are collinear.

Q 2

In the given figure, {tex}\angle 1 = 60^\circ{/tex} and {tex}\angle 6 = 120^\circ{/tex}. Show that the lines m and n are parallel.

Solution

We have,
{tex}\angle 5 + \angle 6 = 180^\circ{/tex}  [Angles of a linear pair]
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle 5 + {120^\circ} = {180^\circ}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \angle 5 = 180^\circ - 120^\circ = 60^\circ{/tex}
Now, {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 5{/tex} [Each = 60°]
But, these are corresponding angles.
Therefore, the lines m and n are parallel.

Q 3

AP and BQ are the bisectors of the two alternate interior angles formed by intersection of a transversal t with parallel lines l and m. See the given figure. Show that AP || BQ.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} l || m and t is the transversal
{tex}\angle MAB = \angle SBA{/tex} [Alt. {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\frac{1}{2}\angle MAB = \frac{1}{2}\angle SBA \Rightarrow \angle 2 = \angle 3{/tex}
But, {tex}\angle 2{/tex} and {tex}\angle 3{/tex} are alternate angles.
Hence, AP || BQ.

Q 4

If in figure, bisectors AP and BQ of the alternate interior angles are parallel, then show that l || m.

Solution

Given, In the figure  AP|| BQ, AP and BQ are the bisectors of alternate interior angles {tex}\angle{/tex}CAB and {tex}\angle{/tex}ABF.
To show: l || m
Proof Since, AP|| BQ and t is transversal, therefore {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABQ
[alternate interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}ABQ    [multiplying both sides by 2]
But, these are alternate angles.
Hence, the lines l and m are parallel.

Q 5

In a figure, BA || ED and BC || EF. Show that {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF
[Hint: Produce DE to intersect BC at P (say)]

 

Solution

Given BA || ED and BC || EF.
To show {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF.
Construction Draw a ray EP opposite to ray ED. 

Proof In the figure, BA || ED or BA || DP
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}EPC [corresponding angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}EPC ....(i)
Again, BC || EF or PC || EF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF = {tex}\angle{/tex}EPC [corresponding angles] ....(ii)
From eqs.(i) and (ii),
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF

Q 6

In the given figure, BA || ED and BC || EF. Show that {tex}\angle ABC + \angle DEF = 180^\circ{/tex}.

Solution

Produce ED to meet BC at P(say)

Now, EF||BC and EP is the transversal.
{tex}\therefore \;\angle DEF = \angle EPC = 180^\circ{/tex} ...(1)
Again, EP || AB and BC is the transversal.
{tex}\therefore \;\angle EPC = \angle ABC{/tex} …(2) [corresponding {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
From (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle DEF = \angle ABC = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle ABC + \angle DEF = 180^\circ{/tex}
Hence, proved.

Q 7

In given figure, DE || QR and AP and BP are bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB and {tex}\angle{/tex}RBA respectively. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}APB.

Solution

Given ED {tex}\parallel{/tex}RQ & AB is transversal.

Since interior angles on the same side of transversal are supplementary.

{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}EAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}RBA = 180° (see figure)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}EAB + {tex}\frac{1} {2}\angle{/tex}RBA = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 180° = 90° .......(1)

As AP and BP are bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB and {tex}\angle{/tex}RBA, respectively
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}EAB and {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}RBA .........(2) (from figure)

From (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 90° ..........(3)

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}1+ {tex}\angle{/tex}2+ {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = 180° (Angle sum property of triangle)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = 180° [Using (3)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = 180° - 90°

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = 90°.

Q 8

The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 2 : 3 : 4. Find the angles of the triangle.

Solution

Given: Ratio of angles is 2 : 3 : 4.
To find: Angles of triangle.
Proof: The ratio of angles of a triangle is 2 : 3 : 4.
Let the angles of a triangle be {tex}\angle A,\angle B{/tex} and {tex}\angle C{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\angle A = 2x{/tex}, then {tex}\angle B = 3x{/tex} and {tex}\angle C = 4x{/tex}.
In {tex}\Delta ABC,\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ {/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of angles of a triangle is 180°]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x + 3x + 4x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow 9x = 180^\circ \Rightarrow x = 180^\circ /9 = 20^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;\angle A = 2x = 2 \times 20^\circ = 40^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\angle B = 3x = 3 \times 20^\circ = 60^\circ{/tex}
And {tex}\angle C = 4x = 4 \times 20^\circ = 80^\circ{/tex}
Hence, the angles of the triangles are 40°, 60° and 80°.

Q 9

A triangle ABC is right angled at A. L is a point on BC such that AL {tex}\perp{/tex} BC. Prove that {tex}\angle BAL = \angle ACB{/tex}.

Solution


Given: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}
{tex}\angle A = 90^\circ{/tex} and {tex}AL \bot BC{/tex}.
To prove: {tex}\angle BAL = \angle ACB{/tex}.
Proof: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} and {tex}\Delta LAC{/tex},
{tex}\angle BAC = \angle ALC{/tex} …(1) [Each = 90°]
And {tex}\angle ABC = \angle ABL{/tex}. ...(2) [Common angle]
Adding equations (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle BAC + \angle ABC = \angle ALC + \angle ABC{/tex} …(3)
In {tex}\Delta ABC,\angle BAC + \angle ACB + \angle ABC = 180^\circ{/tex} [Sum of angles of triangle is 180°]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle BAC + \angle ABC = 180^\circ - \angle ACB{/tex} …(4)
In {tex}\Delta ABL,\angle ABL + \angle ALB + \angle BAL = 180^\circ {/tex} [Sum of the angles of triangle is 180°]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle ABL + \angle ALC = 180^\circ - \angle BAL{/tex} ...(5) {tex}\left[ {\angle ALC = \angle ALB = 90^\circ } \right]{/tex}
Substituting the value from equation (4) and (5) in equation (3), we get
{tex}180^\circ - \angle ACB = 180^\circ - \angle BAL{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \angle ACB = \angle BAL{/tex}
Hence, proved.

Q 10

Two lines are respectively perpendicular to two parallel lines. Show that they are parallel to each other.

Solution


{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = 90o, {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 90o, {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = 90o, {tex}\angle{/tex}4 = 90o . . . . [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3
These angles form a pair of equal corresponding angles
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EF || GH.

sections.label: Exercise 6.4
sections.html:
Q 1

If two lines intersect, prove that the vertically opposite angles are equal.

Solution

Let two lines AB and CD intersect at point O.

To prove: {tex}\angle A O C = \angle B O D{/tex} (vertically opposite angles )
{tex}\angle A O D = \angle B O C{/tex} (vertically opposite angles )

Proof: (i) Since, ray OA stands on the line CD.

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle AOC + \angle AOD {/tex}= 180 °…(1)[Linear pair axiom]

Also, ray OD stands on the line AB.

{tex}\angle A O D + \angle B O D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} …(2) [Linear pair axiom ]

From equations (1) and (2), we get

{tex}\angle A O C + \angle A O D = \angle A O D + \angle B O D{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C = \angle B O D{/tex}

Hence, proved.

(ii) Since, ray OD stands on the line AB.

{tex}\therefore \angle A O D + \angle B O D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}…(3) [Linear pair axiom]

Also, ray OB stands on the line CD.

 {tex}\therefore \angle D O B + \angle B O C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} …(4) [linear pair axiom ]

From equations (3) and (4), we get

{tex}\angle AOD + \angle BOD = \angle BOD + \angle BOC{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle AOD = \angle BOC{/tex}

Hence, proved.

Q 2

Bisectors of interior {tex}\angle{/tex}B and exterior {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC intersect at the point T. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}BTC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{BAC}{/tex}

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}C is an exterior angle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle {/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle {/tex}ABC + {tex}\angle {/tex}CAB     [exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of two opposite angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \angle A C D=\frac{1}{2} \angle C A B+\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C{/tex}     [dividing both sides by 2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle T C D=\frac{1}{2} \angle C A B+\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C{/tex} .....(i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} CT is a bisector of {tex}\angle A C D \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \angle A C D=\angle T C D{/tex}]
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BTC, {tex}\angle T C D=\angle B T C+\angle C B T{/tex} 
[exterior anlge of a triangle is equal to the sum of two opposite interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle T C D=\angle B T C+\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C{/tex} .....(ii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} BT bisects of {tex}\angle A B C \Rightarrow \angle C B T=\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C{/tex} ]
From Eqs.(i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \angle C A B+\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C=\angle B T C+\frac{1}{2} \angle A B C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle B T C=\frac{1}{2} \angle C A B{/tex} 
or {tex}\angle B T C=\frac{1}{2} \angle B A C{/tex}

Q 3

A transversal intersects two parallel lines, prove that the bisectors of any pair of corresponding angles so formed are parallel.

Solution


Let a transversal EF cuts two parallel lines AB and CD at point G and H respectively.
Also, let GL and HM are respectively the bisectors of a pair of corresponding angles {tex}\angle{/tex}EGB and {tex}\angle{/tex}GHD.
To prove: GL {tex}\parallel{/tex} HM
Proof: Since AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} CD and EF is a transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}EGB = {tex}\angle{/tex}GHD (Corresponding angles) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}EGB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}GHD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EGL = {tex}\angle{/tex}GHM (from figure)
But EGL & GHM are corresponding angles formed by the lines GL and HM & corresponding angles formed by the lines can be equal only when the lines are parallel.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} GL {tex}\parallel{/tex} HM   Hence, proved.

Q 4

Prove that through a given point we can draw only one perpendicular to a given line.
[Hint: Use proof by contradiction]

Solution


Suppose we draw two lines PM and PN from a given point P to a given line AB.
Considering two lines are perpendicular then {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = 90o and {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 90 o
But these two angles form a pair of equal corresponding angles.
PM || PN
Two parallel lines never intersect so this is contradiction.
This proves that we can draw only one perpendicular to a given line through a given point.

Q 5

Prove that two lines that are respectively perpendicular to two intersecting lines intersect each other.
[Hint: Use proof by contradiction].

Solution


Let n and p be two intersecting lines, Let {tex}\perp{/tex} l  n and m {tex}\perp{/tex} p.
To Prove : l and m intersect
Now l {tex}\perp{/tex} n . . . . [Given]
and l || m . . . . [By supposition]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m {tex}\perp{/tex} n . . . . . (i)
m {tex}\perp{/tex} p . . . .[Given] . . . . (ii)
n || p . . . . [From (i) and (ii)] is contradiction since n and p intersect.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  l and m intersect.

Q 6

Prove that a triangle must have at least two acute angles.

Solution

If the triangle is an acute angled triangle, then all its three angles are acute angle. Each of these angles is less than 90°, so they can make three angles sum equal to 180°.
If a triangle is an obtused angled triangle, then one angle which is obtuse will be more than 90° but less than 180°, so the other two acute angles can make the three angles sum equal to 180°.
If a triangle is a right triangle, then one angle which is right angle will be equal to 90° and the other two acute angles can make the three angles sum equal to 180°.
Hence, we can say that a triangle must have a least two acute angles.

Q 7

In the given figure, {tex}\angle Q > \angle R{/tex}, PA is the bisector of {tex}\angle QPR{/tex} and {tex}PM \bot QR{/tex}. Prove that {tex}\angle APM = \frac{1}{2}(\angle Q - \angle R){/tex}

Solution

Given: {tex}\triangle PQR,\angle Q < \angle R,{/tex} PA is the bisector of {tex}\angle QPR{/tex} and {tex}PM \bot QR{/tex}.
To prove: {tex}\angle APM = \frac{1}{2}(\angle Q - \angle R){/tex}
Proof: Since, PA is the bisector of {tex}\angle QPR{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle QPA = \angle APR{/tex} …(1)
In {tex}\angle PQM,\angle Q + \angle PMQ + \angle QPM = 180^\circ{/tex} [Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q + 90^\circ + \angle QPM = 180^\circ{/tex} {tex}[\therefore \angle PMR = 90^\circ ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q = 90^\circ - \angle QPM{/tex} …(2)
In {tex}\triangle PMR,\angle PMR + \angle R + \angle RPM = 180^\circ{/tex} [Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow 90^\circ + \angle R + \angle RPM = 180^\circ{/tex} {tex}[\because \angle PMR = 90^\circ ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle R = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - \angle RPM{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q = 90^\circ - \angle QPM{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle PRM = 90^\circ - \angle RPM{/tex} …(3)
Subtracting equation (3) from equation (2), we get
{tex}\angle Q - \angle R = (90^\circ - \angle QPM) - (90^\circ - \angle RPM){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q - \angle R = \angle RPM - \angle QPM{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q - \angle R = (\angle RPM + \angle APM){/tex}{tex}- (\angle QPA - \angle APM){/tex} …(4)
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle Q - \angle R = \angle QPA + \angle APM{/tex}{tex} - \angle QPA + \angle APM{/tex} [Using equation (1)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle Q - \angle R = 2\angle APM{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle APM = \frac{1}{2}(\angle Q - \angle R){/tex}
Hence, proved.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

If two interior angles on the same side of a transversal intersecting two parallel lines are in the ratio 2 : 3, then the greatest of two angles is

Q 2

Let OA, OB, OC and OD are rays in the anticlockwise direction such that {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = 100°, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 82° and {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 78°. Is it true to say that AOC and BOD are lines?

Solution

AOC is not a line, because {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB + {tex}\angle{/tex}COB = 100° + 82° = 182°, which is not equal to 180°.
Similarly, BOD is also not a line.

Q 3

A transversal intersects two lines in such a way that the two interior angles on the same side of the transversal are equal. Will the two lines always be parallel? Give reason for your answer.

Solution


Let the transversal l intersects two given m and n such that the interior angles on the same side of the transversal are equal, i.e. {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
If the lines m and n are parallel, then
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 180o
But {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 90o

Q 4

In the figure, AB, CD and EF are three lines concurrent at O. Find the value of y.

Solution


From the given figure: 
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BOF = 5y  (Vertically opposite angles)
Also,
{tex}\angle{/tex}COE + {tex}\angle{/tex}AOE + {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 180°
So, 2y + 5y + 2y = 180°
or, 9y = 180°, which gives y = 20°.

Q 5

In a figure, x = y and a = b. Prove that l || n.

Solution

x = y (Given)
Therefore, l || m (Corresponding angles) (i)
Also, a = b (Given)
Therefore, n || m (Corresponding angles) (ii)
From (i) and (ii), l || n (Lines parallel to the same line).
 

Q 6

In figure, m and n are two plane mirrors perpendicular to each other. Show that the incident ray CA is parallel to reflected ray BD.

Solution

At B, draw BO ⊥ n and at A. draw AO ⊥ m.
Let BO and AO meet at O.

as, Perpendiculars to two perpendicular lines are also perpendicular.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 90o
In {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}AOB, {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB + {tex}\angle{/tex}OAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}OBA = 180o
(as,The sum of the three angles of a triangle is 180o )
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90o + {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}CAB + {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 180o
( as, By law of reflection, Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CAO = {tex}\angle{/tex}OAB = {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}CAB and {tex}\angle{/tex}ABO = {tex}\angle{/tex}OBD = {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}ABD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1\over2{/tex}({tex}\angle{/tex}CAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD) = 90o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 180o
But these angles from a pair of supplementary consecutive interior angles.
∴ Ray CA || Ray BD.

Q 7

Prove that the sum of three angles of a triangle is 180°.

Solution


Given: {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABC
To Prove: {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
Construction: Through A draw XY {tex}\parallel{/tex} BC
Proof: XY  {tex}\parallel{/tex} BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} Alternate interior angle
And {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}5 ...(ii)
Adding eq (i)  and (ii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 + {tex}\angle{/tex}5
Adding {tex}\angle{/tex}1 to both sides:-
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}4 + {tex}\angle{/tex}5
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = 180° ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1, {tex}\angle{/tex}4 and {tex}\angle{/tex}5 forms a line)
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°

Q 8

Bisectors of angles B and C of a triangle ABC intersect each other at the point O. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 90o + {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A.

Solution

BO is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC ........ (1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC = {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}B
 CO is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB ={tex}1\over2{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB ={tex}1\over2{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C. ......... (2)
In {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}OBC,
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB = 180o
(The sum of the three angles of a {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex} is 180o)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC +{tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o From (1) and (2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180o– {tex}1\over2{/tex}({tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C) ....... (3)
In {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}ABC,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o
(The sum of the three angles of a triangle is 180o)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o – {tex}\angle{/tex}A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1\over2{/tex}({tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C) = {tex}{180^0-A\over2}{/tex} = 90o –{tex}1\over2{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A ......... (4)
From (3) and (4), we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180o – {90o – {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A} = 90o + {tex}1\over2{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A.

sections.label: Exercise 7.2
sections.html:
Q 1

In triangles ABC and PQR, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}R. Which side {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR should be equal to side AB of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC so that the two triangles are congruent? Give reason for your answer.

Solution


In triangle ABC and PQR, we have
{tex}\angle A=\angle Q{/tex} [Given]
{tex}\angle B=\angle R{/tex} [Given]
For the triangle to be congruent, we must AB = QR. They will be congruent by ASA congruence rule.

Q 2

If two sides and an angle of one triangle are equal to two sides and an angle of another triangle, then the two triangles must be congruent. Is the statement true? Why?

Q 3

If two angles and a side of one triangle are equal to two angles and a side of another triangle, then the two triangles must be congruent. Is the statement true? Why?

Q 4

Is it possible to construct a triangle with lengths of its sides as 4 cm, 3 cm and 7 cm? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

We know that the sum of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the third side.
Here, the sum of two sides whose lengths are
4 cm and 3 cm = 4 cm + 3 cm = 7 cm,
Which is equal to the length of third side, i.e., 7 cm.
Hence, it is not possible to construct a triangle with lengths of sides 4 cm, 3 cm and 7 cm.

Q 5

It is given that {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}RPQ. Is it true to say that BC = QR? Why?

Solution

It is False that BC = QR because BC = PQ as {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex}RPQ.

Q 6

It is given that {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}EDF, then is it true to say that PR = EF? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

Yes, PR = EF because they are the corresponding sides of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR and {tex}\triangle{/tex}EDF.

Q 7

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 70° and {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 30°. Which side of this triangle is the longest? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, we have
{tex}\angle {/tex}Q = 180o - ({tex}\angle {/tex}P+ {tex}\angle {/tex}R)
= 180o- (70o + 30o) = 180o - 100o = 80o
Now, in{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR, ∠Q is  larger (greater) and side opposite to greater angle is longer.
Hence, PR is the longest side.

Q 8

AD is a median of the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC. Is it true that AB + BC + CA > 2AD ? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

Since, the sum of two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.
Therefore, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD,
AB + BD > AD ...(i)
in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD,
AC + CD > AD ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
AB + AC + (BD + CD) > AD + AD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB + BC + CA > 2AD. [ From figure, BD + CD = BC ]
Hence, AB + BC + CA > 2AD is true.

Q 9

M is a point on side BC of a triangle ABC such that AM is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC. Is it true to say that perimeter of the triangle is greater than 2 AM? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

We have to prove that
AB + BC + AC > 2AM.
As sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side,
So in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABM, we have
AB + BM > AM…(1)
And in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACM, AC + CM > AM…(2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
AB + BM + AC + CM > 2AM
Or AB + (BM + CM) + AC > 2AM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB + BC + AC > 2AM
Hence, it is true to say that perimeter of the triangle is greater than 2AM.

Q 10

Is it possible to construct a triangle with lengths of its sides as 9 cm, 7 cm and 17 cm? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

No, it is not possible to construct a triangle whose sides are 9cm, 7cm and 17cm because
9cm + 7cm = 16cm < 17cm
Whereas sum of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the third side.

Q 11

Is it possible to construct a triangle with lengths of its sides as 8 cm, 7 cm and 4 cm? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

Yes, it is possible to construct a triangle with lengths of sides as 8 cm, 7 cm and 4 cm as sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.

sections.label: Exercise 7.3
sections.html:
Q 1

ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC and BD and CE are its two medians. Show that BD = CE.

Solution

Given: {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} with AB = AC

And AD = CD, AE = BE.
To prove: BD = CE
Proof: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} we have
AB = AC [Given]
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\frac{1}{2}AB = \frac{1}{2}AC{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = AD
[{tex}\because{/tex} D is the mid-point of AC and E is the mid-point of AB]
Now, in {tex}\Delta ABD{/tex} and {tex}\Delta ACE{/tex}, we have
AB = AC [Given]
{tex}\angle A = \angle A{/tex} (Common angle]
AE = AD [Proved above]
SO, by SAS criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\Delta ABD \cong \Delta ACE{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BD = CE [CPCT]
Hence, proved.

Q 2

In the given figure, D and E are points on side BC of a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} such that BD = CE and AD = AE. Show that {tex}\triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACE{/tex}.

Solution

Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC in which BD = CE and AD = AE.
To Prove: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD ≅ {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE
Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADE, we have
AD = AE [Given]
 {tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle 2 = 1{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Angle opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
Now, {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 3 = 180^\circ{/tex} ...(1) [Linear pair axiom]
{tex}\angle 2 + \angle 4 = 180^\circ{/tex} …(2) [Linear pair axiom]
From equations (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle 1 + \angle 3 = \angle 2 + \angle 4{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \angle 1 = \angle 2} \right]{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE, we have
AD = AE [Given]
{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} [Proved above]
BD = CE [Given]
So, by SAS criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD ≅ {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE
Hence, proved

Q 3

CDE is an equilateral triangle formed on a side CD of a square ABCD (Fig.). Show that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADE {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex} BCE.

Solution

The right one is to prove triangle ADE is congurent to triangle BCE
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD ( 90°) ...(1)
{tex}\angle{/tex}CDE = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCE ( 60°) ....(2)
by adding (1) and (2) we get
{tex}\angle{/tex} ADE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCE
AD = BC ( sides of square )
DE = CE ( sides of euilateral triangle )
so by SAS criteria triangles are congurent.

Q 4

In Fig., BA {tex}\perp{/tex} AC, DE {tex}\perp{/tex} DF such that BA = DE and BF = EC. Show that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}DEF.

Solution

We have BF = EC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BF + FC = CE + FC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = EF
In {tex}\triangle A B C, \angle A=90^{\circ}{/tex} and in {tex}\triangle D E F, \angle D=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle D E F{/tex} are right triangles.
Now, in right triangles ABC and DEF, we have
BA = DE[Given]
{tex}\angle A=\angle D{/tex} (Each 90o)
And BC = EF [Proved above]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\triangle A B C \cong \triangle D E F{/tex} [By RHS congruence rule]

Q 5

Q is a point on the side SR of a {tex}\triangle{/tex} PSR such that PQ = PR. Prove that PS > PQ.

Solution

Given: PQ = PR

To prove: PS > PQ
Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex} PRQ, we have
PR = PQ ..........(1) 
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 1 = \angle R{/tex}.......(2)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of triangle are equal]
But in {tex}\triangle​​ PSQ{/tex}; {tex}\angle 1 > \angle S{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Exterior angle is greater than each of its opposite interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle R > \angle S \quad{/tex}[ from (2) ]
So , in {tex}\triangle PRS ,{/tex}{tex} P S > P R{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} In a triangle, side opposite to the larger angle is larger]
{tex}\Rightarrow P S > P Q{/tex}  [From (1) ]
Hence, proved.

Q 6

S is any point on side QR of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR. Show that: PQ + QR + RP > 2PS.

Solution

Given: A Point S on side QR of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR.

To prove: PQ + QR + RP > 2PS
Proof: In ∆PQS, we have
PQ + QS > PS…(1)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of the length of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side]
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}PSR, we have
RS + RP > PS…(2)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of the length of any two sides of triangle must be greater than the third side]
Adding (1) and (2), we get
PQ + QS + RS + RP > 2PS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ + QR + RP > 2PS
Hence, proved.

Q 7

D is any point on side AC of a △ ABC with AB = AC. Show that CD < BD.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABC, we have
AB = AC [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle {/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB
[{tex}\because{/tex}Angles opp. to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
Now, {tex}\angle {/tex}DBC < {tex}\angle {/tex}ABC
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}DBC <  {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB or {tex}\angle {/tex}DBC < {tex}\angle {/tex}DCB (As, AC = AB)
Hence, CD > BD. [{tex}\because{/tex}Side opposite a greater angle is longer]

Q 8

In given Fig., l||m and M is the mid-point of a line segment AB. Prove that M is also the mid-point of any line segment CD, having its end points on l and m, respectively.
 

Solution

In {tex}\Delta AMC{/tex} and {tex}\Delta BMD{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex} [Alt. {tex}\angle s{/tex} because l||m]
{tex}\angle 2 = \angle 4{/tex} [Vert. opp. {tex}\angle s{/tex} ]
 
AM = BM [Given]
{tex}\therefore \;\Delta AMC \cong \Delta BMD{/tex} [By ASS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore \;CM = DM{/tex} [CPCT]
Hence, M is also the mid-point of CD.

Q 9

Bisectors of the angles B and C of an isosceles triangle with AB = AC intersect each other at O. BO is produced to a point M. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex} MOC = {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC.

Solution

Bisector of the angles B and C of an isosceles triangle ABC with AB = AC intersect each other at O. BO is produced to a point M.Figure is drawn below.

In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, we have

AC = AB. [ Given ] 

{tex}\therefore \angle A B C = \angle A C B{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]

Dividing both sides by 2, we get:-

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A C B{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle O B C = \angle O C B{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex} BO and CO are bisectors of {tex}\angle B{/tex} and {tex}\angle C{/tex}]

 {tex}\Rightarrow \angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} (from figure)......(1)

In {tex}\Delta O B C{/tex}, Exterior angle {tex}\angle M O C = \angle 1 + \angle 2{/tex}

[{tex}\because{/tex} Exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of interior opposite angles]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle M O C = 2 \angle 1{/tex} [Since {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}] .............. (2)                      

 Also ,from figure, {tex}\angle1 = \frac {1} {2} \angle ABC{/tex}.........(3){tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]

From (2) & (3):-

 {tex}\angle M O C = 2 × \frac 1 2 \angle A B C{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle MOC = \frac 1 2 \angle ABC{/tex}.
Hence Proved.

Q 10

Bisectors of the angles B and C of an isosceles triangle ABC with AB = AC intersect each other at O. Show that external angle which is adjacent to {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC is equal to {tex}\angle{/tex} BOC.

Solution

In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} , OB & OC are angular bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC & {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB respectively. Side AB is extended to point D so that {tex}\angle DBC {/tex} is external angle of triangle adjacent to {tex}\angle ABC{/tex}.

To prove :- {tex}\angle DBC = \angle BOC{/tex}

Proof :-

Given, AB = AC

{tex}\therefore \angle B = \angle C{/tex}

[{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]

{tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle B = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle C{/tex} …(1)

 In {tex}\Delta OBC,{/tex} we have

{tex}\angle 1 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle B{/tex}

And {tex}\angle 2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle C{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [By (1)]

{tex}\angle D B C + \angle 1 + \angle O B A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} ABD is a straight line, so using linear pair axiom ]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle D B C + 2 \angle 1 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Since, {tex}\angle 1 = \angle OBA{/tex} ,as OB is angular bisector of angle ABC]…(2)

In {tex}\Delta O B C{/tex},

{tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 + \angle B O C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Angle sum property of triangle ]

{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle 1 + \angle B O C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \angle 1 = \angle 2 ]{/tex} …...(3)

From (2) and (3), we get

{tex}\angle D B C + 2 \angle 1 = 2 \angle 1 + \angle B O C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle D B C = \angle B O C{/tex}.  Hence, Proved.

Q 11

In the given figure, AD is the bisector of {tex}\angle BAC{/tex}. Prove that AB > BD.
 

Solution

Since exterior angle of a triangle is greater than either of the interior opposite angles, therefore, in {tex}\Delta ACD{/tex},
Ext {tex}\angle 3 > \angle 2 \Rightarrow \angle 3 > \angle 1{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} AD is the bisector of {tex}\angle BAC{/tex}, so {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}]
Now, in {tex}\Delta ABD{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 3 > \angle 1{/tex}
Hence, AB > BD. [{tex}\because{/tex} In a triangle, side opposite to greater angle is longer]

sections.label: Exercise 7.4
sections.html:
Q 1

Find all the angles of an equilateral triangle.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex},we have
AB = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle C=\angle B{/tex}…(1)
[{tex}\because{/tex}Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
BC = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle A=\angle B{/tex}…(2)
[{tex}\because{/tex}Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
Now, {tex}\angle A+\angle B+\angle C=180^{\circ}{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex}Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle A+\angle A+\angle A=180^{\circ}{/tex} [From (1) and (2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \angle A=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle A=\frac{180^{\circ}}{3}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle A=\angle B=\angle C=60^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 2

The image of an object placed at a point A before a plane mirror LM is seen at point B by an observer at D as shown in the figure. Prove that the image is as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of the mirror. [Hint: CN is normal to the mirror. Also, angle of incidence = angle of reflection].

Solution

Let AB intersect LM at O. We have to prove that AO = BO.

Now, {tex}\angle i = \angle r{/tex} …(1)

[{tex}\because{/tex} Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection]

Since , BA {tex}\parallel{/tex}CN & BD intersects both ,hence 

{tex}\angle B = \angle r{/tex}  [Corres. {tex}\angle s{/tex} ] …(2)

Since , BA {tex}\parallel{/tex} CN & AC intersects both, hence

{tex}\angle A = \angle i{/tex}  [Alternate int. {tex}\angle s{/tex} ]…(3)

From (1), (2) and (3), we get

{tex}\angle B = \angle A{/tex}.........(4)

Also, {tex}\angle BOC = \angle AOC {/tex} (each 90° ) .......(5)

Subtracting both sides of equation (5) from 90° , we get :-

    90° - {tex}\angle BOC {/tex} = 90° - {tex}\angle AOC{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle BCO = \angle ACO{/tex} ..........(6)

Now, in  {tex}\Delta B O C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta A O C{/tex} we have

{tex}\angle BOC = \angle AOC{/tex} [Each = 90o]

  OC = OC[Common side]

{tex}\angle B C O = \angle A C O{/tex}  [from (6)]

{tex}\therefore \Delta B O C \cong \Delta A O C{/tex} [ASA congruence rule]

Hence, AO = BO[CPCT].   

Q 3

ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC and D is a point on BC such that AD{tex}\perp{/tex}BC (Fig.). To prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD, a student proceeded as follows:

In △ ABD and △ACD,
AB = AC (Given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C (because AB = AC)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC
Therefore, △ABD = △ ACD (AAS)
So, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD (CPCT)
What is the defect in the above arguments?
[Hint: Recall how {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C is proved when AB = AC].

Solution

In the above arguments firstly if AB = AC Then {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}B and after that he/she poved {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACD (AAS) which must be {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}ACD. We can prove it in simpler way as shown below: 
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC, we have

{tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC [{tex}\because{/tex}Each equal to 90o]
AB = AC [Given]
AD = AD [Common side]
So, by RHS criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}ACD
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle B A D=\angle C A D{/tex} [CPCT]
Hence, proved

Q 4

P is a point on the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC. If the line through P, parallel to BA meet BC at Q, prove that BPQ is an isosceles triangle.

Solution

We draw diagram acoording to given conditions in the given problem. We have to prove that BPQ is an isosceles triangle.

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} …....(1)   [{tex}\because{/tex} BP is the bisector of {tex}\angle A B C{/tex}]

Now, PQ is parallel to BA and BP cuts them

{tex}\therefore \angle 1 = \angle 3{/tex} [Alternate angles  ​​​​​]…(2)

From (1) and (2), we get

{tex}\angle 2 = \angle 3{/tex}

In {tex}\Delta B P Q{/tex} we have

{tex}\angle 2 = \angle 3{/tex} [Proved above]

{tex}\therefore P Q = B Q{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Side opposite to equal angles are equal]

Hence, BPQ is an isosceles triangle.

Q 5

ABCD is a quadrilateral in which AB = BC and AD = CD. Show that BD bisects both the angles ABC and ADC.

Solution

In {tex}\Delta ABD{/tex} and {tex}\Delta CBD{/tex}, We have

AB = BC [Given]
AD = CD [Given]
BD = BD [Common side]
{tex}\therefore \Delta ABD \cong \Delta CBD{/tex} [By SSS congruence rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [CPCT]
And {tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}
Hence, BD bisects both the angle ABC and ADC.

Q 6

ABC is a right triangle with AB = AC. Bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A meets BC at D. Prove that  BC = 2 AD.

Solution

Given: ∆ABC is a right angled triangle. Bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A meets BC at D. 

Also, given AB = AC & {tex}\angle A = 90°{/tex}......(1)

To prove: BC = 2AD

Proof: 

Now, in {tex}\Delta C A D{/tex} and {tex}\Delta B A D{/tex} , we have :-

  AC = AB  [from (1) ]

{tex}\angle CAD = \angle BAD{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} AD is the bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex}]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}.  [See figure]

AD = AD [Common side]

So, By SAS criterion of congruency of triangles, we have

{tex}\Delta C A D \cong \Delta B A D{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \quad C D = B D{/tex}  [CPCT]

Hence , D is midpoint of hypotenuse AC.

 Since ,Mid-point of hypotenuse of a rt. {tex}\Delta{/tex} is equidistant from the vertices of the {tex}\Delta{/tex} .

Hence, AD = BD = CD .........(2)

Now, BC = BD + CD

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B C = A D + A D{/tex} [Using (2)]

{tex}\Rightarrow B C = 2 A D{/tex}

Hence, proved.

Q 7

O is a point in the interior of a square ABCD such that OAB is an equilateral triangle. Show that {tex}\Delta{/tex} OCD is an isosceles triangle.

Solution

Given: ABCD is a square & O is an interior point. ∆ OAB is an equilateral triangle. 

Hence, OA = OB = AB.....(1)

So, {tex}\angle OAB = \angle OBA{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}.....(2)

TO prove: {tex}\Delta O C D{/tex} is an isosceles triangle.

Proof: In square ABCD,

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} …....(3)

[{tex}\because{/tex} Each equal to 90o]

Now, in {tex}\Delta O A B{/tex}, we have

{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} ......…(4) [ from (2)]

Subtracting (2) from (1), we get

{tex}\angle 1 - \angle 3 = \angle 2 - \angle 4{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 5 = \angle 6{/tex}. [see figure ].....(5)

Now, in {tex}\Delta D A O{/tex} and {tex}\Delta C B O{/tex}

AD = BC [sides of square]

{tex}\angle 5 = \angle 6{/tex} [from (5)]

OA = OB [from (1)]

So, By SAS criterion of congruency of triangles , we have

{tex}\Delta D A O \cong \Delta C B O{/tex}

{tex}\therefore O D = O C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta O C D{/tex} is an isosceles triangle.

Hence, proved.

Q 8

∆ABC and ∆DBC are two triangles on the same base BC such that A and D lie on the opposite sides of BC, AB = AC and DB = DC. Show that AD is the perpendicular bisector of BC.

Solution

Given: {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta D B C{/tex} are on the same base BC.

Also given,     AB = AC and BD = DC........(1)

To prove:  AD is the perpendicular bisector of BC. i.e., AD {tex}\perp{/tex} BC & OB = OC

Proof: In {tex}\Delta B A D{/tex} and {tex}\Delta C A D{/tex} , we have :-

AB = AC     [from (1) ]

BD = CD    [ from (1) ]

AD = AD   [ Common side ]

So, by SSS criterion of congruency of triangles, we have

{tex}\Delta B A D \cong \Delta C A D{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle DAB =\angle DAC{/tex} [ CPCT]

{tex}\therefore \angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} ........(2)

Now, in {tex}\Delta B A O{/tex}  and {tex}\Delta C A O{/tex} , we have :-

  AB = AC      [from (1) ]

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}   [ From (2)]

AO = AO    [Common side]

So, by SAS criterion of congruency of triangles, we have

{tex}\Delta B A O \cong \Delta C A O{/tex}

{tex}\therefore B O = C O{/tex} [CPCT] .......(3)

&, {tex}\angle AOB = \angle AOC{/tex} [CPCT]

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} .........(4)

But,{tex}\angle 3 + \angle 4 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [angles on the same line ]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 3 + \angle 3 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[ from (4) ]

{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle 3 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 3 = \frac { 180 ^ { \circ } } { 2 } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow AD \perp BC{/tex} .......(5)

Hence ,from (3) & (5)

 AD is perpendicular bisector of BC [{tex}\because{/tex} BO = CO & {tex}AD \perp BC{/tex}]

Hence, proved.

Q 9

ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AC = BC. AD and BE are respectively two altitudes to sides BC and AC. Prove that AE = BD.

Solution

In {tex}\Delta ADC{/tex} and {tex}\Delta BEC{/tex} we have

AC = BC [Given] …(1)
{tex}\angle ADC = \angle BEC{/tex} [Each = 90°]
{tex}\angle ACD = \angle BCE{/tex} [Common angle]
{tex}\therefore \;\Delta ADC \cong \Delta BEC{/tex} [By SSS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore \;CE = CD{/tex} …(2) [CPCT]
Subtracting (2) from (1), we get
AC - CE = BC - CD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = BD
Hence, proved.

Q 10

Prove that the sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than twice the median with respect to the third side.

Solution


Given: AD is a median in {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}.
To prove: AB + AC > 2AD 
Construction: Produce AD to E such that AD = DE. Join EC
Proof: In triangles ADB and EDC, we have 
AD = DE........(1) (By construction)
BD = DC ({tex}\because{/tex} AD is the median) and, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC (Vertically opposite angles)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}EDC (SAS congruency criterion)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = EC (CPCT) ...(2)
As sum of two sides in a triangle is greater than the third side. Hence, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC, we have 
AC + EC > AE. 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC + AB > AE [ from (2) ] ........(3)
Also, AE = 2AD [from (1) ]... ...(4)
Now, from  (3) and (4) ,
AC + AB >2AD .Hence, proved.

Q 11

Show that in a quadrilateral ABCD, AB + BC + CD + DA < 2 (BD + AC).

Solution

Given: A quadrilateral ABCD in which AB,BC,CD and DA are its sides.


To prove: AB + BC + CD + DA < 2(BD + AC)
Proof: In {tex}\triangle A O B{/tex}we have
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} OA + OB > AB…(1)
[{tex}\because{/tex}Sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side]
In {tex}\triangle B O C{/tex}, we have
OB + OC > BC…(2) [Same reason]
In {tex}\triangle C O D{/tex}, we have
OC + OD > CD…(3) [Same reason]
In  {tex}\triangle D O A{/tex}, we have
OD + OA > DA…(4) [ Same reason]
Adding (1), (2), (3) and (4), we get
OA + OB + OB + OC + OC + OD + OD + OA > AB + BC + CD + DA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(OA + OB + OC + OD) > AB + BC + CD + DA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{(OA + OC) + (OB + OD)} > AB + BC + CD + DA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(AC + BD) > AB + BC + CD + DA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB + BC + CD + DA < 2(BD + AC)
Hence, proved.

Q 12

Show that in a quadrilateral ABCD. AB + BC + CD + DA > AC + BD

Solution

Given: A quadrilateral ABCD.
To prove: AB + BC + CD + DA > AC + BD
Proof: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, we have

AB + BC > AC…(1) [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side]
In {tex}\Delta BCD{/tex}, we have
BC + CD > BD...(2) [Same reason]
In {tex}\Delta CDA{/tex}, we have
CD + DA > AC…(3) [Same reason]
In {tex}\Delta DAB{/tex}, we have
AD + AB > BD…(4) [Same reason]
Adding (1), (2), (3) and (4), we get
AB + BC + BC + CD + CD + DA + AD + AB > AC + BD + AC + BD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2AB + 2BC + 2CD + 2DA > 2AC + 2BD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(AB + BC + CD + DA) > 2(AC + BD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB + BC + CD + DA > AC + BD
Hence, proved.

Q 13

In a right triangle, ABC, D is the mid-point of side AC such that BD = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AC. Show that {tex}\angle ABC{/tex} is a right angle.

Solution

We have to prove that ∠ABC = 90°.
As D is the mid-point of AC,
So, AD = DC
Also, {tex}BD = \frac{1}{2}AC = AD{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} D is the mid-point of AC]
{tex}\because{/tex} BD = AD = DC

In {tex}\Delta ABD{/tex} we have
BD = AD
{tex}\therefore \;\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} …(1) [{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
In {tex}\Delta BCD{/tex} we have
BD = DC,
{tex}\therefore \;\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} …(2) [{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 1 + \angle ABC + \angle 4 = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle 1 + \angle 2 + \angle 3 + \angle 4 = 180^\circ{/tex}{tex}[\because \angle ABC + \angle 3 + \angle 2]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2(\angle 2 + \angle 3) = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle 2 + \angle 3 = 90^\circ{/tex} [By (1) and (2)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle ABC = 90^\circ{/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 14

In a right triangle, prove that the line-segment joining the mid-point of the hypotenuse to the opposite vertex is half the hypotenuse.

Solution

ABC is a right triangle, right-angled at B and D is the mid-point of AC.
We have to prove that {tex}B D=\frac{1}{2} A C{/tex}

 
Now, produce BD to E such that BD = DE. Join EC.
In {tex}\triangle A D B{/tex} and {tex}\triangle C D E{/tex} we have
AD = CD  [{tex}\because{/tex}D is the mid-point of AC]
{tex}\angle A D B=\angle C D E{/tex}   [Vertically opposite {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
BD = DE [By construction]
{tex}\triangle A D B \cong \triangle C D E{/tex} [By SAS criterion of congruence]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = EC [CPCT]
And {tex}\angle 1=\angle 2{/tex} [CPCT]
But, {tex}\angle 1{/tex} and {tex}\angle 2{/tex} are alternate angles.
{tex}\therefore {/tex}  EC || BA
Now, EC is parallel to BA and BC is the transversal
{tex}\therefore \angle A B C+\angle B C E=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 90^{\circ}+\angle B C E=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B C E=180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle E B C{/tex} we have
BC = BC [Common side]
AB = EC [Proved above]
{tex}\angle C B A=\angle B C E{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex}Each = 90o]
{tex}\triangle A B C \cong \triangle E B C{/tex} [By SAS criterion of congruence]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AC = EB [CPCT]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} A C=\frac{1}{2} E B \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} A C=B D{/tex}
Hence, {tex}B D=\frac{1}{2} A C{/tex}

Q 15

Two lines l and m intersect at the point O and P is a point on a line n passing through the point O such that P is equidistant from l and m. Prove that n is the bisector of the angle formed by l and m.

Solution

Given: Lines, l and m and n intersect at point O. P is a point on line n and such that P is equidistance from l and n. Hence, PQ=PR. Figure is drawn below accordingly.

To prove: line n is the bisector of {tex}\angle Q O R{/tex}

Proof: In {tex}\Delta O Q P{/tex} and {tex}\Delta O R P{/tex} we have

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}

[{tex}\because{/tex} Each equal to 90o]

OP = OP[Common side]

PQ = PR [Since, P is equidistant from line l & m]

So, by RHS criterion of congruence, we have

{tex}\Delta O Q P \cong \Delta O R P{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}

So, line  n is bisector of {tex}\angle Q O R{/tex}

Hence, proved.

Q 16

Line segment joining the mid-points M and N of parallel sides AB and DC, respectively of a trapezium ABCD is perpendicular to both the sides AB and DC. Prove that AD = BC.

Solution

Join MD and CM.
We have,{tex}\angle D N M=\angle N M B{/tex} [Alt. {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore A B \| C D{/tex}
 
Now, in {tex}\triangle D M N{/tex} and {tex}\triangle C N M{/tex}
CN = DN
[{tex}\because{/tex} N is the mid-point of DC]
{tex}\angle D N M=\angle C N M{/tex}   [Each = 90o]
NM = NM [Common side]
{tex}\therefore \triangle D M N \approx \triangle C N M{/tex} [By SAS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore D M=C M{/tex} and {tex}\angle N M C=\angle N M D{/tex}…(1) [CPCT]
Now, {tex}\angle A M N=\angle B M N{/tex}[Each = 90o]
And{tex}\angle N M D=\angle N M C{/tex} [Proved above]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle A M N-\angle N M D=\angle B M N-\angle N M C{/tex} [On subtraction]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A M D=\angle B M C{/tex}...(2)
AM = BM [Given]
DM = CM [From (1)]
{tex}\angle A M D=\angle B M C{/tex} [From (2)]
{tex}\angle A M D \cong \triangle B M C{/tex} [By SAS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore A D=B C{/tex} [CPCT]

Q 17

ABCD is a quadrilateral such that diagonal AC bisects the angles A and C. Prove that AB = AD and CB = CD.

Solution

Given: A quadrilateral ABCD such that {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} and {tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}.
To prove: AB = AD and CB = CD
Proof: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} and {tex}\Delta ADC{/tex}, we have

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Given]
AC = AC [Common side]
{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} [Given]
So, by SAS criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\Delta ABC \cong \Delta ADC{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;AB = AD{/tex} [CPCT]
And CB = CD [CPCT]
Hence, proved.

Q 18

ABC is a right angled triangle such that AB = AC and bisector of angle C intersects the side AB at D. Prove that AC + AD = BC.

Solution

Given: ∆ ABC is a right angled such that AB = AC and CD is the angular bisector of {tex}\angle C{/tex} . 

 

To prove: AC + AD = BC
Construction: Draw {tex}D E \perp B C{/tex}
Proof: In right angled triangle ABC, we have
AB = AC. [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC is hypotenuse (since, hypotenuse is the largest side)
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}(angle opposite to hypotenuse is 90°)
In {tex}\Delta D A C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta D E C{/tex} , we have :-
{tex}\angle A = \angle 3{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Each Equal to 90o]
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}. [ Since, CD is angular bisector of {tex}\angle C{/tex} ]
DC = DC. [Common side]
So, by AAS criterion of congruency of triangles, we have :-
{tex}\Delta D A C \cong \Delta D E C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  DA = DE ......…(1) [CPCT]
And,  CA = CE…(2) [CPCT]
In {tex}\Delta B A C{/tex} , we have :-
AB = AC. [Given]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle C = \angle B{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
Now ,  {tex}\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow 90 ^ { \circ } + \angle B + \angle B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}  {tex}[ \because \angle B = \angle C ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle B = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B = \frac { 90 ^ { \circ } } { 2 } = 45 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\angle B E D{/tex} we have :-
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 4 + \angle 5 + \angle B = 180^o{/tex} [Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow 90 ^ { \circ } + \angle 5 + \angle 45 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ Since,  {tex}\angle B{/tex}.  = 45° ]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 5 = 180 ^ { \circ } - 135 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 5 = 45 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \angle B = \angle 5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow D E = B E{/tex} …(3) [ Since,  sides opposite to equal angles of triangle are equal]
From (1) and (3), we get
DA = DE = BE…(4)
Now, BC = CE + BE
{tex}\Rightarrow B C = A C + A D{/tex}. [ From (2), (3) & (4) ]
{tex}\Rightarrow A C + A D = B C{/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 19

AB and CD are the smallest and largest sides of a quadrilateral ABCD. Out of {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}D decide which is greater.

Solution

Given: a quadrilateral ABCD in which AB and CD are the smallest and largest sides of quadrilateral ABCD.

Construction: Join BD.
In {tex}\triangle A B D,{/tex} we have
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB < AD
[{tex}\because{/tex} AB is the smallest side of quadrilateral ABCD]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A B D>\angle A D B{/tex}…(1) [{tex}\because{/tex}Angle opposite to longest side is greater]
Again, in {tex}\triangle C B D{/tex},we have
CD > BC [{tex}\because{/tex}CD is the longest side of quadrilateral ABCD]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C B D>\angle B D C{/tex}…(2) [{tex}\because{/tex} Angle opposite to longest side is greater]
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle A B D+\angle C B D>\angle A D B+\angle B D C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A B C>\angle A D C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B>\angle D{/tex}

Q 20

Prove that in a triangle, other than an equilateral triangle, angle opposite the longest side is greater than {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} of a right angle.

Solution


Let {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC be a triangle in which AC is the longest side.
Since, angle opposite to the longest side is largest.
Hence, {tex}\angle{/tex}B is the largest angle
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\angle{/tex}A ...(i)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\angle{/tex}C ...(ii) 
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
 {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}B+ {tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\angle{/tex}A+ {tex}\angle{/tex}B+ {tex}\angle{/tex}C (adding {tex}\angle B {/tex}  both sides )
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3{tex}\angle{/tex}B > 180°  (Angle sum property of triangle) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B > 60°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B > {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 90°                 
Hence, the largest angle is greater than 2/3rd of right angle. Proved.

Q 21

ABCD is quadrilateral such that AB = AD and CB = CD. Prove that AC is the perpendicular bisector of BD.

Solution

Given: ABCD is a quadrilateral . AB = AD & CB = CD
To prove: AC is the perpendicular bisector of BD.
Proof: 

Let diagonals AC & BD intersect at O.
Let, {tex}\angle BAC = \angle 1{/tex}, {tex}\angle DAC = \angle 2{/tex}, {tex}\angle AOB{/tex} = {tex}\angle 3{/tex} and {tex}\angle AOD{/tex} = {tex}\angle 4{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC & {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC, we have :-
AB = AD [Given]
BC = CD [Given]
AC = AC [Common side]
So, By SSS criterion of congruency of triangles , we have
{tex}\Delta A B C \cong \Delta A D C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [CPCT]
Now, in {tex}\Delta A O B{/tex} and {tex}\Delta A O D{/tex} , we have :-
AB = AD  [Given]
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Proved above]
AO = AO [Common side]
So, By SAS criterion of congruency of triangles , we have :-
{tex}\Delta A O B \cong \Delta A O D{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B O = D O{/tex} [CPCT]
And {tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} [CPCT]
But, {tex}\angle 3 + \angle 4 = 180^o{/tex} [Linear pair axiom]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 3 + \angle 3 = 180 ^ { \circ } [ \because \angle 3 = \angle 4 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle 3 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 3 = \frac { 180 ^ { \circ } } { 2 } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC is perpendicular bisector of BD.  [{tex}\because \angle 3 = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} and BO = DO]
Hence, proved.
