Q 1

If {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC{tex}\cong \triangle{/tex} PQR and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is not congruent to {tex}\triangle{/tex} RPQ, then which of the following is not true:

Q 2

In the two triangles ABC and DEF, AB = DE and AC = EF. Name two angles from the two triangles that must be equal so that the two triangles are congruent. Give reason for your answer

Solution

We have the required two angles are {tex}\angle{/tex}A and {tex}\angle{/tex}E. When {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}E,  {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex} EDF by SAS criterion.

Q 3

In triangles ABC and DEF, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}D, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}E and AB = EF. Will the two triangles be congruent? Give reasons for your answer.

Solution

Two triangles need not be congruent, because AB and EF are not corresponding sides in the two triangles.

Q 4

In Fig, PQ = PR and {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}\angle{/tex}R. Prove that {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS {tex}\cong \triangle{/tex}PRT.

Solution

We have in {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PRT,
PQ = PR (Given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}Q = {tex}\angle{/tex}R (Given)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}QPS = {tex}\angle{/tex}RPT (Same angle)
Therefore, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}PRT (ASA)

Q 5

In Fig., two lines AB and CD intersect each other at the point O such that BC || DA and BC = DA. Show that O is the midpoint of both the line-segments AB and CD.

Solution

BC || AD (Given)
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}CBO = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAO (Alternate interior angles)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}BCO = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADO (Alternate interior angles)
Also, BC = DA (Given)
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex} BOC {tex}\cong\triangle{/tex}AOD (ASA)
Therefore, OB = OA and OC = OD, i.e., O is the mid-point of both AB and CD.

Q 6

In the given figure, PQ>PR, QS and RS are the bisectors of the {tex}\angle {/tex}Q and {tex}\angle {/tex}R respectively. Prove that SQ>SR.

Solution

Since PQ>PR
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\angle R{\text{ > }}\angle {\text{Q}}{/tex} [angle opposite to larger side is larger]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\angle R > \frac{1}{2}\angle Q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \angle {\text{SRQ > }}\angle {\text{SQR}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} SQ>SR [Side opposite to greater angle is larger]

Q 7

In Fig., ABC is a right triangle and right-angled at B such that {tex}\angle {/tex}BCA = 2{tex}\angle {/tex}BAC. Show that hypotenuse AC = 2BC

Solution

Produce CB to a point D such that BC = BD and join AD.
In {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABD, we have
BC = BD (By construction)
AB = AB (Same side)
{tex}\angle {/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle {/tex}ABD (Each of 90°)
Therefore, {tex}\triangle {/tex} ABC {tex}\cong\triangle {/tex}ABD (SAS)
So, {tex}\angle {/tex}CAB = {tex}\angle {/tex}DAB .......(1)
and AC = AD......(2)
Thus, {tex}\angle {/tex}CAD = {tex}\angle {/tex}CAB + {tex}\angle {/tex}BAD = x + x = 2x [From (1)] ....(3)
and {tex}\angle {/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle {/tex}ADB = 2x [From (2), AC = AD] ...(4)
That is, {tex}\triangle {/tex} ACD is an equilateral triangle. [From (3) and (4)]
or AC = CD, i.e., AC = 2 BC (Since BC = BD)

Q 8

Prove that if in two triangles two angles and the included side of one triangle are equal to two angles and the included side of the other triangle, then the two triangles are congruent.

Solution

Given: Two triangles {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF such that {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} E, {tex}\angle{/tex} C = {tex}\angle{/tex} F
and BC = EF.
To prove: ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} DEF
Proof: We prove the result in following cases,
Case I: If AB = DE then in
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF, we have
AB = DE [by supposition]
BC = EF [given]
and {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} E [given]
Thus, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF [SAS criterion]
Case II: If AB < DE
Take a point G on ED such that EG = AB.
Join GF.
In ABC and GEF, we have
AB = GE [by supposition]
BC = EF [given]  
{tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} E [given]
Thus, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}GEF [SAS criterion]  
{tex}\angle{/tex} ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex} GFE [corresponding parts of congruent triangles are equal]
But {tex}\angle{/tex} ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex} DFE [given]  
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex} GFE = {tex}\angle{/tex} DFE,
This is only possible when FG coincides with FD or G coincides with D.  
AB must be equal to DE and hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} DEF (by SAS)
Case III: If AB > ED
With a similar argument (as in case II), we may conclude that
{tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF (by SAS)
Thus, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEF.

Q 9

If the bisector of an angle of a triangle bisects the opposite side, prove that the triangle is isosceles.

Solution


Given: In ∆ ABC, AD is the angular bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A which meets BC in D such that BD = DC........(a)
To prove:  ∆ABC is an isosceles.
Construction: Produce AD to E such that AD = DE and then join CE.
Proof: In ∆ ABD and ∆ ECD, we have
BD = CD [ from (a) ]
AD = ED (By construction)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC (Vertically opposite angles)
Therefore, ∆ ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} ∆ ECD (SAS congruency criterion of triangle)
So, AB = EC (CPCT) ...(i)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CED (CPCT) ...(ii)
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD (Given, BD is angular bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex} ).....(iii)
Therefore, from (ii) and (iii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}CAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CED
So, AC = EC (Sides opposite to equal angles are equal ) ...(iv)
From (i) and (iv), we get
AB = AC.
Therefore, ∆ ABC is isosceles. 
Hence, Proved.

Q 10

S is any point in the interior of {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQR. Show that SQ + SR < PQ + PR.

Solution

Produce QS to meet PR in T.

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQT,
PQ + PT > QT ...[As the sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side]
PQ + PT > QS + ST ...[As QT = QS + ST] ...(1)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}SRT,
TR + ST > SR ...[As the sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side] ...(2)
Adding (1) and (2)
PQ + PT + TR + ST > QS = ST + SR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ + PR + ST > QS + ST + SR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ + PR > QS + SR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SQ + SR < PQ + PR.

sections.label: Exercise 8.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Diagonals AC and BD of a parallelogram ABCD intersect each other at O. If OA = 3 cm and OD = 2 cm, determine the lengths of AC and BD.

Solution

We know that diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = 2 × OA = 2 × 3 cm = 6 cm
And BD = 2OD = 2 × 2 cm = 4 cm
Hence, lengths of AC and BD are 6 cm and 4 cm respectively.

Q 2

Diagonals of a parallelogram are perpendicular to each other. Is this statement true? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

False.
Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other but not at 90°.
So, they are not perpendicular to each other.
Hence, this statement is false.

Q 3

Can the angles 110°, 80°, 70° and 95° be the angles of a quadrilateral? Why or why not?

Solution

Sum of these angles 110° + 80° + 70° + 95° = 355°
But, sum of the angles of a quadrilateral is always 360°. Hence, 110°, 80°, 70° and 95° cannot be the angles of a quadrilateral.

Q 4

In quadrilateral ABCD, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 180º. What special name can be given to this quadrilateral?

Solution

We know that,
Sum of co-interior angles is 180°.
AB || CD
Hence, ABCD is a trapezium.

Q 5

All the angles of a quadrilateral are equal. What special name is given to this quadrilateral?

Solution

All the angle of a quadrilateral are equal. Also, the sum of angles of a quadrilateral is 360°. Therefore, each angle of quadrilateral is 90°.
So, the given quadrilateral is a rectangle.
Hence, special name given quadrilateral is rectangle.

Q 6

Diagonals of a rectangle are equal and perpendicular. Is this statement true? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

Diagonals of a rectangle bisect each other therefore they are equal but they are not perpendicular. Hence, the statement is not true.

Q 7

Can all the four angles of a quadrilateral be obtuse angles? Give reason for your answer.

Solution
No, because then the sum of four angles of the quadrilateral will be more than 360° whereas sum of four angles of a quadrilateral is always equal to 360°.
Q 8

In ∆ABC, AB = 5 cm, BC = 8 cm and CA = 7 cm. If D and E are respectively the mid-points of AB and BC, determine the length of DE.

Solution

In ∆ABC, AB = 5 cm, BC = 8 cm and CA= 7 cm. D and E are respectively the mid-points of AB and BC.
{tex}\therefore \;DE = \frac{1}{2}AC = \frac{1}{2} \times 7cm = 3.5cm{/tex} [Using the mid-point theorem]

Q 9

In Fig. BDEF and FDCE are parallelograms. Can you say that BD = CD?

Solution

As opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal.
Therefore, in parallelograms BDEF and FDCE.
We have BD = FE ....(i)
DC = FE .....(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
BD = DC

Q 10

In Fig, ABCD and AEFG are two parallelograms. If {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 55º, determine {tex}\angle{/tex}F.

Solution

Given: {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 55°


Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal
{tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 55°
Also,
{tex}\because{/tex} AEFG is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore\angle{/tex}F = {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 55°
Hence, {tex}\angle{/tex}F = 55°

Q 11

Can all the angles of a quadrilateral be acute angles? Give reason for your answer.

Solution
We know that an acute angle is less than 90°. All the angles of a quadrilateral cannot be acute angles because than equal sum of a quadrilateral will be than a 360°, whereas angle sum of quadrilateral is 360°.
Q 12

Can all the angles of a quadrilateral be right angles? Give reason for your answer.

Solution
Yes, all the angles of a quadrilateral can be right angles. Angles sum of a quadrilateral will be 360°, which is true.
Q 13

Diagonals of a quadrilateral ABCD bisect each other. If {tex}\angle A = 35^\circ{/tex}, determine {tex}\angle B{/tex}.

Solution

As the diagonals of a quadrilateral ABCD bisect each other, so ABCD is a parallelogram.
Now, ABCD is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore \;\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary]
{tex}\therefore \;35^\circ + \angle B = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle B = 180^\circ - 35^\circ = 145^\circ{/tex}

Q 14

Opposite angles of a quadrilateral ABCD are equal. If AB = 4 cm, determine CD.

Solution

Since opposite angles of a quadrilateral are equal
Now, ABCD is a parallelogram, so AB = CD.[{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal]
But, AB = 4cm, therefore CD = 4cm.
Hence, CD = 4cm.

sections.label: Exercise 8.3
sections.html:
Q 1

One angle of a quadrilateral is of 108° and the remaining three angles are equal. Find each of the three equal angles.

Solution

One angle of a quadrilateral is of 180° and let each of the three remaining equal angles be x°. 
As the sum of the angles of a quadrilateral is 360°.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 108° + x + x + x = 360° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 360° - 108° = 252°
{tex}\therefore \;x = 252^\circ \div 3 = 84^\circ{/tex}
Hence, each of the three angles be 84°.

Q 2

ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || DC and {tex}\angle A = \angle B = 45^\circ{/tex}. Find angles C and D of the trapezium.

Solution

ABCD is a trapezium in which AB||DC.

Now, AB||DC and AD is transversal.
{tex}\therefore \;\angle A + \angle D = 180^\circ{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of interior angles on the side of the transversal is 180°]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;45^\circ + \angle D = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle D = 180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;45^\circ + \angle C = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle C = 180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\angle A = \angle B = 45^\circ{/tex} and {tex}\angle C = \angle D = 135^\circ{/tex}.

Q 3

The angle between two altitudes of a parallelogram through the vertex of an obtuse angle of the parallelogram is 60°. Find the angles of the parallelogram.

Solution

In quadrilateral PBQD,

 {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 + \angle B + \angle 3 = 360^\circ {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 60^\circ + 90^\circ + \angle B + 90^\circ = 360^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle B + 240^\circ = 360^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle B = 360^\circ - 240^\circ{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;\angle B = 120^\circ{/tex}
Now, {tex}\angle ADC = \angle B = 120^\circ{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]
{tex}\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of consecutive interior angles is 180°]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle A + 120^\circ = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle A = 180^\circ - 120^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle A = 60^\circ{/tex}
But, {tex}\angle C = \angle A = 60^\circ{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]

Q 4

ABCD is a rhombus in which altitude from D to side AB bisects AB. Find the angles of the rhombus.

Solution

The required diagram is shown below:

In {tex}\triangle A P D{/tex} and {tex}\triangle B P D{/tex}, we have

AP = BP  ...........[Given]

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}   .........[{tex}\because{/tex} Each equal to 90o]

PD = PD .......... [Common side]

So, by SAS Criterion of congruence, we have

{tex}\Delta A P D \cong \triangle B P D{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \angle A = \angle 3{/tex} ..........[CPCT]

But,{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}  ....... [{tex}\because{/tex} Diagonals bisect opposite angles of a rhombus]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = \angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}…........................(1)

Now, AD || BC

SO, {tex}\angle A + \angle A B C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of consecutive interior angles is 180o]

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A + \angle 3 + \angle 4 = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A + \angle A + \angle A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}     [ by Using (1)]

{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \angle A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = \frac { 180 ^ { \circ } } { 3 } = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Now, {tex}\angle A B C = \angle 3 + \angle 4{/tex}

 {tex}= 60 ^ { \circ } + 60 ^ { o }{/tex}

 {tex}= 120 ^ { \circ }{/tex}     ....... [{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite angles of a rhombus are equal]

{tex}\therefore \angle A D C = \angle A B C = 120 ^ { \circ }{/tex}  .... [{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite angles of a rhombus are equal]

Q 5

E and F are points on diagonal AC of a parallelogram ABCD such that AE = CF. Show that BFDE is a parallelogram.

Solution

Given: A parallelogram ABCD: E and F are points of diagonal AC of parallelogram ABCD such that AE = CF.

To prove: BFDE is parallelogram.
Proof: ABCD is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OD = OB    ...(1) [{tex}\because{/tex} Diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other]
OA = OC   ...(2) [{tex}\because{/tex} Diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other]
AE = CF  …(3) [Given]
By subtracting (3) from (2), we obtain
OA – AE = OC – CF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OE = OF  …(4)
Therefore, BFDE is a parallelogram. [Because OD = OB and OE = OF]

Q 6

E is the mid-point of the side AD of the trapezium ABCD with AB||DC. A line through E is drawn parallel to AB intersect BC at F.  Show that F is the mid-point of BC. [hint: Join AC]

Solution

It is given that in a trapezium ABCD AB||CD and E is the mid-point of the side AD. Also, EF||AB.
we have to prove that F is the mid-point of BC.

Construction: Join AC which intersects EF at O.
Proof:  Here,  E is the mid-point of AD and EF||DC   ...............[ since  EF || AB and DC || AB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB||EF||DC ]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By the Converse of mid-point theorem, we can say that O is the mid-point of AC.
Now, in {tex}\Delta C A B{/tex},  O is the mid-point of AC and OF||AB.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OF bisects BC  [By Converse of mid-point theorem]
Or F is the mid-point of BC.
Hence proved

Q 7

Through A, B and C, lines RQ, PR and QP have been drawn, respectively parallel to sides BC, CA and AB of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC as shown in Fig., Show that {tex}\mathrm{BC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}QR

Solution


Given,
PQ||AB, PR||AC and RQ||BC.
In quadrilateral BCAR,
BR||CA and BC||RA
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BCAR is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BC = AR …(i)
Now, in quadrilateral BCQA,
BC||AQ and AB||QC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BCQA is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BC = AQ …(ii)
Adding Eqn. (i) and (ii), we get
2BC = AR + AQ
2BC = RQ
{tex}\mathrm{BC}=\frac{\mathrm{Q} \mathrm{R}}{2}{/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 8

D, E and F are the mid-points of the sides BC, CA and AB, respectively of an equilateral triangle ABC. Show that {tex}\triangle{/tex} DEF is also an equilateral triangle.

Solution

Given:  ABC is an equilateral triangle. D, E and F are the mid-points of the sides BC, CA and AB, respectively of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC.

 

To prove: {tex}\Delta{/tex} DEF is an equilateral triangle.
Proof: EF joints mid-points of sides of AB and AC respectively.
{tex}\therefore E F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B C{/tex}  ...(1) [Mid-point theorem]
Similarly, D E = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} A B ....(2) [Mid-point theorem]
{tex}D F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } A C{/tex}  ....(3) [Mid-point theorem]
But, AB = BC = CA  …(4)   [Sides of an equilateral {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC]
From (1), (2), (3) and (4), we have
DE = EF = FD
{tex}\therefore \Delta D E F{/tex} is an equilateral triangle.

Q 9

Points P and Q have been taken on opposite sides AB and CD, respectively of a parallelogram ABCD such that AP = CQ. Show that AC and PQ bisect each other.

Solution

Points P and Q have been taken on opposite sides AB and CD respectively of a parallelogram ABCD such that AP = CQ.

(in figure AC  and PQ intersect at O)
{tex}\angle P A O = \angle 1 , \angle Q C O = \angle 2 , \angle A O P = \angle 3 , \angle C O Q = \angle 4{/tex}, mark it in figure)
In {tex}\triangle A O P{/tex} and {tex}\triangle\mathrm { COQ }{/tex} we have
AP = CQ [Given]
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Alt. int. {tex}\angle s{/tex} are equal]
{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} [Vertically opp. {tex}\angle s{/tex} ]
{tex}\therefore \triangle A O P \cong \triangle C O Q{/tex}  [By SAS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OA = OC and OP = OQ [CPCT]
Hence, AC and PQ bisect each other.

Q 10

P is the mid-point of side BC of a parallelogram ABCD such that ∠BAP = ∠DAP. Prove that AD = 2CD.

Solution

 

From the above figure we have
{tex}\angle B A P = \angle D A P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A{/tex}   ...(1)
Since ABCD is a parallelogram, we have
{tex}\angle A + \angle B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}  ....(2)    [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of interior angles on the same sides of transversal is 180o]
In {tex}\Delta A B P{/tex} , we have
{tex}\angle B A P + \angle B + \angle A P B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}  (Angle sum property)
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A + \angle A P B + \angle B = \angle A + \angle B{/tex}   [Using (1) and (2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A P B = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A{/tex}  ....(3)
From (1) and (3), we get
{tex}\angle B A P = \angle A P B{/tex}
BP = AB  ....(4)   [{tex}\because{/tex} side of opposite to equal angles are equal]
Since opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal, we have
{tex}A D = B C \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } A D = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } A D = B P{/tex}    [{tex}\because{/tex} P is the mid-point of BC]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } A D = A B{/tex}    [{tex}\because{/tex} From (4), BP = AB]
Since opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal, we have
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } A D = C D {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow A D = 2 C D{/tex}   (As, AB = CD)

sections.label: Exercise 8.4
sections.html:
Q 1

A square is inscribed in an isosceles right triangle so that the square and the triangle have one angle common. Show that the vertex of the square opposite the vertex of the common angle bisects the hypotenuse.

Solution


Given, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC with {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} and
AB = AC …(i)
Let ADEF is a square,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AD = AF = EF = AD …(ii)
Subtracting eqn (ii) from (i),
AB - AD = AC - AF
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BD = CF
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}CFE and {tex}\triangle{/tex}EDB,
BD = CF
DE = EF
{tex}\angle{/tex}CFE = {tex}\angle{/tex}EDB = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} (Side of square)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CEF {tex}\sim \triangle{/tex}BED (By SAS)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}CE = BE
Hence, vertex E of the square bisect the hypotenuse BC.

Q 2

In a parallelogram ABCD, AB = 10 cm and AD = 6 cm. The bisector of ∠A meets DC in E.  AE and BC produced meet at F. Find the length of CF.

Solution

It is given that ABCD is a parallelogram, in which AB = 10 cm and AD = 6 cm. The bisector of ∠A meets DC in E. AE and BC produced meet at F.

 

From the figure we have 

{tex}\angle B A E = \angle E A D{/tex} ….....................(1) [{tex}\because{/tex} bisect of ∠A]

{tex}\angle E A D = \angle E F B{/tex}…......................(2) [ Alt. {tex}\angle s{/tex}]

Therefore, from (1) and (2), we obtain
{tex} \angle B A E = \angle E F B{/tex}   
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BF = AB     [{tex}\because{/tex} Sides opposite to equal {tex}\angle S{/tex} are equal]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BF = 10 cm   [{tex}\because{/tex} AB = 10 cm]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC + CF = 10 cm 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 cm + CF = 10 cm     [{tex}\because{/tex}  BC = AD = 6 cm, opposite sides of a || gm]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CF = 10 – 6 cm = 4 cm

Q 3

P, Q, R and S are respectively the mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA of a quadrilateral ABCD in which AC = BD. Prove that PQRS is a rhombus.

Solution

Given: A quadrilateral ABCD in which AC = BD and P, Q, R, and S are respectively the mid-point of the sides of AB, BC, CD, and DA of quadrilateral ABCD.


To prove: PQRS is a rhombus
Proof: In {tex}\Delta ABC,{/tex} P and Q are the mid-points of AB and BC respectively, i.e., PQ joins mid-points of sides AB and BC.
{tex}\therefore P Q \| A C{/tex}   ....(1)
And  {tex}P Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } A C{/tex} [ by Mid-point theorem]  …(2)
In triangle ADC, R and S are the mid-points of CD and AD respectively.
{tex}\therefore S R \| A C{/tex}  ....(3)
And  {tex}S R = \frac { 1 } { 2 } A C{/tex}  …(4) [ by Mid-point theorem]
From (1) and (3), we obtain
PQ || SR
From (2) and (4), we obtain
PQ = RS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQRS is a parallelogram.
In triangle ABD, join mid-points of sides of DA and AB respectively i.e; join SP.
{tex}\therefore S P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B D{/tex} …(5) [Mid-point theorem]
Since, AC = BD   ...(6) [Given]
From equations (2), (5) and (6), we obtain
SP = PQ
Since adjacent sides of a parallelogram are equal, so, Parallelogram PQRS is a rhombus.

Q 4

P, Q, R and S are respectively the mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA of a quadrilateral ABCD such that AC {tex}\perp{/tex} BD. Prove that PQRS is a rectangle.

Solution

Given, P, Q, R and S are mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA, respectively.
Also,
AC is perpendicular to BD
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}COD = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}COB = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC, by mid-point theorem,
SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, by mid-point theorem,
PQ || AC and PQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ || SR and SR = PQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
Similarly,
SP || RQ and SP = RQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}BD
Now, in quad EOFR,
OE || FR, OF || ER
{tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}\angle{/tex}ERF = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence, PQRS is a rectangle.

Q 5

P, Q, R and S are respectively the mid-points of sides AB, BC, CD and DA of quadrilateral ABCD in which AC = BD and AC ⊥ BD. Prove that PQRS is a square

Solution


Given, P, Q, R and S are mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA, respectively.
Also, AC = BD and AC is perpendicular to BD.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC, by mid-point theorem,
SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, by mid-point theorem,
PQ || AC and PQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}PQ || SR and PQ = SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD, by mid-point theorem,
SP || BD and SP = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}BD = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD, by mid-point theorem,
RQ || BD and RQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}BD = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}SP = RQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}PQ = SR = SP = RQ
Thus, all four sides are equal.
Now, in quadrilateral EOFR,
OE || FR, OF || ER
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}EOF = {tex}\angle{/tex}ERF = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}(Opposite angles of parallelogram)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}QRS = {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence, PQRS is a square.

Q 6

A diagonal of a parallelogram bisects one of its angles. Show that it is a rhombus.

Solution

ABCD is a parallelogram and diagonal AC bisect {tex}\angle A{/tex}. We have to show that ABCD is a rhombus.

{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} …(1) [{tex}\because{/tex} AC bisect {tex}\angle A{/tex}]
{tex}\angle 2 = \angle 4{/tex} …(2) [Alt. interior angles]
From (1) and (2), we get
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 4{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 4{/tex} [Proved above]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = AB [{tex}\because{/tex} Side. Opp. To equal {tex}\angle s{/tex} are equal]
Also, AB = DC and AD = BC [{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal]
So, ABCD is a parallelogram in which its sides AB = BC = CD = AD.
Hence, ABCD is a rhombus.

Q 7

P and Q are the mid-points of the opposite sides AB and CD of a parallelogram ABCD. AQ intersects DP at S and BQ intersects CP at R. Show that PRQS is a parallelogram.

Solution


Given, P and Q are mid-points of AB and CD.
Now, AB||CD,
{tex}\therefore{/tex}AP || QC
Also, AB = DC
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}DC
AP = QC
Now, AP || QC and AP = QC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}APCQ is a parallelogram.
AQ || PC or SQ || PR
Again,
AB || DC {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}DC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BP = QD
Now, BP||QD and BP = QD
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BPDQ is a parallelogram
So, PD||BQ or PS||QR
Thus, SQ||RP and PS||QR
{tex}\therefore{/tex}PQRS is a parallelogram.

Q 8

ABCD is a quadrilateral in which AB || DC and AD = BC. Prove that ∠A = ∠B .

Solution


Given, ABCD is a quadrilateral in which AB || DC and AD = BC.
Extend AB to E and draw a line CE parallel to AD.
Since AD||CE and transversal AE cuts them at A and E, respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}180^\circ{/tex}- {tex}\angle{/tex}E
Since, AB || CD and AD || CE
So quadrilateral AECD is a parallelogram.
Now, AD = CE {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}BC = CE
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCE,
CE = BC
{tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}CEB (opposite angles of equal sides are equal)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}180^\circ{/tex}- {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}E
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}180^\circ{/tex}- {tex}\angle{/tex}E = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
Hence, proved

Q 9

In Fig., AB || DE, AB = DE, AC || DF and AC = DF. Prove that BC || EF and BC = EF.

Solution

In quad ABED, we have AB || DE and AB = DE
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ABED is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}AD || BE and AD = BE
Now, in quad ACFD, we have
AC || FD and AC = FD
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ACFD is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}AD || CF and AD = CF
{tex}\therefore{/tex}AD = BE = CF and CF || BE
Now, in quad BCFE,
BE = CF and BE || CF.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BCFE is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}BC = EF and BC || EF
Hence proved.

Q 10

In fig AD is a median of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, E is mid-Point of AD.BE produced meet AC at F. Show that AF = {tex}\frac {1} {3}{/tex}AC

Solution

Let M is mid-Point of CF Join DM
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DM {tex}\parallel{/tex} BF

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADM, E is mid- Point of AD and
DM{tex}\parallel{/tex}EF {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F is mid-point of AM
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AF = FM
FM = MC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AF = FM = MC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = AF + FM + MC
= AF + AF + AF 
AF = 3AF
{tex} \Rightarrow AF = \frac{1}{3}AC{/tex}
Hence Proved

Q 11

Show that the quadrilateral formed by joining the mid-points of the consecutive sides of a square is also a square.

Solution


Let a square ABCD in which L, M, N & O are the midpoints.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AML and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CNO
AM = CO (AB = DC and M and O are the midpoints)
AL = CN (AD = BC and L and N are the midpoints)
{tex}\angle{/tex} MAL =  {tex}\angle{/tex}NCO (all angles of a square = 90°)
by AAS criteria
{tex}\triangle{/tex}AML {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CNO
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ML = ON (CPCT)
similarly {tex}\triangle{/tex}MBN {tex}\cong{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}LDO
now,
in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AML,
{tex}\angle{/tex}AML = {tex}\angle{/tex}ALM (AM = AL)
= 45°
similarly in  {tex}\triangle{/tex}LDO
 {tex}\angle{/tex}DLO = 45°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} MLO = 90°
by the properties of SQUARE
All sides are equal and angles are 90°

Q 12

E and F are respectively the mid-points of the non-parallel sides AD and BC of a trapezium ABCD. Prove that EF || AB and EF ={tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(AB + CD). [Hint: Join BE and produce it to meet CD produced at G.]

Solution


Given: A trapezium ABCD in which E and F are respectively the mid-points of the non-parallel sides AD and BC.
To prove: EF||AB and {tex}E F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A B + C D ){/tex}
Construction: Join DF and produce it to intersect AB produced at G.
Proof: In {tex}\Delta C F D{/tex} and {tex}\Delta B F G{/tex} we have
DC||AB
{tex}\therefore \angle C = \angle 3{/tex} [Alternate interior angles]
CF = BF
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Vertically opposite angles]
So, By ASA criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\triangle C F D \cong \triangle B F G{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CD = BG[CPCT]
In {tex}\Delta D A G{/tex}, EF joins mid-points of sides AD and GD respectively
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EF||AG [{tex}\because{/tex} Mid-point theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EF||AB 
So,  {tex}E F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } A G{/tex} [Mid-point theorem]
{tex}E F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A B + B G ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EF ={tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}(AB + CD) [{tex}\because{/tex} CD = BG]
Hence, proved.

Q 13

Prove that the quadrilateral formed by the bisectors of the angles of a parallelogram is a rectangle.

Solution

Given that: A parallelogram ABCD in which bisectors of angles A, B, C, D intersect at P.Q,R,S to form a quadrilateral PQRS.
To prove: PQRS is a rectangle.
Proof: since ABCD is a parallelogram.
Therefore, AB || DC

Now, AB ll DC and transversal AD intersects them at D and A respectively.
Therefore, {tex}\angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{D}=180^{\circ}{/tex}    [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of consecutive interior angles is 180o]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{A}+\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{D}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{DAS}+\angle \mathrm{ADS}=90^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} DS and AS are bisectors of {tex}\angle \mathrm{A}{/tex} and {tex}\angle \mathrm{D}{/tex} respectively]
But, in {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DAS}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle \mathrm{DAS}+\angle \mathrm{ASD}+\angle \mathrm{ADS}=180^{\circ}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of the angles of a triangle is 180o]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 90^{\circ}+\angle A S D=180^{\circ}{/tex} [Using (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle 90^{\circ}+\angle A S D=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{PSR}=90^{\circ}{/tex}    [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle \mathrm{ASD}{/tex} and {tex}\angle \mathrm{PSR}{/tex} are vertically opposite angles 
{tex}\therefore \angle \mathrm{PSR}=\angle \mathrm{ASD}{/tex}
Similarly {tex}\angle \mathrm{SRQ}=90^{\circ}, \angle \mathrm{RQP}=90^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle \mathrm{SPQ}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence PQRS is a rectangle.

Q 14

P and Q are points on opposite sides AD and BC of a parallelogram ABCD such that PQ passes through the point of intersection O of its diagonals AC and BD. Show that PQ is bisected at O.

Solution

ABCD is a parallelogram. Its diagonals AC and BD bisect each other at O. PQ passes through the point of intersection O of its diagonal AC and BD.

In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{AOP}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{COQ}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 3=\angle 4{/tex} [Alternate int. {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
OA = OC [Diagonals of a || gm bisect each other]
{tex}\angle 1=\angle 2{/tex} [Vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm{AOP} \cong \triangle \mathrm{COQ}{/tex} [By ASA Congruence rule]
So, OP = OQ [CPCT]
Hence, PQ is bisected at O.

Q 15

ABCD is a rectangle in which diagonal BD bisects {tex}\angle B{/tex}. Show that ABCD is a square.

Solution

Given: A rectangle ABCD in which diagonal BD bisects {tex}\angle B{/tex}.
 
To prove: ABCD is a square.
Proof: DC||AB [{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite sides of a rectangle are parallel]
{tex}\Rightarrow \;\angle 4 = \angle 1{/tex} ..(1) [Alternate interior angles]
Similarly, {tex}\angle 3 = \angle 2{/tex} …(2) [Alternate interior angles]
And {tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex}…(3) [Given]
From equation (1), (2) and (3), we get
{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex}
In {tex}\Delta BDA{/tex} and {tex}\Delta BDC{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 1 = \angle 2{/tex} [Given]
BD = BD [Common side]
{tex}\angle 3 = \angle 4{/tex} [proved above]
So, By ASA criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\Delta BDA \cong \Delta BDC{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = BC [CPCT]
So, ABCD is a square.
Hence, proved.

Q 16

D, E and F are respectively the mid-points of the sides AB, BC and CA of a triangle ABC. Prove that by joining these mid-points D, E and F, the triangles ABC is divided into four congruent triangles.

Solution

Given: A {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DEF}{/tex} which is formed by joining the mid-points D, E and F of the sides AB, BC and CA of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex}.
To prove: {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong \triangle \mathrm{EDB} \cong \triangle \mathrm{CFE} \cong \triangle \mathrm{FAD}{/tex}
Proof: DF joins mid-points of sides AB and AC respectively of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} DF || BC [Mid-point theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DF || BE
Similarly, EF || BD
So, quadrilateral BEFD is a parallelogram.
{tex}\Rightarrow \triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong \triangle\mathrm{EDB}{/tex} ...(i) [{tex}\because{/tex} A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles]
Similarly., {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong \triangle \mathrm{CFE}{/tex} ...(ii)
and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong \triangle\mathrm{FAD}{/tex} ...(iii)
From equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\triangle \mathrm{DEF} \cong\triangle \mathrm{EDB} \cong \triangle \mathrm{CFE} \cong \triangle \mathrm{FAD}{/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 17

Prove that the line joining the mid-points of the diagonals of a trapezium is parallel to the parallel sides of the trapezium.

Solution


Given that: A trapezium ABCD in which E and F are the mid-points of sides AD and BC respectively.
To prove: EF || AB || DC
Construction : Join DF and produce it to intersect AB produced at G.
Proof: In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DCF}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle\mathrm{GBF}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle 1=\angle 2{/tex} [Alternate interior angles because DC || BG]
{tex}\angle 3=\angle 4{/tex} [Vertically opposite angles]
CF = BF [{tex}\because{/tex} F is the mid-point of BC]
So, by AAS criterion of congruence, we have
{tex}\triangle \mathrm{DCF} \cong \triangle \mathrm{GBF}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DF = GF [CPCT]
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DAG}{/tex}, EF joins mid-points of sides DA and DG respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EF || AG [Mid-point theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EF || AB
But, AB || DC [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EF || AB || DC

Q 18

P is the mid-point of the side CD of a parallelogram ABCD. A line through C parallel to PA intersects AB at Q and DA produced at R. Prove that DA = AR and CQ = QR.

Solution

ABCD is a parallelogram. P is the mid-point of CD. CR which intersects AB at Q is parallel to AP
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{DCR}, \mathrm{P}{/tex} is the mid-point of CD and AP || CR,

{tex}\therefore{/tex} A is the mid-point of DR, i.e., AD = AR.
[{tex}\because{/tex}The line drawn through the mid-point of one side of a triangle parallel to another side intersects the third side at its mid-point.]
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ARQ}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{BCQ}{/tex}, we have
AR = BC [{tex}\because{/tex} AD = AR [proved above) and AD = BC]
{tex}\angle 1=\angle2{/tex} [Vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\angle3=\angle4{/tex} [Alt. {tex}\angle s{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm{ARQ} \cong \triangle \mathrm{BCQ}{/tex} [By AAS Congruence rule]
CQ = QR [CPCT]
CQ = QR
Hence, DA = AR and CQ = QR

sections.html:
Q 1

Diagonals of a Parallelogram ABCD intersect at O. If {tex}\angle BOC = 90^\circ {/tex}, {tex}\angle BDC = 50^\circ {/tex} then {tex}\angle OAB{/tex} is

Q 2

ABCD is a parallelogram. If its diagonals are equal, then find the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC.

Solution

As diagonals of the parallelogram ABCD are equal, hence it is a rectangle. We know that, each angle of the rectangle is 90° .
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 90o 

Q 3

Diagonals of a rhombus are equal and perpendicular to each other. Is this statement true? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

This statement is false, because diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular but not equal to each other.

Q 4

Three angles of a quadrilateral ABCD are equal. Is it a parallelogram? Why or why not?

Solution

It need not be a parallelogram, because if {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 70° and {tex}\angle{/tex}D =150°. But {tex}\angle{/tex}B {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}D.

Q 5

Diagonals AC and BD of a quadrilateral ABCD intersect each other at O such that OA : OC = 3: 2. Is ABCD a parallelogram? Why or why not?

Solution

ABCD is not a parallelogram, because diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other. Here OA ≠ OC.

Q 6

Angles of a quadrilateral are in the ratio 3 : 4 : 4 : 7. Find all the angles of the quadrilateral.

Solution

Let the angles of the quadrilateral be 3x, 4x, 4x and 7x
So, 3x + 4x + 4x + 7x = 360º
or 18x = 360º, i.e., x = 20º
Thus, required angles are 60º, 80º, 80º and 140º. 

Q 7

In Fig. X and Y are respectively the mid-points of the opposite sides AD and BC of a parallelogram ABCD. Also, BX and DY intersect AC at P and Q, respectively. Show that AP = PQ = QC.

Solution

AD = BC (Opposite sides of a parallelogram)
Therefore,{tex}\mathrm{DX}=\mathrm{BY}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{AD}\right.\left.=\frac{1}{2} B C\right){/tex}
Also, DX || BY (As AD || BC)
So, XBYD is a parallelogram (A pair of opposite sides equal and parallel)
i.e., PX || QD
Therefore, AP = PQ (From {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQD where X is mid-point of AD) ...(1)
Similarly, from {tex}\triangle{/tex}CPB, CQ = PQ ...(2)
Thus, AP = PQ = CQ [From (1) and (2)]

Q 8

In Fig. AX and CY are respectively the bisectors of the opposite angles A and C of a parallelogram ABCD. Show that AX || CY

Solution

Given: ABCD is a parallelogram and line segments AX, CY bisect the angles A and C respectively.

To Prove : AX || CY
Proof : ABCD is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C . . .[Opposite {tex}\angle{/tex}s]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C . . .[As halves of equals are equal]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 ...[As AX bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A and CY bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}C] . .(1)
Now, AB || DC and CY intersects them
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 . . . .[Alternate interior {tex}\angle{/tex}s] . . . (2)
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 . . .[From (1) and (2)]
But these are corresponding angles
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AX || CY.

Q 9

PQ and RS are two equal and parallel line-segments. Any point M not lying on PQ or RS is joined to Q and S and lines through P parallel to QM and through R parallel to SM meet at N. Prove that line segments MN and PQ are equal and parallel to each other.

Solution

Given: PQ = RS, PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} RS, PN {tex}\parallel{/tex} QM, RN {tex}\parallel{/tex} MS
To prove: MN = PQ, MN {tex}\parallel{/tex} PQ

Proof: Since PQ = RS and PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} RS, therefore PQSR is a parallelogram.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR = QS, PR {tex}\parallel{/tex} QS
Since PN {tex}\parallel{/tex} QM and MN is the transversal, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3   (Corresponding angles)   ...(i)
Similarly, RN {tex}\parallel{/tex} MS, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4  ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we obtain
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 + {tex}\angle{/tex}4 i.e., {tex}\angle{/tex}PNR - {tex}\angle{/tex}QMS
Again, {tex}\angle{/tex}PRS = {tex}\angle{/tex}QSX   [Corresponding angles as PR {tex}\parallel{/tex} QS]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}6 = {tex}\angle{/tex}5   [Corresponding angles as RN {tex}\parallel{/tex} SM]
Subtracting the above two equations, we get,
{tex}\angle{/tex}PRS - {tex}\angle{/tex}6 = {tex}\angle{/tex}QSX - {tex}\angle{/tex}5
i.e., {tex}\angle{/tex}PRN = {tex}\angle{/tex}QSM
Now, in {tex}\angle{/tex}PNR and {tex}\angle{/tex}QMS,
PR - QS    [Opp. sides of {tex}\parallel{/tex}gm]
{tex}\angle{/tex}PNR = {tex}\angle{/tex}QMS (Proved above)
{tex}\angle{/tex}PRN = {tex}\angle{/tex}QSM
Therefore, By AAS congruence criterion, we have 
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PNR = {tex}\triangle{/tex}QMS 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PN = QM (CPCT)
Also, PN {tex}\parallel{/tex} QM (Given)
Therefore, PNMQ is a parallelogram.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} MN and PQ = MN.

Q 10

Prove that the diagonal divides a parallelogram into two congruent triangles.

Solution


It is given that ABCD is a parallelogram.
We have to prove that  {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDA.
Construction: Join AC.
Proof: Since ABCD is a parallelogram, therefore, AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} DC and AD {tex}\parallel{/tex} BC.
Now, AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} DC and transversal AC cuts them at A and C respectively. 
{tex}\angle{/tex}DCA = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC [Alternate interior angles]
Again AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} BC and AC is the transversal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB   [Alternate interior angles]
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDA, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex} BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA
AC = AC  (Common)
{tex}\angle{/tex}DAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB
So, by ASA congruence criterion, we have
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDA
Hence proved.

Q 11

Show that the quadrilateral formed by joining the mid-points the sides of a rhombus, taken in order, form a rectangle.

Solution

Let ABCD be a rhombus and P, Q, R and S be the mid-points of sides AB, BC, CD and DA, respectively (Fig.). Join AC and BD.


From triangle ABD, we have {tex}\mathrm{SP}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BD}{/tex} and
SP || BD (Because S and P are mid-points)
Similarly {tex}\mathrm{RQ}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BD}{/tex} and RQ || BD
Therefore, SP = RQ and SP || RQ
So, PQRS is a parallelogram ...(1)
Also, AC {tex}\perp{/tex} BD (Diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular)
Further PQ || AC (From {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAC)
As SP || BD, PQ || AC and AC {tex}\perp{/tex} BD,
therefore, we have BD {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ, i.e. ∠SPQ = 90º. ..(2)
Therefore, PQRS is a rectangle. [From (1) and (2)]

Q 12

A diagonal of a parallelogram bisects one of its angle. Prove that it will bisect its opposite angle also.

Solution

Let us draw the figure as per given condition (Fig.). In it, AC is a diagonal which bisects ∠BAD of the parallelogram ABCD, i.e., it is given that ∠BAC = ∠DAC. We need to prove that ∠BCA = ∠DCA.
AB || CD and AC is a transversal.
Therefore, ∠BAC = ∠DCA (Alternate angles) ...(1)
Similarly, ∠DAC = ∠BCA (From AD || BC) ...(2)
But it is given that ∠BAC = ∠DAC ...(3)
Therefore, from (1), (2) and (3), we have
∠BCA = ∠DCA

sections.label: Exercise 9.2
sections.html:
Q 1

ABCD is a parallelogram and X is the mid-point of AB. If ar (AXCD) = 24 cm2, then ar (ABC) = 24 cm2

Solution

We have ABCD is a parallelogram and X is the mid-point of AB
Now, ar (ABCD) = ar (AXCD) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} XBC) ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} Diagonal AC of a parallelogram divides it into two triangles of equal area.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar (ABCD) = 2ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC) ...(ii)
Again X is the mid-point of AB, so
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CXB) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC) ...(iii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Median divides the triangle in two triangles of equal area]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) [Using (i), (ii) and (iii)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{2 \times 24}{3}=16 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Hence, the given statement is false. 

Q 2

PQRS is a rectangle inscribed in a quadrant of a circle of radius 13 cm. A is any point on PQ. If PS = 5 cm, then ar (PAS) = 30 cm2.

Solution

It is given that A is any point on PQ, therefore, PA < PQ
It is given that A is any point on PQ, therefore PA < PQ

Now, ar {tex}(\Delta P Q R)=\frac{1}{2} \times \text { base } \times \text { height }{/tex}
Now, ar {tex}(\triangle \mathrm{PQR})=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{PQ} \times \mathrm{QR}=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 5=30 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} PQRS is a rectangle {tex}\therefore{/tex} RQ = SP = 5 cm]
As PA < PQ (= 12 cm)
So {tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PAS})<\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PQR}){/tex}
or {tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PAS})<30 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR) = 30 cm2]
Hence, the given statement is false.

Q 3

PQRS is a parallelogram, whose area is 180 cm2 and A is any point on the diagonal QS. Then, the area of {tex}\triangle ASR{/tex} = 90 cm2.

Solution

True

Q 4

ABC and BDE are two equilateral triangles such that D is the mid-point of BC. Then ar(BDE) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ar (ABC).

Solution

{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BDE are two equilateral triangles.
Let each side of triangle ABC be x
Again, D is the mid-point of BC, so each side of triangle BDE is {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{\operatorname{arc}(\Delta B D E)}{\operatorname{ar}(\Delta A B C)}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times x^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}}{4 x^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle B D E)=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle A B C){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The given statement is true.

Q 5

In Fig., ABCD and EFGD are two parallelograms and G is the mid-point of CD. Then ar (DPC) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (EFGD).

Solution

As {tex}\triangle{/tex}DPC and || gm ABCD are on the same base DC and between the same parallels AB and DC, so
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (|| gm. ABCD)
= ar (|| gm EFGD) [{tex}\because{/tex} G is the mid point of DC]
Hence, the given statement is false.

sections.label: Exercise 9.3
sections.html:
Q 1

 In a given figure, PSDA is a parallelogram. Points Q and R are taken on PS such that PQ = QR = RS and AP {tex}\parallel{/tex} BQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} CR {tex}\parallel{/tex} DS . Prove that ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}PQE) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}CFD).

Solution

Since, AP {tex}\parallel{/tex} BQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} CR {tex}\parallel{/tex} DS and AD {tex}\parallel{/tex} PS,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ = CD .....(i)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BED, C is the mid-point of BD and CF {tex}\parallel{/tex} BE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F is the mid-point of ED
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} EF = FD
Similarly, EF = PE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PE = FD .....(ii)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}'s PQE and CFD, we have
PE = FD
{tex}\angle{/tex}EPQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}FDC [Alternate angles]
And, PQ = CD.
So, by SAS congruence criterion, we have
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQE {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DCF
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}PQE) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}DCF)

Q 2

In the given Fig., X and y are points on the side LN of the triangle LMN such that LX = XY = YN. Through X, a line is drawn parallel to LM to meet MN at Z. Prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}LZY) = ar (MZYX).

Solution

We have to prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}LZY) = ar (MZYX)
since {tex}\triangle{/tex}LXZ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}XMZ are on the same base and between the same parallels LM and XZ, we have
ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}LXZ) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}XMZ) ...(i)
Adding ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}XYZ) to both sides of (i), we get
ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}LXZ) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}XYZ) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}XMZ) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}XYZ)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}LZY) = ar(MZYX)

Q 3

The area of the parallelogram ABCD is 90 cm2 (see Fig.). Find ar (ABEF)

  1. Find ar (ABEF)
  2. ar (ABD)
  3. ar (BEF)
Solution
  1. Since parallelograms on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area, so we have
    ar (|| gm ABEF) = ar (||gm ABCD)
    Hence, ar (||gm ABEF) = ar (||gm ABCD) = 90 cm2 
  2. ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (|| gm ABCD) [{tex}\because{/tex} A diagonal of a parallelogram divides the parallelogram in two triangles of equal area].
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 90 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex} = 45 cm2
  3. ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BEF) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (|| gm ABEF) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 90 cm2 = 45 cm2
Q 4

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, D is the mid-point of AB and P is any point on BC. If CQ || PD meets AB in Q (Fig.), then prove that ar (BPQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (ABC).

Solution

D is the mid-point of AB and P is any point on BC of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC. CQ || PD meets AB in Q, we have to prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BPQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC)
Join CD. Since median of a triangle divides it into triangles of equal area, so we have
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) ...(i)
since triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area, so we have
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPQ) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPC) ...(ii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Triangle DPQ and DPC are on the same base DP and between the same parallels FP and CQ]
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPQ) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPB) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPC) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPB)
Hence, ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BPQ) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) [using (i)]

Q 5

ABCD is a square. E and F are respectively the midpoints of BC and CD. If R is the mid-point of EF (see fig.) prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} AER) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} AFR).

Solution

It is given that ABCD is a square. E and F are respectively the mid-points of BC and CD respectively. Also, R is the mid-point of EF,
To prove:  {tex}ar ( \triangle A E R ) = ar ( \triangle A F R ){/tex}
Now in {tex}\triangle A B E{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A D F{/tex}, we have
AB = AD [Sides of a square are equal]
{tex}\angle A B E = \angle A D F{/tex} [Each 90o]
BE = DF [{tex}\because{/tex} E is the mid-point of BC and F is the mid-point of CD. Also {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C D{/tex} ]
Therefore, by SAS Congruence rule,
{tex}\triangle A B E \cong \triangle A D F{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE = AF [CPCT] ...(1)
Now, in {tex}\triangle A E R{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A F R{/tex} we have
AE = AF [From (1)]
ER = RF [{tex}\because{/tex} R is mid-point of EF]
And AR = AR [Common side]
{tex}\triangle A E R \cong \triangle A F R{/tex}  [By SSS rule of congruence]
Hence, {tex}\operatorname { ar } ( \triangle A E R ) = \operatorname { ar } ( \triangle A F R ){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Congruent triangles are equal in area]

Q 6

O is any point on the diagonal PR of a parallelogram PQRS. Prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}PSO) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}PQO).

Solution

Given:- In a parallelogram PQRS, O is any point on the diagonal PR
To Prove:-  {tex}\ ar ( \triangle P S O ) = ar ( \triangle P Q O ){/tex}
Construction:- Join SQ, which intersects PR at B.
Proof:-  We know that diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other. So  B is the midpoint of SQ.
Here, PB is a median of {tex}\triangle P Q S{/tex} and we know that a median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal area.
{tex}a r ( \triangle P S B ) = ar ( \triangle P Q B ){/tex} .............(1)   

 

Also, OB is the median of {tex}\triangle O S Q{/tex}
Therefore,  {tex}\operatorname { ar } ( \triangle O S B) = \operatorname { ar } ( \triangle O Q B){/tex}…..........(2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}ar( \triangle P S B ) + ar ( \triangle O S B ) = ar ( \triangle P Q B) + ar ( \triangle O Q B ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ar ( \triangle P S O ) = ar ( \triangle P Q O ){/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 7

ABCD is a parallelogram in which BC is produced to E such that CE = BC (see fig.). AE intersects CD at F. If ar (DFB) = 3 cm2, find the area of parallelogram ABCD.

Solution

From the given figure, In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADF and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ECF, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}DAF = {tex}\angle{/tex}CEF (Alternate interior angles) 
AD = CE [({tex}\because{/tex}AD = BC = CE) Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADF = {tex}\angle{/tex}FCE [Alt. interior {tex}\angle{/tex}s]
{tex}\therefore \quad \triangle A D F \cong \Delta E C F{/tex} [By SAS rule of congruence]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DF = CF [CPCT]
As BF is median of {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BDF) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD) ...(i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Median divides a triangle into two triangles of equal area]
Now, if a triangle and a parallelogram are on the same base and between the same parallels, then the area of the triangles is equal to half the area of the parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (|| gm ABCD) ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By (i), we have ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BDF) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (|| gm ABCD)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 cm2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ar (|| gm ABCD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar (|| gm ABCD) = 12 cm2
Hence, the area of the parallelogram ABCD is 12 cm2

Q 8

In trapezium ABCD, AB || DC and L is the mid-point of BC. Through L, a line PQ || AD has been drawn which meets AB in P and DC produced in Q (see fig.). Prove that ar (ABCD) = ar (APQD)

Solution

As AB || DC, so AB || DQ
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}CLQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BLP, we have
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QCL = {tex}\angle{/tex}LBP [Alt. {tex}\angle{/tex}s]
CL = LB [{tex}\because{/tex} L is the mid-point of BC]
{tex}\angle{/tex}CLQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}BLP [Vertically opposite {tex}\angle{/tex}s]
{tex}\therefore \triangle C L Q \cong \triangle B L P{/tex} [By ASA Congruence rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CLQ) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BLP) ...(i) [Congruent {tex}\triangle s{/tex} are equal in area]
Adding ar (APLCD) to both sides of (i), we get
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CLA) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APLCD) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BLP) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APLCD)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar (APQD) = ar (ABCD)
Hence, ar (ABCD) = ar (APQD)  .

Q 9

If the mid-points of the sides of a quadrilateral are joined in order, prove that the area of the parallelogram so formed will be half of the area of the given quadrilateral.
[Hint: Join BD and draw perpendicular from A on BD.]

Solution


Given: A quadrilateral ABCD in which the mid-points of the sides of it are joined in order to form parallelogram PQRS.
To prove: ar (|| gm PQRS) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
Construction: Join BD and draw perpendicular from A on BD which interect SR and BD at X and Y respectively.
Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD, S and R are the mid-points of sides AB and AD respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SR || BD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SX || BY
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X is the mid-point of AY [Converse of mid-point theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AX = XY ...(i) [{tex}\because{/tex} S is the mid-point of AB and SX || BY]
And, {tex}\mathrm{SR}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BD}{/tex} ...(ii) [{tex}\because{/tex} Mid-point theorem]
Now, ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} BD {tex}\times{/tex} AY
and ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ASR) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} SR {tex}\times{/tex} AX
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ASR})=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BD}\right) \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{AY}\right){/tex} [Using (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{ASR})=\frac{1}{4} \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{BD} \times \mathrm{AY}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{ASR})=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABD}){/tex} ...(iii)
Similarly,
{tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{CPQ})=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{CBD}){/tex} ...(iv)
{tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{BPS})=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{BAC}){/tex} ...(v)
{tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{DRQ})=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{DAC}){/tex} ...(vi)
Adding (iii),(iv),(v) and (vi), we get
{tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{ASR})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{CPQ})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{BPS})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{DRQ}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABD})+\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle\mathrm{CBD})+\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{BAC}){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{D} \mathrm{AC}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}[\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABD})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{CBD}){/tex} {tex}+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{BAC})+\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{DAC})]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}[\operatorname{ar}(\square A B C D)+\operatorname{ar}(\square A B C D)]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times 2 \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{ASR})+\operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{CPQ})+\operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{BPS})+\operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{DRQ}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD})-\operatorname{ar}(\| \mathrm{gm} \mathrm{PQRS})=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\| \mathrm{gm} \mathrm{PQRS})=\operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD})-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{ar}(\| \mathrm{gm} \mathrm{PQRS})=\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{ar}(\square \mathrm{ABCD}){/tex}
Hence, proved.

sections.label: Exercise 9.4
sections.html:
Q 1

A point E is taken on the side BC of a parallelogram ABCD. AE and DC are produced to meet at F. Prove that ar (ADF) = ar (ABFC)

Solution

Given:- ABCD is a parallelogram. A point E is taken on the side BC. AE and DC are produced to meet at F.
Proof: Since ABCD is a parallelogram and diagonal AC divides it into two triangles of equal area, we have

{tex}ar ( \Delta A D C ) = ar ( \Delta A B C ){/tex} ...(1)
As DC || AB, So CF || AB
Since triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area, so we have
{tex}ar ( \Delta A C F ) =ar ( \Delta B C F ){/tex} ....(2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}ar ( \Delta A D C ) + ar ( \Delta A C F ) = ar ( \Delta A B C ) + ar ( \Delta B C F ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ar ( \Delta A D F ) = ar ( A B F C ){/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 2

The diagonals of a parallelogram ABCD intersect at a point O. Through O, a line is drawn to intersect AD at P and BC at Q. Show that PQ divides the parallelogram into two parts of equal area.
 

Solution

Given: In a parallelogram ABCD, diagonals interact at O and draw a line PQ, which intersects AD and BC.
To Prove: PQ divides the parallelogram ABCD into two parts of equal area.
i.e., ar(ABQP) = ar(CDPQ)

Proof: We know that, diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OA = OC and OB = OD ...(i)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}COD,
OA = OC
OB = OD [from eq.(i)]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD [vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}COD [by SAS congruence rule]
Then, ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}COD)...(ii) [Since, congruent figures have equal area]
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOP and {tex}\triangle{/tex}COQ,
{tex}\angle{/tex}PAO = {tex}\angle{/tex}OCQ [alternate interior angles]
OA = OC [from eq.(i)]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}AOP = {tex}\angle{/tex}COQ [vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOP {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}COQ [by ASA congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}AOP) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}COQ)...(iii) [since, congruence figures have equal area]
Similar, ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}POD) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOQ)...(iv)
Now, ar(ABQP) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}COQ) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}COD) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}POD) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}AOP) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB) + ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOQ) [From eqs.(ii),(iii) and (iv)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar(ABQP) = ar(CDPQ) 
Hence proved. 

Q 3

The medians BE and CF of a triangle ABC intersect at G. Prove that the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}GBC = area of the quadrilateral AFGE.

Solution

BE and CF are medians of a triangle ABC intersect at G. We have to prove that the ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}GBC) = area of the quadrilateral AFGF.
Since, median (CF) divides a triangle into two triangles of equal area, so we have
 {tex}ar(\Delta BCF) = ar(\Delta ACF){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;ar(\Delta GBF) + ar(\Delta GBC){/tex}{tex}= ar(AFGE) + ar(\Delta GCE){/tex} ...(1)
 
Since, median (BE) divides a triangle into two triangle of equal area, so we have
{tex}\Rightarrow \;ar(\Delta GBF) + ar(AFGE){/tex}{tex} = ar(\Delta GCE) + ar(\Delta GBE){/tex}  …(2)
Subtracting (2) and (1), we get
{tex}ar(\Delta GBC) - ar(AFGE){/tex}{tex} = ar(AFGE) - ar(\Delta GBC){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;ar(\Delta GBC) + ar(\Delta GBC){/tex}{tex} = ar(AFGE) + ar(AFGE){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2ar(\Delta GBC) = 2ar(AFGE){/tex}
Hence, {tex}ar(\Delta GBC) = ar(AFGE){/tex}

Q 4

In Fig. CD || AE and CY || BA. Prove that ar (CBX) = ar (AXY)

Solution

Given :CD || AE and CY || BA.
We have to prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CBX) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AXY)
since triangle on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area, so we have
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABY)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CBX) + ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABX) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABX) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AXY)
Hence, ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CBX) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AXY)
[Cancelling ar ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABX) from both sides]

Q 5

ABCD is a trapezium in which AB||DC, DC = 30 cm and AB = 50 cm. If X and Y are, respectively the mid-points of AD and BC, prove that
ar(DCYX) = {tex}\frac79{/tex} ar(XYBA)

Solution

Given: ABCD is a trapezium with AB || DC.
Construction: Join DY and produce it to meet AB produced at M.

Now, In {tex}\Delta{/tex}MBY and {tex}\Delta{/tex}DCY, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 [Vertically opposite angles]
{tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 [{tex}\because{/tex} AB || DC and alternate angles ​​​​are equal]
BY = CY  [{tex}\because{/tex} Y is the mid-point of BC]
Therefore, by ASA congruence rule, we have
{tex}\Delta{/tex}MBY {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}DCY
So, MB = DC = 30 cm [by CPCT]
Now, AM = AB + BM = 50 + 30 = 80 cm
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ADM, we have X and Y are midpoints of sides AD and DM respectively, so by midpoint theorem, XY || AM and XY is half of AM, i.e.,
XY = {tex}\frac12{/tex}AM = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 80 = 40 cm
As AB || XY || DC and X and Y are the mid-points of AD and BC, so the height of trapezium DCYX and XYBA are equal. Let the equal height be h cm.
{tex}\frac { \operatorname { ar } ( D C X Y ) } { \operatorname { ar } ( X Y B A ) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 30 + 40 ) \times h } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 40 + 50 ) \times h } = \frac { 70 } { 90 } = \frac { 7 } { 9 }{/tex}
Hence, ar(DCYX) = {tex}\frac79{/tex} ar(XYBA)
Hence proved. 

Q 6

In {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}, if L and M are the points on AB and AC, respectively such that LM || BC. Prove that ar (LOB) = ar (MOC).
 

Solution


Since triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area, So we have
{tex}\therefore \;ar(\Delta LBM) = ar(\Delta LCM){/tex}
[{tex}\triangle LBM{/tex} and {tex}\triangle LCM{/tex} are on the same base LM and between the same parallels LM and BC]
{tex}\therefore \;ar(\triangle LBM) = ar(\triangle LCM){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;ar(\triangle LOM) + ar(\triangle LOB){/tex}{tex} = ar(\triangle LOM) + ar(\triangle MOC){/tex}
Hence, {tex}ar(\triangle LOB) = ar(\triangle MOC){/tex}
[Cancelling {tex}(\triangle LOM){/tex} from both sides]

Q 7

In given figure, ABCDE is any pentagon. BP drawn parallel to AC meets DC produced at P and EQ drawn parallel to AD meets CD produced at Q. Prove that ar(ABCDE) = ar(APQ).

Solution

From the given figure it is clear that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}APC are on the same base AC and between the same parallels BP and AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APC) .........................(i)  (Triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area)
Similarly, EQ{tex}\parallel{/tex} AD
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AED) = ar (AQD) ............................(ii)
Add the equations (i) and (ii), and then adding ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD) to both the sides, we find that 
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AED) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APC) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}AQD) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD)

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABCDE) = ar(APQ).

Q 8

If the medians of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC intersect at G, show that ar (AGB) = ar (AGC) = ar (BGC) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 } \operatorname { ar } ( A B C ){/tex}

Solution

Given: It is given that the medians AE, BF and CD of {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC intersect at G.

To prove: {tex}ar ( \Delta A G B ) = ar ( \Delta A G C ) = ar ( \Delta B G C ){/tex}{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \operatorname { ar } ( \Delta A B C ){/tex}
Construction: Draw {tex}B P \perp E G{/tex} as  shown in the figure.
Proof: {tex}A G = \frac { 2 } { 3 } A E{/tex}    (because Centroid divides the median in the ratio 2:1)
Now, {tex}\operatorname { ar } ( \Delta A G B ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times A G \times B P{/tex}
 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times A E \times B P = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times A E \times B P = \frac { 2 } { 3 } a r ( \Delta A B E ){/tex}
Since Median divides a triangle into two triangles equal in area, we have
{tex}\operatorname { ar } ( \Delta A G B ) = {/tex}{tex} \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \operatorname { arc } ( \Delta A B C ){/tex}{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \operatorname { arc } ( \Delta A B C ){/tex}
Similarly, we have ar ({tex}\Delta{/tex}AGC) = ar ({tex}\Delta{/tex}BGC) ={tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 } \operatorname { ar } ( \Delta A B C ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore ar ( \Delta A G B ) = ar ( \Delta A G C ){/tex}{tex}= { ar } ( \Delta B G C ) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \operatorname { ar } ( \Delta A B C ){/tex}
Hence proved.

Q 9

In Fig., X and Y are the mid-points of AC and AB respectively, QP || BC and CYQ and BXP are straight lines. Prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABP) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ACQ).

Solution

From the given figure it is clear that X and Y are the mid-points of AC and AB respectively.
 Therefore,  XY {tex}\parallel{/tex} BC
Since {tex}\triangle{/tex}BYC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BXC are on the same base BC and between the same parallels XY and BC, we can write
   ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BYC) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BXC)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BYC) - ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BXC) - ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOY) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}COX)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BOY) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}XOY) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}COX) + ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}XOY)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BXY) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CXY) ...............(i)
Since quadrilaterals XYAP and XYQA are on the same base XY and between the same parallels XY and PQ, we have
  ar(XYAP) = ar (XYQA) .....................(ii)
On adding (i) and (ii), we obtain
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}BXY) + ar (XYAP) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}CXY) + ar (XYQA)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}ABP) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} ACQ)
Hence proved.

Q 10

In Fig., ABCD and AEFD are two parallelograms. Prove that ar (PEA) = ar (QFD).
[Hint: Join PD]

Solution

ABCD and AEFD are two parallelograms.
We have to prove that ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}PEA) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}QFD). Join PD
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PEA and {tex}\triangle{/tex}QDQF [{tex}\because{/tex} Corresp. {tex}\angle s{/tex} are equal as AB || CD]
{tex}\angle{/tex}APE = {tex}\angle{/tex}DQF [{tex}\because{/tex} Corresp. {tex}\angle s{/tex} are equal as AB || CD]
{tex}\angle{/tex}AEP = {tex}\angle{/tex}DFQ [{tex}\because{/tex} Corresp. {tex}\angle s{/tex} are equal as AE || DF]
AE = DF [{tex}\because{/tex} Opposite sides of a ||gm are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle \mathrm{PEA} \cong \Delta \mathrm{QFD}{/tex} [By AAS Cong. rule]
Hence, {tex}\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{PEA})=\operatorname{ar}(\triangle \mathrm{QFD}){/tex}

sections.html:
Q 1

The area of the figure formed by joining the mid-points of the adjacent sides of a rhombus with diagonals 16 cm and 12 cm is

Q 2

If P is any point on the median AD of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, then ar (ABP) {tex}\neq{/tex} ar (ACP).

Solution

False, because ar (ABD) = ar (ACD) and ar (PBD) = ar (PCD), therefore, ar (ABP) = ar (ACP).

Q 3

In the given figure, PQRS and EFRS are two parallelograms, then

Solution

True because ar (PQRS) = ar (EFRS) = 2.ar.(MFR).

Q 4

In the figure, PQRS is a square and T and U are, respectively, the mid-points of PS and QR. Find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}OTS if PQ = 8 cm. where O is the point of intersection of TU and QS.

Solution

Since T and U are the mid-points of PS and QR respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} TU || PQ
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} TO || PQ
Thus, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQS, T is the mid-point of PS and TO II PQ.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} TO = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} PQ = 4 cm
Also, TS = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}PS = 4 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}OTS) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (TO {tex}\times{/tex} TS) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4) cm2 = 8 cm2

Q 5

In figure, ABCD is a parallelogram and BC is produced to a point Q such that AD = CQ. If AQ intersect DC at P, show that ar(BPC) = ar(DPQ).

Solution

Given: ABCD is a parallelogram and BC is produced to a point Q such that AD = CQ.
AQ intersects DC at P.
To Prove : ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}BPC) = ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}DPQ)
Construction : Join AC.

Proof : {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QAC and {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QDC are on the same base QC and between the same parallels AD and QC.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QAC) = ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QDC) . . . [Two triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal] . . .(1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QAC) – ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QPC) = ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}QDC) – ar({tex}\triangle{/tex}QPC) . . .[Subtracting the same areas from both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PAC) = ar(QDP) . . . (2)
As {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PAC and {tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PBC are on the same base PC and between the same parallels AB and DC.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PAC) = ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PBC) . . .[Two triangles on the same base and between the same parallels are equal] . . .(3)
ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}PBC) = ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}QDP) . . . [From (2) and (3)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}BPC) = ar({tex}\bigtriangleup{/tex}DPQ).

Q 6

In figure, ABCD is a parallelogram. Points P and Q on BC trisects BC. Prove that ar(APQ) = ar(DPQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}ar(ABCD).


 

Solution

Through P and Q, draw PR and QS parallel to AB. Now, PQSR is a parallelogram and its base PQ ={tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}BC.

Since {tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DPQ lie on the same base PQ and between the same parallels AD and BC.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPQ) ... (i)
Since {tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ and parallelogram PQSR are on the same base PQ and between same parallels PQ and AD.
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar({tex}\parallel{/tex}gm PQRS) ...(ii)
Now,{tex}\frac{\operatorname({ar} \|^{g m} A B C D)}{a r( \|^{g m} P Q R S)}=\frac{B C \times \text { height }}{P Q \times \text { height }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3PQ}{PQ}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex}Height of the two {tex}\parallel{/tex}gm is same
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ar({tex}\parallel{/tex}gm  PQRS) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}ar({tex}\parallel{/tex}gm ABCD) ...(iii)
Using equation (ii) and (iii), we get
ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}ar( {tex}\parallel{/tex}gm PQRS) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}ar( {tex}\parallel{/tex}gm ABCD)
Hence, ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}APQ) = ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex}DPQ) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}ar({tex}\parallel{/tex}gm ABCD). [by Using (i)]

Q 7

In Fig., l, m, n, are straight lines such that l || m and n intersects l at P and m at Q. ABCD is a quadrilateral such that its vertex A is on l. The vertices C and D are on m and AD || n. Show that ar (ABCQ) = ar (ABCDP)

Solution

ar (APD) = ar (AQD) ...(i)
[Have same base AD and also between same parallels AD and n].
Adding ar (ABCD) on both sides in (i), we get
ar (APD) + ar (ABCD) = ar (AQD) + ar (ABCD)
or ar (ABCDP) = ar (ABCQ).

Q 8

In Fig., BD || CA, E is mid-point of CA and BD = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CA. Prove that are (ABC) = 2ar (DBC)

Solution

Let us Join DE, so that BCED becomes a parallelogram.
since BD = CE and BD || CE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ar (DBC) = ar (EBC) ...(i)
[Have the same base BC and between the same parallels]
Now,In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, BE is the median,
So, ar (EBC) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ar (ABC)
Now, ar (ABC) = ar (EBC) + ar (ABE)
Also, ar (ABC) = 2 ar (EBC), therefore,
ar (ABC) = 2 ar (DBC)
