sections.label: Exercise 10.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Two chords AB and CD of a circle are each at distances 4 cm from the centre. Then AB = CD.

Solution

We know that chords equidistant from the centre of a circle are equal. Here we are given that two chords AB and CD of a circle are each at distance 4 cm (equidistant) from the centre of a circle. So, chords are equal, i.e., AB = CD.
Hence, the given statement is true.

Q 2

If two chords AB and AC of a circle with centre O are on the opposite sides of OA, then, {tex}\angle OAB = \angle OAC{/tex}.

Solution

False
This is only possible if the chords are of equal length. Since, no such condition is mentioned, so, this is not true.

Q 3

Two congruent circles with centres O and O′ intersect at two points A and B. Then {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}AO′B

Solution

The given statement is true because equal chords of congruent circles subtend equal angles at the respective centres.

Q 4

Through three collinear points a circle can be drawn.

Solution

False, Because a circle through two points cannot pass through a point which is collinear to these two points.

Q 5

A circle of radius 3 cm can be drawn through two points A, B such that AB = 6cm.

Solution

True, Because AB will be the diameter

Q 6

If AOB is a diameter of a circle and C is a point on the circle, then AC2 + BC2 = AB2

Solution

AOB is a diameter of a circle and C is a point on the circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle \mathrm{ACB}=90^{\circ}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Angle in a semicircle is a right angle]
In right {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC,
AC2 + BC2 = AB2 [By Pythagoras theorem]
Hence, the given statement is true.

Q 7

ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral such that {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90°, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 70°, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 95° and {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 105°.

Solution

We know that opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.
Here, sum of opposite angles is not. {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{C}=90^{\circ}+95^{\circ}=185^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence, ABCD is not a cyclic quadrilateral. The given statement is false.

Q 8

If A, B, C, D are four points such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 30° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC = 60°, then D is the centre of the circle through A, B and C.

Solution

The given statement is false because there can be many points D such that {tex}\angle \mathrm{BDC}=60^{\circ}{/tex} and each such point cannot be centre of the circle through A, B, C.

Q 9

If A, B, C and D are four points such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 45° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC = 45°, then A, B, C, D are concyclic.

Solution

The given statement is true, because the two angles {tex}\angle \mathrm{BAC}=45^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle \mathrm{BDC}=45^{\circ}{/tex} are in the same segment of a circle.
Hence, A, B, C and D are concyclic.

Q 10

In Fig., if AOB is a diameter and ∠ADC = 120°, then ∠CAB = 30°.

Solution

AOB is a diameter of circle with centre O.
{tex}\angle \mathrm{ADC}+\angle \mathrm{ABC}=180^{\circ}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a cyclic quardrilateral]
{tex}\Rightarrow 120^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{ABC}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{ABC}=180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\angle \mathrm{ACB}={/tex} 300 [{tex}\because{/tex} Angle in a semicircle and {tex}\angle \mathrm{ABC}=60^{\circ}{/tex} (Proved above)]
{tex}\therefore \angle \mathrm{CAB}=180^{\circ}-\left(90^{\circ}+60^{\circ}\right)=30^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence, the given statement is true.

sections.label: Exercise 10.3
sections.html:
Q 1

If arcs AXB and CYD of a circle are congruent, find the ratio of AB and CD.

Solution

We have {tex}\widehat{A X B} \equiv \widehat{C Y D}{/tex} since if two arcs of a circle are congruent,
then their corresponding areas are equal, so we have chord AB = chord CD
Hence, A B: CD = 1 : 1.

Q 2

If the perpendicular bisector of a chord AB of a circle PXAQBY intersects the circle at P and Q, prove that arc PXA {tex} \cong {/tex} arc PYB.

Solution

Give: In the figure PQ is perpendicular bisector of chord AB

To prove : arc PXA= arc PYB



Construction:Join AP and BP.
Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APM and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BPM
AM = MB ( given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}PMA = {tex}\angle{/tex}PMB (90° each)
PM = PM (Common)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}APM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BPM (SAS)
PA = PB (CPCT)
Hence, arc PXA {tex}\cong{/tex} arc PYB.( As the arc of equal chords are equal)

Q 3

A, B and C are three points on a circle. Prove that the perpendicular bisectors of AB, BC and CA are concurrent.

Solution

Given: Three non-collinear points A, B and C are on a circle.
To prove: Perpendicular bisectrors of AB, BC and CA are concurrent.
Construction: Join AB, BC and CA.
Draw perpendicular bisectors ST of AB, PM of BC and QR of CA are respectively. As point A, B, C are not collinear, so ST, PM and QR are not parallel and will intersect.
Proof: {tex}\because{/tex} O lies on ST, the {tex}\perp{/tex} bisector of AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OA = OB ...(i)
And, O lies on QR, the {tex}\perp{/tex} bisector of CA ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OC = OA ...(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii), OA = OB = OC = r(say)
With O as a centre and r as the radius, draw circle C(O, r) which will pass through A, B and C. This proves that there is a circle passing through the points A, B and C. since ST, PM and QR can cut each other at one and only one point O.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} O is the only point equidistant from A, B and C. Hence, the perpendicular bisectors of AB, BC and CA are concurrent.

Q 4

AB and AC are two equal chords of a circle. Prove that the bisector of the angle BAC passes through the centre of the circle.

Solution

Given: AB =AC and AM is the bisector of {tex}\angle B A C{/tex} .

To prove: AM passes through O.

Construction: Join BC. Let AM intersect BC at P.

Proof: In {tex}\Delta B A P{/tex} and {tex}\Delta C A P{/tex}

AB = AC   [Given]

{tex}\angle B A P = \angle C A P{/tex} [Given]

And AP = BP [Common side]

{tex}\therefore \Delta B A P \cong \Delta C A P{/tex} [By SAS congruency]

{tex}\therefore \angle B P A = \angle C P A{/tex} [By C.P.C.T.]

And CP = PB

But {tex}\angle B P A + \angle C P A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Linear pair {tex}\angle s{/tex} ]

{tex}\therefore \angle B P A = \angle C P A = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  AP is perpendicular bisector of the chord BC, which will pass through the centre O on being produced.

Hence, AM passes through O.

Q 5

If a line segment joining mid-points of two chords of a circle passes through the centre of the circle, prove that the two chords are parallel.

Solution

Given: AB and CD are two chords of a circle whose centre of O. The mid-points of AB and CD are L and M respectively.

To prove: AB||CD
Proof: {tex}\because{/tex} L is the mid-point of chord AB
{tex}\therefore OL \bot AB{/tex}, or {tex}\angle ALO = 90^\circ{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} The line joining the centre of a circle to the mid-point of a chord is perpendicular to the chord]
Similarly, {tex}\angle CMO = 90^\circ{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore \;\angle ALO = \angle CMO{/tex}
But, these are corresponding angles.
So, AB||CD.
Hence, proved

Q 6

ABCD is such a quadrilateral that A is the centre of the circle passing through B, C and D. Prove that {tex}\angle C B D + \angle C D B = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle B A D{/tex}

Solution

To prove: {tex}\angle C B D + \angle C D B = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle B A D{/tex}

Construction: Join AC.

Since angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double of the angle subtended by it at point on the remaining part of the circle.

Therefore,  {tex}\angle C A D = 2 \angle C B D{/tex} …(1) 

And  {tex}\angle B A C = 2 \angle C D B{/tex} …(2)

Adding (1) and (2), we get

{tex}\angle C A D + \angle B A C = 2 ( \angle C B D + \angle C D B ){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B A D = 2 ( \angle C B D + \angle C D B ){/tex}

Hence,  {tex}\angle C B D + \angle C B D = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle B A D{/tex}

Q 7

O is the circumcentre of the triangle ABC and D is the mid-point of the base BC. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}A

Solution

Given: O is the circumcentre of {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}O D \perp B C{/tex}
To prove: {tex}\angle B O D = \angle A{/tex}

Construction: Join OC.
Proof: |given
{tex}\therefore \angle O D B = \angle O D C{/tex} |Each = 90o
Now, in right angle triangles ODB and ODC,
OB = OC |Radii of the same circle
OD = OD |common side
{tex}\therefore \triangle O D B \cong \triangle O D C{/tex} |R.H.S. criterion
{tex}\therefore \angle B O D = \angle C O D{/tex} |c.p.c.t
Now, BC subtends {tex}\angle B O C{/tex}  at the centre of the circle and {tex}\angle B A C{/tex} at the point A in the alternate segment.
{tex}\angle B O C = 2 \angle B A C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O D + \angle C O D = 2 \angle B A C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O D + \angle B O D = 2 \angle B A C{/tex}{tex}| \because \angle C O D = \angle B O D{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle B O D = 2 \angle B A C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O D = \angle B A C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O D = \angle A{/tex}. Proved

Q 8

On a common hypotenuse AB, two right triangles ACB and ADB are situated on opposite sides. Prove that ∠BAC = ∠BDC.

Solution

The figure is shown below:

In right triangles ACB and ADB, we have

{tex}\angle A C B = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} and {tex}\angle A D B = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \angle A C B + \angle A D B = 90 ^ { \circ } + 90 ^ { \circ } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Since, we know that if the sum of any pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is 180o then, the quadrilateral is cyclic. So, ADBC is a cyclic quadrilateral.

Now on Joining CD, we can say that the angles {tex}\angle B A C{/tex} and {tex}\angle B D C{/tex} are made by {tex}\widehat { B C }{/tex} in the same segment.

Therefore, {tex}\angle B A C = \angle B D C{/tex} [{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal]

Hence proved

Q 9

Two chords AB and AC of a circle subtends angles equal to 90° and 150°, respectively at the centre. Find {tex}\angle BAC{/tex}, if AB and AC lie on the opposite sides of the centre.

Solution

We have
Reflex {tex}\angle BOC = 90^\circ + 150^\circ = 240^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;\angle BOC = 360^\circ - 240^\circ = 120^\circ{/tex}
Now, {tex}\angle BOC = 2\angle BAC{/tex}

Hence, {tex}\angle BAC = \frac{1}{2}\angle BOC = \frac{1}{2} \times 120^\circ = 60^\circ{/tex}

Q 10

If BM and CN are the perpendiculars, drawn on the sides AB and AC of the {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, then prove that the points B, C, M, and N are cyclic.

Solution

Consider BC as a diameter of the circle.
Angles subtended by the diameter in a semicircle is 90°.
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}BNC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BMC = 90° 
So the points M and N should be on the same circle.
Hence, BCMN is a cyclic quadrilateral. Therefore, the points B, C, M, and N are cyclic.

Hence proved.

Q 11

If a line is drawn parallel to the base of an isosceles triangle to intersect its equal sides, prove that the quadrilateral so formed is cyclic.

Solution

{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC, DE is drawn parallel to BC. We have to prove that quadrilateral BCED is a cyclic quadrilateral i.e., point B, C, E and D lie on a circle.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC, we have
AB = AC         [Given]
{tex}\therefore \angle 1=\angle 2{/tex}     [{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opp, to equal sides are equal]
Now, DE||BC and AB cuts them,
 {tex}\therefore\angle 1+\angle 3 = 180^\circ{/tex}    [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of int. {tex}\angle s{/tex} on the same side of the transversal]
{tex}\angle 2+\angle 3=180^{\circ} {/tex}      {tex}[\because \angle 1=\angle 2]{/tex}
Similarly, we can show that {tex}\angle 1+\angle 4=180^{\circ}{/tex}
Since if a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral are supplementary, then the quadrilateral is cyclic.
Hence, BCED is a cyclic quadrilateral.

Q 12

If a Pair of opposite sides of a cyclic quadrilateral are equal, then the diagonals are also equal.

Solution

Given: A cyclic quadrilateral ABCD in which AB = DC
To Prove: diagonal AC = diagonal BD
Proof:
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 (Angle in same segment of circle)
{tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 + {tex}\angle{/tex}4
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD
 But these are the angles subtended by the diagonals AC and BD in the same circle
Therefore, AC = BD

Q 13

The circumcentre of the triangle ABC is O. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 90°

Solution

To prove: In the given figure ∠OBC + ∠BAC = 90°


Proof:O is the centre of the circle.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}OB = OC = r{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB = x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + x + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180° (Angle sum property of {tex}\triangle{/tex}OBC)
2x + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180°- 2x
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC 
180 - 2x = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90 - x = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC = (90 - x) + x
{tex}\angle\mathrm{BAC}+\angle\mathrm{OBC}=90^\circ{/tex}

Q 14

A chord of a circle is equal to its radius. Find the angle subtended by this chord at a point in major segment.

Solution

Since chord of a circle is equal radius, so we have AB = OA = OB.
Therefore, ABC is an equilateral triangle.

Since each angle of an equilateral triangle is 60°, so we have {tex}\angle AOB = 60^\circ{/tex}
Since angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle, so we have
{tex}\angle AOB = 2\angle ACB{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\angle ACB = \frac{1}{2}\angle AOB = \frac{1}{2} \times 60^\circ = 30^\circ{/tex}

Q 15

In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = 130° and chord BC = chord BE. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE.

Solution

In the given figure consider the points A, B, C, and D which form a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180° (because of property of opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral)
    130° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180°   
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 50°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOE, we have 
BC = BE     (Equal chords) 
OC = OE     (Radii)
OB = OB     (Common)
Therefore, By SSS rule, we have

   {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC = {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOE
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}OBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}OBE = 50° ( by CPCT)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBO + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBO = 50° + 50° = 100°

Q 16

In the given figure, {tex}\angle ACB = 40^\circ{/tex}. Find {tex}\angle OAB{/tex}.

Solution

Since the angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle, so we have
{tex}\angle AOB = 2\angle ACB = 2 \times 40^\circ = 80^\circ{/tex}
So, in {tex}\triangle OAB{/tex} we have
{tex}p^\circ + p^\circ + \angle AOB = 180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2p^\circ + 80^\circ = 180^\circ{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 2p^\circ = 180^\circ - 80^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2p^\circ = 100^\circ{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow p^\circ = 100^\circ \div 2 = 50^\circ{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\angle OAB = 50^\circ{/tex}

Q 17

In the given figure, O is the centre of a circle and {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = 130°. If {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = x°, then find the value of x.

Solution

Since ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 130° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 50o
Since {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB is the angle in a semi-circle.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 90o
Using angle sum property in {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC, we obtain
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC + 90° + 50° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 40°

Q 18

Two circles with centre O and O' intersect at two points A and B. A line PQ is drawn parallel to OO' through B intersecting the circles at P and Q. Prove that PQ = 2OO'.

Solution

Construction: Draw two circles having centres O and O' intersecting at points A and B.
Draw a parallel line PQ to OO' Join OO', OP, O'Q, OM and O'N

To Prove: PQ = 2OO'
Proof: In {tex}\angle{/tex}OPB,
BM = MP ..................(i)
({tex}\perp{/tex} from the centre to the circle bisects the chord)
Similarly, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}O’BQ,
BN = NQ .........................(ii)
({tex}\perp{/tex} from the centre to the circle bisects the chord)
Adding (i) and (ii),
BM + BN = PM + NQ
Adding BM + BN to both the sides
BM + BN + BM + BN = BM + PM + NQ + BN
2BM + 2BN = PQ
2(BM + BN) = PQ .................(iii)
Again,
OO' = MN [As OO' NM is a rectangle] .................(iv)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2OO' = PQ
Henced proved.

Q 19

In given figure, AOB is a diameter of the circle and C, D, E are any three points on the semi-circle. Find the value of  {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD + {tex}\angle{/tex} BED.

Solution


Join BC,
Then, {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB = 90° (Angle in the semicircle)
Since DCBE is a cyclic quadrilateral. 
{tex}\angle{/tex}BCD + {tex}\angle{/tex}BED = 180°
Adding {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB both the sides, we get
{tex}\angle {/tex}BCD + {tex}\angle {/tex}BED + {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB +180°
({tex}\angle {/tex}BCD + {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB) + {tex}\angle {/tex}BED = 90° + 180°
{tex}\angle {/tex}ACD + {tex}\angle {/tex}BED = 270°

Q 20

∠OAB = 30º and ∠OCB = 57º. Find ∠BOC and ∠AOC.

Solution

In {tex}\Delta O B C{/tex} we have

OB = OC [Radii of the same circle]

{tex}\therefore \angle O C B = \angle O B C = 57 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Now, in {tex}\Delta B O C{/tex} , we have

{tex}\angle O C B + \angle O B C + \angle B O C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}57^\circ+57^\circ+\angle\mathrm{BOC}=180^\circ{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B O C = 180 ^ { \circ } - 114 ^ { \circ } = 66 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Again, in {tex}\Delta A O B{/tex} , we have

OB = OA [Radii of the same circle]

{tex}\angle O A B = \angle O B A =30{/tex}

{tex}\angle O A B + \angle O B A + \angle A O B = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 30 ^ { \circ } + 30 ^ { \circ } + ( \angle A O C + \angle B O C ) = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 60 ^ { \circ } + \angle A O C + 66 ^ { \circ } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O C = 180 ^ { \circ } - 126 ^ { \circ } = 54 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Hence, {tex}\angle B O C = 66 ^ { \circ }{/tex} and {tex}\angle A O C = 54 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

sections.html:
Q 1

If two equal chords of a circle intersect within the circle, prove that the segments of one chord are equal to corresponding segments of the other chord.

Solution

Given: Let AB and CD are two equal chords of a circle of centers
O intersecting each other at point E within the circle.
To prove: (a) AE = CE (b) BE = DE
Construction: Draw OM {tex}\bot {/tex} AB, ON {tex}\bot {/tex} CD. Also join OE.

Proof: In right triangles OME and ONE,
{tex}\angle {/tex}OME = {tex}\angle {/tex}ONE = 90°
OM = ON
[Equal chords are equidistance from the centre]
OE = OE [Common]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OME {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ONE [RHS rule of congruency]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ME = NE [By CPCT] ...(i)
Now, O is the centre of circle and OM {tex}\bot {/tex} AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AM = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AB [Perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord] ...(ii)
Similarly, NC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CD ...(iii)
But AB = CD [Given]
From eq. (ii) and (iii), AM = NC ...(iv)
Also MB = DN ...(v)
Adding (i) and (iv), we get,
AM + ME = NC + NE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = CE [Proved part (a)]
Now AB = CD [Given] ...(v)
AE = CE [Proved] ...(vi)
Subtracting eq. (vi) from eq. (v), we have
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - AE = CD - CE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BE = DE [Proved part (b)]

Q 2

If the non-parallel sides of a trapezium are equal, prove that it is cyclic.

Solution

Given: Non-parallel sides AD and BC of a trapezium are equal.
To prove: ABCD is a cyclic trapezium.
Construction: Draw DK {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and CP{tex}\perp{/tex} AB 
Proof: In it {tex}\Delta ADK{/tex} and {tex}\Delta BCP{/tex}
AD = BC
DK = CP [Distance between || sides]
DKA = CBP [each 90{tex}{}^\circ{/tex}]
By RHS criteria of congruency,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}DKA {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CPB
{tex}\angle A=\angle B {/tex}
{tex}\angle ADK=\angle BCP\\ \angle ADK+90{}^\circ =\angle BCP+90{}^\circ {/tex}
{tex}\angle ADC=\angle BCD{/tex}
{tex}\angle D=\angle C{/tex} ................(ii)
{tex}\angle A+\angle B+\angle C+\angle D=360{}^\circ [\angle A= \angle B , \angle C= \angle D]{/tex}
{tex}\angle B+\angle B+\angle D+\angle D=360{}^\circ \\ \angle B+\angle D=180{}^\circ {/tex}
Hence, ABCD is a cyclic trapezium.

Q 3

If P, Q and R are the mid-points of the sides BC, CA and AB of a triangle and AD is the perpendicular from A to BC, prove that P, Q, R and D are concyclic.

Solution

Given: P,Q,R and S are mid points and {tex}\mathrm{AD}\perp\mathrm{BC}{/tex}
To prove : R, D, P and Q are con-cyclic.

Construction:Join RD,RP,RQ and PQ
Proof :In ΔABC, R and Q are mid points of AB and CA respectively.
∴ RQ || BC (Mid point theorem)
Similarly, PQ || AB and PR || CA
Now in quadrilateral BPQR,
BP || RQ and PQ || BR
∴ Quadrilateral BPQR is a parallelogram.
Similarly, quadrilateral ARPQ is a parallelogram.
∴ ∠A = ∠RPQ (Opposite angles of parallelogram are equal)
PR || AC and PC is the transversal,
∴ ∠BPR = ∠C (Corresponding angles)
∠BPQ = ∠DPR + ∠RPQ = ∠A + ∠C ...(1)
RQ || BC and BR is the transversal,
∴ ∠ARO = ∠B (Corresponding angles) ...(2)
In ΔABD, R is the mid point of AB and OR || BD.
∴ O is the mid point of AD (Converse of mid point theorem)
⇒ OA = OD
In ΔAOR and ΔDOR,
OA = OD (Proved)
∠AOR = ∠DOR (90°) {∠ROD = ∠ODP (Alternate angles) & ∠AOR = ∠ROD = 90° (linear pair)}
OR = OR (Common)
∴ ΔAOR {tex}\cong{/tex} ΔDOR (SAS congruence criterion)
⇒ ∠ARO = ∠DRO (CPCT)
⇒ ∠DRO = ∠B (Using (2))
In quadrilateral PRQD,
∠DRO + ∠DPQ = ∠B + ( ∠A + ∠C) = ∠A + ∠B + ∠C (Using (1))
⇒ ∠DRO + ∠DPQ = 180° ( ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180°)
Hence, quadrilateral PRQD is a cyclic quadrilateral.
Thus, the points P, Q, R and D are concyclic.

Q 4

ABCD is a parallelogram. A circle through A, B is so drawn that it intersects AD at P and BC at Q. Prove that P, Q, C and D are concyclic.

Solution

ABCD is a parallelogram. A circle through A, B is so drawn that it intersects AD at P and BC at Q. We have to prove that P, Q, C and D are concyclic. Join PQ.

Now, side AP of the cyclic quadrilateral APQB is produced to D.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ext. {tex}\angle{/tex} 1 = int. opp. {tex}\angle{/tex}B
{tex}\because{/tex}  BA||CD and BC cuts them
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \angle \mathrm{B}+\angle \mathrm{C}=180^{\circ}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of int. {tex}\angle{/tex}S on the same side of the transversal is {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}]
or {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}B (proved)] 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PDCQ is cyclic quadrilateral.
Hence, the points P, Q, C and D are concyclic.

Q 5

Prove that angle bisector of any angle of a triangle and perpendicular bisector of the opposite side if intersect, they will intersect on the circumcircle of the triangle.

Solution

Here we have a {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} and L is perpendicular bisector of side BC.

To prove: Angles bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex} and perpendicular bisector of BC intersect on the circumcircle of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex}.
Proof: Let the angle bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex} intersect circumcircle of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex}  at P.
Construction: Join BP and CP.
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B A P = \angle B C P{/tex} ...[Angles in the same segment are equal]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B A P = \angle B C P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A{/tex} …...........................(1) [AP is bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex}]
Similarly, we have
{tex}\angle P A C = \angle P B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A{/tex}…................(2)
From equation (1) and (2), we obtain
{tex}\angle B C P = \angle P B C{/tex}
Since we know that if the angles subtended by two Chords of a circle at the centre are equal, the chords are equal.
Therefore, BP = CP  
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P is on perpendicular bisector of BC.
Hence, angle bisector of {tex}\angle A{/tex} and perpendicular bisector of BC intersect on the circumcircle of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} .

Q 6

If two chords AB and CD of a circle AYDZBWCX intersect at right angles, prove that arc CXA + arc DZB = arc AYD + arc BWC = semicircle.

Solution

Here it is given that the two chords AB and CD of circle AYDZBWCX intersect at right angles.

We need to prove that: arc CXA + arc DZB = arc AYD + arc BWC = semicircle.
Construction: Join AC, AD, BD and BC as shown in the figure.
Proof: O is any point inside the circle.
Now, consider the chord CA. The angle subtended by the chord AC at the circumference is {tex}\angle C B A{/tex} and similarly, the angle subtended by the chord BD at the circumference is {tex}\angle B C D{/tex}

Now, consider the right triangle BOC, we have:
{tex}\angle C O B + \angle C B A + \angle B C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}   (by angle sum property)

{tex}\Rightarrow 90 ^ { \circ } + \angle C B A + \angle B C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C B A + \angle B C D = 180 ^ { \circ } - 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C B A + \angle B C D = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

That is the sum of angle subtended by the arc CXA and the angles subtended by the arc BZD = 90o.

{tex}\operatorname arc \widehat { CXA } + \operatorname { arc } \widehat { B Z D } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ...........................….(1)

Now, consider the chord BC.

The angles subtended by the chord BC at the centre is {tex}\angle B A C{/tex}

Similarly, the angle subtended by the Chord AD at the centre is {tex}\angle A C D{/tex}

Now, consider the right triangle AOC, we have:

{tex}\angle C O A + \angle B A C + \angle A C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}  (by angle sum property)

{tex}\Rightarrow 90 ^ { \circ } + \angle B A C + \angle A C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B A C + \angle A C D = 180 ^ { \circ } - 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \angle B A C + \angle A C D = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

That is the sum of angle subtended by the are CWB and the angle subtended by the are AYD = 90o.

{tex}\operatorname arc \widehat { CWB } + \operatorname { arc } \widehat { AYD } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}.........................................(2)

From equations (1) and (2), we obtain

{tex}\operatorname arc\widehat{CXA}+\operatorname{arc}\widehat{BZD}=\operatorname arc\widehat{CWB}+\operatorname{arc}\widehat{AYD}=90^\circ{/tex}

We know that the arc of a circle subtending a right angle at any point of the circle in its alternate segment is a semi-circle.

Thus, we have

{tex}\operatorname arc\widehat{CXA}+\operatorname{arc}\widehat{BZD}=\operatorname arc\widehat{CWB}+\operatorname{arc}\widehat{AYD}={/tex}semicircle

Hence proved.

Q 7

If ABC is an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle and P be any point on the minor arc BC which does not coincide with B or C, prove that PA is angle bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}BPC.

Solution

Since equal chords of a circle subtends equal angles at the centre, so we have chord AB = chord AC [Given]

So {tex}\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\angle \mathrm{AOC}{/tex} ...(i)
since the angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle,
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{APC}=\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{AOC}{/tex} ...(ii)
and {tex}\angle \mathrm{APB}=\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{AOB}{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{APC}=\angle \mathrm{APB}{/tex} [from (i), (ii) and (iii)]
Hence, PA is the bisector of  {tex}\angle{/tex}BPC.

Q 8

In Fig., AB and CD are two chords of a circle intersecting each other at point E. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}AEC = {tex}\frac12{/tex} (Angle subtended by arc CXA at centre + angle subtended by arc DYB at the centre).

Solution

AB and CD are two chords of a circle intersecting each other at point E.

We have to prove that {tex}\angle \mathrm{AEC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Angle subtended by arc CXA at the centre + angle subtended by are DYB at the centre)
since the angle subtended by an are at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle,
Now, are CXA subtends {tex}\angle AOC{/tex} at the centre and {tex}\angle ABC{/tex} at the remaining part of the circle, so
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AOC}=2 \angle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex} ...(i)
Similarly, {tex}\angle \mathrm{BOD}=2 \angle \mathrm{BCD}{/tex} ...(ii)
Now, adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AOC}+\angle \mathrm{BOD}=2(\angle \mathrm{ABC}+\angle \mathrm{BCD}){/tex} ...(iii)
since the exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of interior opposite angles, so in {tex}\Delta \mathrm{CEB}{/tex} we have,
{tex}\therefore \ \angle A E C=\angle A B C+\angle B C D{/tex} ...(iv)
From (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\angle \mathrm{AOC}+\angle \mathrm{BOD}=2 \angle \mathrm{AEC}{/tex}
or
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AEC}=\frac{1}{2}(\angle \mathrm{AOC}+\angle \mathrm{BOD}){/tex}
Hence, {tex}\angle \mathrm{AEC}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Angle subtended by an arc CXA at the centre angle subtended by an arc DYB at the centre).

Q 9

If bisectors of opposite angles ({tex}\angle \mathrm{A}{/tex} and {tex}\angle C{/tex}) of a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD intersect the circle, circumscribing it at the points P and Q, prove that PQ is a diameter of the circle.

Solution

The bisectors of opposite angles {tex}\angle \mathrm{A}{/tex} and {tex}\angle C{/tex} of a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD intersect the circle at the point P and Q respectively.

We have to prove that PQ is a diameter of the circle. Join AQ and DQ. since opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary so in cyclic quadrilateral ABCD,
we have {tex}\angle \mathrm{DAB}+\angle \mathrm{DCB}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{DAB}+\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{DCB}=\frac{1}{2}\left(180^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow &\angle 1+\angle 2=90^{\circ} \end{aligned}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} AP and CQ are the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}A and {tex}\angle{/tex}C respectively]
{tex}\therefore \ \angle 1+\angle 3=90^{\circ} \quad[\because \angle 2=\angle 3]{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 and {tex}\angle{/tex}3 are angles in the same segment of a circle with chord QD]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{PAQ}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PAQ is in a semi-circle
Hence, PQ is a diameter of circle.

Q 10

A circle has radius {tex}\sqrt 2 cm{/tex}. It is divided into two segments by a chord of length 2 cm. Prove that the angle subtended by the chord at a point in major segment is 45°.

Solution

A circle with centre O and radius {tex}\sqrt 2 cm{/tex}. Chord BC, 2cm long divides the circle into two segment.
{tex}\angle BAC{/tex} lies in the major segment.

We have to prove that {tex}\angle BAC = 45^\circ{/tex}
Join OB and OC.
{tex}B{C^2} = {(2)^2} = 4 = 2 + 2 = {\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)^2} + {\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)^2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;B{C^2} = O{B^2} + O{C^2}{/tex}
In {tex}\Delta BOC{/tex}, we have
 {tex}B{C^2} = O{B^2} + O{C^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;\angle BOC = 90^\circ{/tex} [By converse of Pythagoras theorem]
Now,  BC subtends {tex}\angle BOC{/tex} at the centre O and {tex}\angle BAC{/tex} at the remaining part of the circle.
{tex}\therefore \;\angle BAC = \frac{1}{2}\angle BOC = \frac{1}{2} \times 90^\circ = 45^\circ{/tex}
Hence, proved.

Q 11

Two equal chords AB and CD of a circle when produced intersect at a point P. Prove that PB = PD.

Solution

Given: AB and CD two equal chords of a circle with centre O when produced intersect at P.
To prove: PB = PD
Construction: Draw OR {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and OQ {tex}\perp{/tex} CD. Join OP.
Proof: Since OR {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and OQ {tex}\perp{/tex} CD from the centre O of circle

{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is mid-point of AB and Q is the mid-point of CD  [{tex}\because \ \perp{/tex} from the centre to a chord bisects the chord]
as AB= CD [Given]
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{AB}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CD}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  AR = CQ and RB = QD ...(i)
NOW,AB = CD,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OR = OQ ...(ii)    [{tex}\because{/tex} Equal chords are equidistant from the centre] Now, in right-angled {tex}\Delta{/tex}s ORP and OQP, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}ORP = {tex}\angle {/tex}OQP   [Each {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}]
hyp. OP = hyp. OP   [Common side OR = OQ [From (ii)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ORP {tex}\cong{/tex} OQP        [By R.H.S. axiom] RP = QP [CPCT]
Now, subtracting (i) from (iii) RP - RB = QP - QD
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PD}{/tex}
Hence, proved.

Q 12

AB and AC are two chords of a circle of radius r such that AB = 2AC. If p and q are the distances of AB and AC from the centre, prove that 4q2 = p2 + 3r2.

Solution

Here it is given that the circle with centre O and radius r in which there are two chords such that AB = 2AC. {tex}O L \perp A B{/tex} and {tex}OM \perp A C{/tex}. Also OL = p and OM = q.

 

We need to prove that 4q2 = p2 + 3r2  

Since the perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord In right angled {tex}\Delta A O L{/tex} , we have

{tex}r ^ { 2 } = A L ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 }{/tex}  (by pythagoreous theorem)

{tex}\Rightarrow A L ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } A B \right) ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 4 } A B ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow A B ^ { 2 } = 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow ( 2 A C ) ^ { 2 } = 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} AB = 2AC]

{tex}\Rightarrow 4 A C ^ { 2 } = 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} …..................(1)

Again, in right {tex}\Delta A O M{/tex} we have

{tex}r ^ { 2 } = A M ^ { 2 } + q ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow A M ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Since {tex}\bot{/tex}from the centre to a chord bisects the chord

{tex}\therefore \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } A C \right) ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 4 } A C ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow A C ^ { 2 } = 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} ….....................(2)

From equations (1) and (2), we obtain

{tex}4 \left\{ 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 } \right) \right\} = 4 \left( r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 4 r ^ { 2 } - 4 q ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow 4 q ^ { 2 } = 3 r ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Hence, {tex}4 q ^ { 2 } = p ^ { 2 } + 3 r ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Q 13

O is the centre of the circle, ∠BCO = 30°. Find x and y.

Solution


In right {tex}\Delta O C P{/tex} we have
{tex}\angle P O C = 180 ^ { \circ } - ( \angle O P C + \angle P C O ){/tex}
{tex}\angle\mathrm{POC}=180^\circ-(90+30)=60^\circ{/tex}
And {tex}\angle A O D = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Given]
{tex}\angle A O D + \angle D O P = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Angles of a linear pair]
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle D O P = 180 ^ { \circ } - \angle A O D{/tex}
{tex}= 180 ^ { \circ } - 90 ^ { \circ } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Now,  {tex}\angle C O D = 90 ^ { \circ } - \angle P O C = 90 ^ { \circ } - 60 ^ { \circ } = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Since the angles subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it any point on the remaining part of the circle,
{tex}\therefore \angle C B D = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle C O D \Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 30 ^ { \circ } = 15 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Also, {tex}\angle A B D = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \angle A O D = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 90 ^ { \circ } = 45 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\Delta A B P{/tex} we have {tex}x + \left( 45 ^ { 0 } + y \right) + 90 ^ { 0 } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x + 45 ^ { \circ } + 15 ^ { 0 } + 90 ^ { \circ } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = 180 ^ { \circ } - 150 ^ { \circ } = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Therefore x =30° and y = 15°

Q 14

In the figure, O is the centre of the circle, BD = OD and CD ⊥ AB. Find ∠CAB.

Solution

From the figure shown below, In {tex}\Delta{/tex} BOD, we have

BD = OD [Given]

{tex}\therefore \angle D O B = \angle D B O{/tex} ........................[{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opp. to equal sides of triangle are equal]

 

Let CD intersect AB at P.

Now in  {tex}\Delta O D P{/tex} and {tex}\Delta B D P{/tex}, we have

{tex}\angle D O P = \angle D B P{/tex} [{tex}\because \angle D O B = \angle D B O{/tex}]

{tex}\angle D P O = \angle D P B{/tex}  ......[Each is 90o]

OD = BD ......[Given]

{tex}\therefore \Delta O D P \cong \Delta B D P{/tex} [By AAS congruence rule]

{tex}\therefore\angle ODP=\angle BDP{/tex} …(1) [By C.P.C.T.]

Now, OD = OB [Radii of the same circle]

And OD = BD [Given]

{tex}\therefore{/tex} OB = OD = BD, so {tex}\Delta O B D{/tex} is an equilateral triangle.

{tex}\therefore\angle ODB=60^\circ{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Each angle of equilateral triangle is 60o]

Now,{tex}\angle B D P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 60 ^ { \circ } = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex} or {tex}\angle C D B = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Since, angles in the same segment of a circle are equal, so we have

So, {tex}\angle C A B = \angle C D B = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

The given figures show two congruent circles with centre O and O’. Arc AXB subtends an angle of {tex}{75^o}{/tex} at the centre and arc A’YB’ subtends an angle of {tex}{25^o}{/tex} at the centre O’. Then, the ratio of arcs AXB to A ‘YB’ is

Q 2

In Fig., AB and CD are two equal chords of a circle with centre O. OP and OQ are perpendiculars on chords AB and CD, respectively. If {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ = 150º, then {tex}\angle{/tex}APQ is equal to

Q 3

The angles subtended by a chord at any two points of a circle are equal.

Solution

False, If two points lie in the same segment (major or minor) only, then the angles will be equal otherwise they are not equal.

Q 4

Two chords of a circle of lengths 10 cm and 8 cm are at the distances 8.0 cm and 3.5 cm, respectively from the centre.

Solution

False. As the larger chord is at smaller distance from the centre.

Q 5

In Fig., AOC is a diameter of the circle and arc AXB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} are BYC. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC.

Solution

As arc AXB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} arc BYC,
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\frac{1}{2} \angle \mathrm{BOC}{/tex}
Also {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180º
Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180º
or {tex}\angle \mathrm{BOC}=\frac{2}{3} \times 180^{\circ}=120^{\circ}{/tex}.

Q 6

In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 45o, prove that OA {tex}\perp{/tex}OC.

Solution

As we know that the angle subtended by an arc at the center is twice the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle.
Therefore, we can write
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 45o = 90o
Thus, OA {tex}\perp{/tex}OC.

Hence proved

Q 7

Prove that two circles cannot intersect at more than two points.

Solution

Let there be two circles which intersect at three points say at A, B and C. Clearly, A, B and C are not collinear. We know that through three non-collinear points A, B and C one and only one circle can pass. Therefore, there cannot be two circles passing through A, B and C. In other words, the two circles cannot intersect at more than two points.

Q 8

Prove that among all the chords of a circle passing through a given point inside the circle that one is smallest which is perpendicular to the diameter passing through the point.

Solution

Let P be the given point inside a circle with centre O. Draw the chord AB which is perpendicular to the diameter XY through P. Let CD be any other chord through P. Draw ON perpendicular to CD from O. Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}ONP is a right triangle (Fig.).
Therefore, its hypotenuse OP is larger than ON. We know that the chord nearer to the centre is larger than the chord which is farther to the centre. Therefore, CD > AB. In other words, AB is the smallest of all chords passing through P.

sections.label: Exercise 11.2
sections.html:
Q 1

An angle of 52.5o can be constructed.

Solution

True
To construct an angle of 52.5o firstly construct an angle of 90o, then construct an angle of 120o and then plot an angle bisector of 120o and 90o to get an angle 105o (90o + 15o). Now, bisect this angle to get an angle of 52.5o.

Q 2

An angle of 42.5o can be constructed.

Solution

False
We know that, 42.5o = {tex}\frac 12 \times{/tex} 85o and an angle of 85o cannot be constructed with the help of ruler and compass.

Q 3

A triangle ABC can be constructed in which AB = 5 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 45o and BC + AC = 5 cm.

Solution

False
We know that, a triangle can be constructed, if sum of its two sides is greater than third side.
Here, BC + AC = AB = 5 cm
So, triangle ABC cannot be constructed.

Q 4

A triangle ABC can be constructed in which BC = 6 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 30° and AC – AB = 4 cm.

Solution

True
We know that, a triangle can be constructed if sum of its two sides is greater than third side. i.e., in triangle ABC, AB + BC > AC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC > AC – AB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 > 4, which is true, so triangle ABC with given conditions can be constructed.

Q 5

A triangle ABC can be constructed in which {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 105o, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 90o and AB + BC + AC = 10 cm.

Solution

False
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 105o + 90o = 195o > 80o and the sum of all the angles of a triangle is 180o.

Q 6

A triangle ABC can be constructed in which {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 60o, {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45o and AB + BC + AC = 12 cm.

Solution

True
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 60o + 45o = 105o < 180o

sections.label: Exercise 11.3
sections.html:
Q 1

Draw an angle of 110° with the help of a protractor and bisect it. Measure each angle. Justify your answer.

Solution

Given: An angle ABC = 110°.
Required: To draw the bisector of {tex}\angle ABC{/tex}
  
Steps of construction:

  1. With B as centre and a convenient radius draw an arc to intersect the ray’s BA and BC at P and Q respectively.
  2. With centre P and a radius greater than half of PQ, draw an arc.
  3. With Centre Q and the same radius (as in step 2), draw another arc to cut the previous arc at R.
  4. Draw ray BR. This ray BR is the required bisectors of {tex}\angle ABC{/tex}.
Q 2

Draw a line segment AB of 4 cm in length. Draw a line perpendicular to AB through A and B, respectively. Are these lines parallel?

Solution

Given: A line segment AB of length 4cm.
Required: To draw a perpendicular to AB through A and B, respectively.

Steps of construction:

  1. Draw AB = 4 cm.
  2. With A as a centre and any convenient radius, draw an arc, cutting Ab at P.
  3. With P as a centre and the same radius, draw an arc cutting the arc drawn in step 2 at Q.
  4. With Q as a centre and the same radius as in steps 2 and 3, draw an arc, cutting the arc drawn in step 3 at R.
  5. With Q as a centre and the same radius, draw an arc.
  6. With R as a centre and the same radius, draw an arc, cutting the arc drawn in step 5 at X.
  7. Draw OX and produced it to C and D.
  8. Now, repeat the steps from 2 to 7 to draw the line EF perpendicular through B.
    Yes, these lines are parallel because the sum of the interior angles on the same side of the transversal is 180°.
Q 3

Draw an angle of 80° with the help of a protractor. Then construct angles of

  1. 40°
  2. 160° and
  3. 120°.
Solution

First, draw an angle of 80o say {tex}\angle{/tex}QOA = 180o with the help of protractor. Now, use the the following steps to construct angles of (1) 40o (2) 160o (3) 120o.

  1. Taking O as centre and any radius draw an arc which intersect OA at E and OO at F.
  2. Taking E and F as centres and radius more than {tex}\frac12{/tex}EF draw arcs which intersect each other at P.
  3. Join OP Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}POA = 40o [{tex}\therefore{/tex} 40o = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 80o]
  4. Now, taking F as centre and radius equal to EF draw an arc which intersect previous arc obtained in step ii at S.
  5. Join OS. Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}SOA = 160o [{tex}\therefore{/tex} 160o = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 80o]
  6. Taking S and F as centre and radius more than {tex}\frac12{/tex}SF draw arcs which intersect each other at R.
  7. Join OR. Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}ROA = {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ = 40o + 80o = 120o.
Q 4

Construct a triangle whose each sides are 3.6 cm, 3 cm and 4.8 cm. Bisect the smallest angle and measure each part.

Solution

To construct a triangle ABC in which AB = 3.6 cm, AC = 3.0 cm and BC = 4. 8 cm, use the following steps.

  1. Draw a line segment BC of length 4.8 cm.
  2. From B, point A is at a distance of 3.6 cm. So, having B as centre, draw an arc of radius 3.6 cm.
  3. From C, point A is at a distance of 3 cm. So, having C as centre, draw an arc of radius 3 cm which intersect the previous arc at A.
  4. Join AB and AC.
  5. Thus, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is the required triangle.
Q 5

Construct a triangle ABC in which BC = 5 cm, {tex}\angle B = 60^\circ{/tex} and AC + AB = 7.5 cm.

Solution

Given: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} , BC = 5 cm, AC + AB = 7.5 cm and {tex}\angle B = 60^\circ{/tex}
Required: To construct
 
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a ray BX and cut off a line segment BC = 5 cm from it.
  2. At B, construct  
  3. With B as centre and radius = 7.5 cm, draw an arc at meet BY at D.
  4. Join CD.
  5. Draw the perpendicular bisector of CD, intersecting BD at A.
  6. Join AC. Then, ABC is the required triangle.
Q 6

Construct a square of side 3 cm.

Solution


Steps of construction:

  1. Take AB = 3 cm.
  2. At A, draw {tex}AY \bot AB{/tex}.
  3. With A as centre and radius = 3cm, describe an arc cutting AY at D.
  4. With B and D as centres and radii equal to 3 cm, draw arc intersecting at C.
  5. Join BC and DC,  ABCD is the required square.
Q 7

Construct a rectangle whose adjacent sides are of lengths 5 cm and 3.5 cm.

Solution


Steps of construction:

  1. Take AB = 5cm.
  2. Draw {tex}AY \bot AB{/tex}.
  3. With A as centre and radius = 3.5cm, describe an arc cutting AY at D.
  4. With D as centre and radius 5cm, describe an arc with B as centre and radius 3.5cm, describe another arc intersecting the first arc at C.
  5. Join BC and DC. ABCD is the required rectangle.
Q 8

Construct a rhombus whose each side is of length 3.4 cm and one of its angles is 45°.

Solution

Steps of construction:

  1. Take AB = 3.4 cm
  2. At A and B, construct {tex}\angle{/tex}BAM = 45o and {tex}\angle{/tex}TBP = 45o respectively
  3. From AM cut off AD = 3.4 cm and from BP cut Off BC = 3.4 cm.
  4. Join AD, DC and BC. ABCD is the required rhombus.

sections.label: Exercise 11.4
sections.html:
Q 1

Construct and justify a triangle if its perimeter is 10.4 cm and two angles are 45o and 120o.

Solution

Let ABC be a triangle. Then, given perimeter = 10.4 cm i.e., AB+ BC + CA = 10.4 cm and two angles are 45o and 120o.
say {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 45o and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 120o
Now, to construct the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC use the following steps.

  1. Draw a line segment say XY and equal to perimeter i.e., AB+ BC + CA = 10.4 cm.
  2. Make angle {tex}\angle{/tex}LXY = ∠B = 45° and {tex}\angle{/tex}MYX = ∠C = 120°.
  3. Bisect {tex}\angle{/tex}LXY and {tex}\angle{/tex}MYX and let these bisectors intersect at a point A (say).
  4. Draw perpendicular bisectors PQ and RS of AX and AY, respectively.
  5. Let PQ intersect XY at B and RS intersect XY at C. Join AB and AC. Thus, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is the required triangle.


Justification
Since, B lies on the perpendicular bisector PQ of AX.
Thus, AB + BC + CA = XB + BC + CY = XY
Again, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAX = {tex}\angle{/tex}AXB [{tex}\therefore{/tex} in ΔAXB, AB = XB] …(i)
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAX + ∠AXB [ ∠ABC is an exterior angle of ΔAXB]
= {tex}\angle{/tex}AXB + {tex}\angle{/tex}AXB [from Eq. (i)]
= 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}AXB = {tex}\angle{/tex}LXY [ AX is a bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}LXB]
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}CAY = {tex}\angle{/tex}AYC [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} in A AYC, AC = CY]
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAY + {tex}\angle{/tex}AYC [ {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB is an exterior angle of {tex}\triangle{/tex}AYC]
= {tex}\angle{/tex}CAY + {tex}\angle{/tex}CAY
= 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}CAY = {tex}\angle{/tex}MYX [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} AY is a bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}MYX]
Thus, our construction is justified.

Q 2

Construct and justify a triangle PQR, given that QR = 3cm, {tex}\angle PQR = 45^\circ{/tex} and QP – PR = 2 cm.

Solution

  1. Draw a ray OX and cut off a line segment QR = 3 cm.
  2. At Q, construct {tex}\angle PQR = 45^\circ{/tex}.
  3. From QY, cut off QS = 2 cm.
  4. Join RS.
  5. Draw perpendicular bisector of RS to Meet QY at P.
  6. Join PR. Then PQR is the required triangle.

Justification
Base QR and {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR are drawn as given.
Since, the point P lies on the perpendicular bisector of SR.
PS = PR
Now, QS = PQ - PS
= PQ - PR
Thus, our construction is justified.

Q 3

Construct and justify a right triangle whose one side is 3.5 cm and the sum of other sides and the hypotenuse is 5.5 cm.

Solution

Given: In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, base BC = 3.5 cm, the sum of other side and hypotenuse i.e., AB + AC = 5.5 and {tex}\angle ABC = 90^\circ{/tex}. 

Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a ray BX and cut off a line segment BC = 3.5 cm from it.
  2. Construct {tex}\angle XBY = 90^\circ{/tex}.
  3. From BY cut off a line segment BD = 5.5 cm.
  4. Join CD.
  5. Draw the perpendicular bisector of CD intersecting BD at A.
  6. Join AC. Then, ABC is the required triangle.

Justification
Base BC and {tex}\angle{/tex}B are drawn as given.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC [by construction]
AD = AC …(i)
[sides opposite to equal angles are equal] Now, AB = BD – AD = BD – AC [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BD = AB + AC
Thus, our construction is justified.

Q 4

Construct and justify an equilateral triangle if its altitude is 3.2 cm.

Solution


Steps of Construction:

  1. Draw a line.
  2. Mark any point D on the line.
  3. At point D, draw {tex}\overrightarrow {DX} \bot l{/tex} and cut DA = 3.2 cm from {tex}\overrightarrow {DX}{/tex}.
  4. At the point A, construct AB and AC which meets the l at points B and C respectively such that
    {tex}\angle DAB = 30^\circ{/tex} and {tex}\angle DAC = 30^\circ{/tex}
    Then {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} is the required equilateral triangle because
    {tex}\angle ABC = 180^\circ - (90^\circ + 30^\circ ) = 60^\circ{/tex}
    {tex}\angle ACB = 180^\circ - (90^\circ + 30^\circ ) = 60^\circ{/tex}
    And {tex}\angle BAC = 30^\circ + 30^\circ = 60^\circ{/tex}.

Justification
Here, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD
= 30o + 30° = 60o.
Also, AD ⊥ SC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADS = 90o.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = 180o [angle sum property]
30o + 90o + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = 180o [{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = 30o, by construction]
{tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = 60o
Similary, {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA = 60o
Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B= {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 60o
Hence, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an equilateral triangle.

Q 5

Construct and justify a rhombus whose diagonals are 4 cm and 6 cm in lengths.

Solution


Steps of construction:

  1. Take AC = 6 cm.
  2. Draw BD the right bisectors of AC.
  3. Cut off MB = MD = 2 cm.
  4. Join AB, BC, CD and DA.
    Hence, ABCD is the required rhombus.

Justification
Since, D and B lie on perpendicular bisector of AC.
DA = DC and BA = BC …(i)
[since, every point on perpendicular bisector of line segment is equidistant from end points
of line segment]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}DOC = 90o
Also, OD = OB = 3 cm
Thus, AC is perpendicular bisector or BD.
CD = CB …(ii)
AB = BC =CD = DA
from Eq (i)and (ii)
ABCD is a rhombus.

Q 1

With the help of a ruler and a compass, it is possible to construct an angle of:

Q 2

The construction of a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} in which AB = 4 cm, {tex}\angle A = {60^0}{/tex} is not possible when difference of BC and AC is equal to ____.

Q 3

An angle of 67.5° can be constructed.

Solution

True. As 67.5o = {tex}\frac {135^o}2 = \frac 12{/tex} (90o + 45o).

Q 4

Construct a triangle ABC in which BC = 8 cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 45° and AB – AC = 3.5 cm.

Solution

The below given steps will be followed to draw the required triangle.
Step I: Draw the line segment BC = 8 cm and at point B, make an angle of 45°, say {tex}\angle{/tex}XBC.
Step II: Cut the line segment BD = 3.5 cm (equal to AB − AC) on ray BX.
Step III: Join DC and draw the perpendicular bisector PQ of DC.
Step IV: Let it intersect BX at point A. Join AC. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is the required triangle.

Q 5

Construct an equilateral triangle, given its side 6 cm and justify the construction.

Solution

Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line segment BC of length 6 cm.
  2. At B draw{tex} \angle XBC = {{60}^{\circ }}.{/tex}
  3. Draw perpendicular bisector PQ of line segment BC.
  4. Let A and D be the points where PQ intersects the ray BX and side BC respectively.
  5. Join AC.


Thus ABC is the required equilateral triangle.
Justification:
In right triangle ADB and right triangle ADC,
AD = AD [Common]
{tex}\angle ADB = \angle ADC = {{90}^{\circ }}{/tex} [By construction]
BD = CD [By construction]
{tex}\therefore \Delta ADB \cong \Delta ADC{/tex} [By SAS congruency]
{tex}\therefore\angle B = \angle C = {{60}^{\circ }}{/tex}[By CPCT]
{tex}\therefore \angle A = 180{}^\circ - ( \angle B + \angle C){/tex}
={tex} {{180}^{\circ }}-\left( {{60}^{\circ }}+{{60}^{\circ }} \right)\\ = {{180}^{\circ }}-{{120}^{\circ }} = {{60}^{\circ }}{/tex} = 180o - (60o + 60o) = 180o - 120o = 60o 
{tex}\therefore \angle A = \angle B = \angle C = {{60}^{\circ }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} ABC is an equilateral triangle.
{tex}\therefore \Delta ABC {/tex} is an equilateral triangle.

sections.label: Exercise 12.2
sections.html:
Q 1

The area of a triangle with base 4 cm and height 6 cm is 24 cm2. Justify your answer.

Solution

False
We know that, area of a triangle {tex}= \frac {1} {2} {/tex}(Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height)
Here Base = 4 cm and Height = 6 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a triangle  {tex}= \frac {1} {2} \times 4 \times 6{/tex} = 12 cm2

Q 2

The area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is 8 cm2 in which AB = AC = 4 cm and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90º. justify your answer.

Solution

True
We have, ABC is a right angled triangle at A, where sides are given AB = AC = 4 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a triangle ABC {tex}= \frac {1} {2} (Base \times Height){/tex}
{tex}= \frac {1} {2} \times AC \times AB{/tex}
{tex}= \frac {1} {2} \times 4 \times 4{/tex} = 8 cm2

Q 3

The area of the isosceles triangle is {tex}\frac{5}{4} \sqrt{11} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}, if the perimeter is 11 cm and the base is 5 cm. justify your answer.

Solution

True
Let equal sides of an isosceles triangle to b.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of a triangle, 2s = b + b + 5 [{tex}\because{/tex} 2s = a + b + c]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 11 = 2b + 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b = 11 - 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac {6} {2}{/tex} = 3 cm
We know that, area of an isosceles triangle {tex}=\frac{a}{4} \sqrt{4 b^{2}-a^{2}}{/tex}
Here, sides of triangle are a = 5 cm and b = 3 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of an isosceles triangle {tex}=\frac{5 \sqrt{4(3)^{2}-(5)^{2}}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5 \sqrt{4 \times 9-25}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=5 \frac{\sqrt{36-25}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5 \sqrt{11}}{4} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}

Q 4

The area of the equilateral triangle is {tex}20 \sqrt{3}{/tex} cm2 whose each side is 8 cm. justify your answer.

Solution

False
Given, side of an equilateral triangle be 8 cm.
Area of the equilateral triangle {tex}=(\frac {{\sqrt 3}} {4}){/tex} (Side)2
{tex}=(\frac {{\sqrt 3}} {4} ) \times (8)^2 = (\frac {64} {4}){/tex} {tex}\sqrt {3}{/tex} [{tex}\therefore{/tex} side = 8 cm]
{tex}= 16 \sqrt {3} cm^2{/tex}

Q 5

If the side of a rhombus is 10 cm and one diagonal is 16 cm, the area of the rhombus is 96 cm2. justify your answer.

Solution

True
Given, side of a rhombus PQRS is 10 cm and one of the diagonal is 16 cm.
i.e., PQ = QR = RS = SP = 10 cm and PR = 16 cm

In {tex}\triangle {/tex}PQR, PQ2 = OP2 + OQ2 [by Pythagoras theorem]
[Since, the diagonal of rhombus bisects each other at 90°] 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OQ2 = PQ2 - OP2 = (10)2 - (8)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OQ2 = 100 - 64 = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OQ = 6 cm
[taking positive square root because length is always positive]
SQ = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} OP = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 12 cm
Area of rhombus {tex}=\frac {1} {2}{/tex} (Product of diagonals)
{tex}=\frac {1} {2}{/tex} (OS {tex}\times{/tex} PR) {tex}=\frac {1} {2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 96 cm2

Q 6

The base and the corresponding altitude of a parallelogram are 10 cm and 3.5 cm, respectively. The area of the parallelogram is 30 cm2. justify your answer.

Solution

False
Given, parallelogram in which base = 10 cm and altitude = 3.5 cm
Area of a parallelogram = Base {tex}\times{/tex} Altitude = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 cm2

Q 7

The area of a regular hexagon of side a is the sum of the areas of the five equilateral triangles with side a. justify your answer.

Solution

False
We know that regular hexagon is divided into six equilateral triangles

Area of a regular hexagon of side a = Sum of area of the six equilateral triangles.

Q 8

The cost of levelling the ground in the form of a triangle having the sides 51 m, 37 m and 20 m at the rate of Rs 3 per m2 is Rs 918. justify your answer.

Solution

True
Let sides of a triangle be a = 51m, b = 37m and c = 20m
Now, semi-perimeter of triangle {tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{51+37+20}{2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{108}{2}{/tex} = 54m 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} [by Heron's formula]
{tex}=\sqrt{54(54-51)(54-37)(54-20)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{54 \times 3 \times 17 \times 34}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{9 \times 3 \times 2 \times 3 \times 17 \times 17 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}=3 \times 3 \times 2 \times 17{/tex}
= 306m2
{tex}\because{/tex} Cost of levelling per m2 = Rs.3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of levelling per 306 m2
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 306
= Rs. 918

Q 9

In a triangle, the sides are given as 11 cm, 12 cm and 13 cm. The length of the altitude is 10.25 cm corresponding to the side having length 12 cm. justify your answer.

Solution

True
Since the sides of a triangle are a = 11cm, b = 12cm, and c = 13cm.
Now, semi-perimeter, {tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{11+12+13}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18cm
Area of triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} [by Heron's formula]
{tex}=\sqrt{18(18-11)(18-12)(18-13)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{18 \times 7 \times 6 \times 5}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{3 \times 6 \times 7 \times 6 \times 5}{/tex}
{tex}=6 \sqrt{3 \times 7 \times 5}{/tex}
{tex}=6 \sqrt{105}=6 \times 10.25{/tex}
= 615 cm2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times B C \times A D{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} area of triangle {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (base {tex}\times{/tex} height)]
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 10.25{/tex}
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10.25 = 615cm2

sections.label: Exercise 12.3
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the cost of laying grass in a triangular field of sides 50 m, 65 m and 65 m at the rate of Rs7 per m2.

Solution

We have, 2s = 50 m + 65 m + 65 m = 180 m
S = 180 ÷ 2 = 90 m
Area of {tex}\Delta = \sqrt {s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {90(90 - 50)(90 - 65)(90 - 65)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {90 \times 40 \times 25 \times 25} = 60 \times 25{/tex}
= 1500m2.
Cost of laying grass at the rate of Rs7 per m2 = Rs(1500 × 7) = Rs10,500.

Q 2

The triangular side walls of a flyover have been used for advertisements. The sides of the walls are 13 m, 14 m and 15 m. The advertisements yield an earning of Rs2000 per m2 a year. A company hired one of its walls for 6 months. How much rent did it pay?

Solution

The sides of triangular side walls of flyover which have been used for advertisements are 13 m, 14 m, 15 m.
 {tex}s = \frac{{13 + 14 + 15}}{2} = \frac{{42}}{2} = 21m{/tex}
 {tex} = \sqrt {21(21 - 13)(21 - 14)(21 - 15)}{/tex}
 {tex} = \sqrt {21 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6}{/tex}
 {tex} = \sqrt {7 \times 3 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 7 \times 3 \times 2}{/tex}
 {tex} = 7 \times 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 84{m^2}{/tex}
It is given that the advertisement yield an earning of Rs. 2,000 per m2 a year.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rent for 1 m2 for 1 year = Rs. 2000
So, rent for 1 m2 for 6 months or {tex}\frac{1}{2}year = Rs(\frac{1}{2} \times 2000){/tex} = Rs. 1,000.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rent for 84 m2 for 6 months = Rs. (1000 × 84) = Rs. 84,000.

Q 3

From a point in the interior of an equilateral triangle, perpendiculars are drawn on the three sides. The lengths of the perpendiculars are 14 cm, 10 cm and 6 cm. Find the area of the triangle.

Solution

Let ABC be an equilateral triangle, O be the interior point and OQ, OR and OC are the perpendicular drawn from points O. Let the sides of an equilateral triangle be a m.

Area of {tex}\Delta AOB = \frac{1}{2} \times AB \times OP{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} Area of a triangle {tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times (base \times height){/tex}]
{tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times a \times 14 = 7a\,c{m^2}{/tex} …(1)
Area of {tex}\Delta OBC = \frac{1}{2} \times BC \times OQ = \frac{1}{2} \times a \times 10{/tex}
= 5a cm2 …(2)
Area of {tex}\Delta OAC = \frac{1}{2} \times AC \times OR = \frac{1}{2} \times a \times 6{/tex}
= 3a cm2…(3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of an equilateral {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}
= Area of {tex}(\Delta OAB + \Delta OBC + \Delta OAC){/tex}
= (7a + 5a + 3a) cm2
= 15a cm2…(4)
We have, semi-perimeter {tex}s = \frac{{a + a + a}}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;s = \frac{{3a}}{2}cm{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of an equilateral {tex}\Delta ABC = \sqrt {s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)}{/tex} [By Heron’s formula]
{tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{3a}}{2}\left( {\frac{{3a}}{2} - a} \right)\left( {\frac{{3a}}{2} - a} \right)\left( {\frac{{3a}}{2} - a} \right)}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{3a}}{2} \times \frac{a}{2} \times \frac{a}{2} \times \frac{a}{2}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4}{a^2}{/tex} …(5)
From equations (4) and (5), we get
{tex}\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4}{a^2} = 15a{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;a = \frac{{15 \times 4}}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{{60}}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \;a = \frac{{60}}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 }} = 20\sqrt 3 cm{/tex}
On putting {tex}a = 20\sqrt 3{/tex} in equation (5), we get
Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4}{(20\sqrt 3 )^2}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4} \times 400 \times 3 = 300\sqrt 3 c{m^2}{/tex}
Hence, the area of an equilateral triangle is {tex}300\sqrt 3 c{m^2}{/tex}.

Q 4

The perimeter of an isosceles triangle is 32 cm. The ratio of the equal side to its base is 3: 2. Find the area of the triangle.

Solution

As the sides of the equal to the base of an isosceles triangle is 3 : 2, so let the sides of an isosceles triangle be 3x, 3x and 2x.
Now, perimeter of triangle = 3x + 3x + 2x = 8x
Given Perimeter of triangle = 32 m
{tex}\therefore 8x = 32;x = 32 \div 8 = 4{/tex}
So, the sides of the isosceles triangle are {tex}(3 \times 4)cm,(3 \times 4)cm,(2 \times 4)cm{/tex} i.e., 12 cm, 12 cm and 8cm
{tex}\therefore \;s = \frac{{12 + 12 + 8}}{2} = \frac{{32}}{2} = 16cm{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {16(16 - 12)(16 - 12)(16 - 8)}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {16 \times 4 \times 4 \times 8} = \sqrt {4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex} = 4 \times 4 \times 2\sqrt 2 = 32\sqrt 2 c{m^2}{/tex}

Q 5

Find the area of a parallelogram given in Fig. Also find the length of the altitude from vertex A on the side DC.

Solution

For {tex}\Delta B C D{/tex}:
Let {tex}a\; =\; 17\; cm, b\; =\; 12\; cm, c \;= \;25 \;cm{/tex}
So its semi-perimeter, {tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{17+12+25}{2}=27 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \text { Area of } \Delta B C D=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{27(27-17)(27-12)(27-25)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{27 \times 10 \times 15 \times 2}=90 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Now, area of parallelogram ABCD = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta B C D{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 90 = 180 cm2
Also, area of parallelogram ABCD = base {tex}\times{/tex} height =  DC {tex}\times{/tex} AE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 180 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} AE
{tex}\Rightarrow A E=\frac{180}{12}{/tex} = 15 cm
Hence the length of altitude  is 15 cm.

Q 6

A field in the form of a parallelogram has sides 60 m and 40 m and one of its diagonals is 80 m long. Find the area of the parallelogram.

Solution

Let the field be ABCD.

Area of the parallelogram ABCD = 2(area of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex})…(1)
Now, the sides of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} are
a = 40 m, b = 60 m and c = 80 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Semi-perimeter of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex},
{tex}s = \frac{{a + b + c}}{2}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{40 + 60 + 80}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{180}}{2} = 90m{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \sqrt {s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)}{/tex} [By Heron’s Formula]
{tex} = \sqrt {90(90 - 40)(90 - 60)(90 - 80)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {90 \times 50 \times 30 \times 10}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {3 \times 30 \times 5 \times 10 \times 30 \times 10}{/tex}
{tex} = 300\sqrt {15} c{m^2} = 1161.895{m^2}{/tex}
Formula equation (1), we get
Area of parallelogram ABCD = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1161.895 = 2323.79 m2.

Q 7

The perimeter of a triangular field is 420 m and its sides are in the ratio 6 : 7 : 8. Find the area of the triangular field.

Solution

Suppose that the sides in metres are 6x, 7x and 8x.
Now, 6x + 7x + 8x = perimeter = 420
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 21x = 420
{tex}\Rightarrow \;x = \frac{{420}}{{21}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 20
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The sides of the triangular field are {tex}6 \times 20m,7 \times 20m,8 \times 20m,{/tex} i.e., 120 m, 140 m and 160 m.
Now, s = Half the perimeter of triangular field.
{tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times 420m = 210m{/tex}
Using Heron’s formula,
Area of triangular field {tex} = \sqrt {s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {210(210 - 120)(210 - 140)(210 - 160)}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {210 \times 90 \times 70 \times 50}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {66150000} = 8133.265{m^2}{/tex}
Hence, the area of the triangular field = 8133.265 m2.

Q 8

The sides of a quadrilateral ABCD are 6 cm, 8 cm, 12 cm and 14 cm (taken in order) respectively, and the angle between the first two sides is a right angle. Find its area.

Solution

ABCD is a given quadrilateral where
AB =6 cm , BC = 8  cm, CD = 12 cm and  AD = 14 cm
And ΔABC is a right triangle.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} By Pythagoras theorem, we have
AC2 = AB2 + BC2

{tex}A C = \sqrt { A B ^ { 2 } + B C ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 36 + 64 }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 100 } = 10 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}

 

Area of {tex}\Delta A B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times A B \times B C{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6 \times 8 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 24 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Now, in triangle ACD, Let a = 10 cm, b = 12 cm and c = 14 m

{tex}\therefore s = \frac { a + b + c } { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 10 + 12 + 14 } { 2 } = \frac { 36 } { 2 }{/tex} = 18 cm

Area of {tex}\Delta A C D = \sqrt { s ( s - a ) ( s - b ) ( s - c ) }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 18 ( 18 - 10 ) ( 18 - 12 ) ( 18 - 14 ) }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 18 \times 8 \times 6 \times 4 } = \sqrt { 3456 }{/tex}

{tex}= 58.787 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex} (approx.)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of quadrilateral ABCD = Area of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} + Area of {tex}\Delta A C D{/tex}

{tex}= 24 c m ^ { 2 } + 58.787 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}= 82.787 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex} (approx.)
Hence the area of given quadrilateral ABCD is 82.787 cm2.

Q 9

A rhombus shaped sheet with perimeter 40 cm and one diagonal 12 cm, is painted on both sides at the rate of Rs 5 per m2. Find the cost of painting.

Solution

Perimeter of rhombus = 40 cm
 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} side = 40
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} side {tex} = \frac{{40}}{4} = 10cm{/tex}
One diagonal = 12 cm
As rhombus is also a parallelogram, so it diagonal divide it into two congruent triangles of equal area.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of rhombus = 2(Area of triangle with sides 10cm, 10cm and 12cm)
So, let a = 10 cm, b = 10 cm and c = 12 cm
{tex}\therefore s = \frac{{a + b + c}}{2} = \frac{{10 + 10 + 12}}{2} = \frac{{32}}{2} = 16cm{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \sqrt {s(s - a)(s - b(s - c)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {(16(16 - 10)(16 - 10)(16 - 12)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {16 \times 6 \times 6 \times 4} = \sqrt {2304} = 48c{m^2}{/tex}
Now, area of rhombus ABCD = 2(area of {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex})
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 48 cm2 = 96 cm2.
Now, cost of painting both sides of rhombus shaped sheet ABCD
= Rs2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 96 = Rs960

Q 10

Find the area of the trapezium PQRS with height PQ given in Figure.

Solution

Draw RT {tex}\bot{/tex} PS from the figure, it is clear that
ST = PS - PT
= 12 m - 7 m
= 5 m
Now, from right triangle RTS, we have
RS2 = RT2 + ST2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RT2 = RS2 - ST2 = (13)2 - 52
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RT2 = 169 - 25 = 144 {tex}\Rightarrow RT = + \sqrt {144} = 12m{/tex}
Now area of trapezium PQRS
{tex} = (PS + QR) \times RT = \frac{1}{2}(12m + 7m) \times 12m{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times 19m \times 12m = \frac{1}{2} \times 228{m^2} = 114{m^2}{/tex}

sections.label: Exercise 12.4
sections.html:
Q 1

How much paper of each shade is needed to make a kite given in Figure, in which ABCD is a square with diagonal 44 cm?

Solution

Each diagonal of square = 44 cm
So, AC = BD = 44 cm
And as diagonal of a square bisect each other at right angles
So,
BO =  {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} BD  = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times{/tex} 44 = 22 cm

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of square ABCD = 2(area of {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex})

{tex}= 2 \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 44 \times 22 \right) = 2 ( 44 \times 11 ){/tex}

= 968 cm2.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Paper of Red shade needed to make the kite

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 968 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } \right) = 242 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Paper of yellow shade needed to make the kite = (242 + 242) = 484 cm2.

Let us find the area of a triangle with sides 20 cm, 20 cm and 14 cm which is at the bottom of the square ABCD.
a = 20 cm, b = 20 cm and c = 14 cm

Now, semi-perimeter

{tex}s = \frac { a + b + c } { 2 } = \frac { 20 + 20 + 14 } { 2 } = \frac { 54 } { 2 } = 27 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}

Area of {tex}\Delta = \sqrt { s ( s - a ) ( s - b) ( s - c ) }{/tex}     [Using Heron's Formula]

{tex}= \sqrt { 27 ( 27 - 20 ) ( 27 - 20 ) ( 27 - 14 ) }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 27 \times 7 \times 7 \times 13 } = 21 \sqrt { 39 }{/tex}

{tex}= 21 \times 6.245 = 131.15 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Paper of Green shade needed to make the kite

= (242 + 131.15) cm2 = 373.15 cm2.
Hence, paper of Red, yellow and green shade needed to make the kite is 242 cm2,484 cm2 and  373.15 cm2 respectively .

Q 2

The perimeter of a triangle is 50 cm. One side of a triangle is 4 cm longer than the smaller side and the third side is 6 cm less than twice the smaller side. Find the area of the triangle.

Solution

Let the smaller side of the triangle be x cm. therefore, the second side will be (x + 4) cm, and third side is (2x - 6) cm.
Now, perimeter of triangle = x (x + 4) + (2x - 6)
= (4x - 2) cm
Also, perimeter of triangle = 50 cm.
4x = 52; x = 52 ÷ 4 = 13
Therefore, the three sides are 13 cm, 17 cm, 20 cm
{tex}s = \frac{{13 + 17 + 20}}{2} = \frac{{50}}{2} = 25cm{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta = \sqrt {25(25 - 13)(25 - 17)(25 - 20)}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {25 \times 12 \times 8 \times 5} = \sqrt {5 \times 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5} {/tex}
{tex} = 5 \times 4 \times \sqrt {3 \times 2 \times 5} = 20\sqrt {30} c{m^2}{/tex}

Q 3

The area of a trapezium is 475 cm2 and the height is 19 cm. Find the lengths of its two parallel sides if one side is 4 cm greater than the other.

Solution

Area of trapezium {tex}= \frac{1}{2} \times{/tex}(Sum of the parallel side) {tex}\times{/tex} height
{tex}\Rightarrow \;475 = \frac{1}{2} \times (x + x + 4) \times 19cm{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2x + 4 = \frac{{950}}{{19}} = 50{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \;2x = 50 - 4 = 46;x = 46 \div 2 = 23{/tex}
Hence, the length of two parallel sides are 23 cm and (23 + 4) cm i.e., 23 cm and 27 cm.

Q 4

A rectangular plot is given for constructing a house, having a measurement of 40 m long and 15 m in the front. According to the laws, a minimum of 3 m, wide space should be left in the front and back each and 2 m wide space on each of other sides. Find the largest area where house can be constructed.

Solution

The length of the rectangular plot = 40 m
And the breath of the plot = 15 m.
As a minimum of 3 m wide space should be left in the front and back 2 m wide space each of other side, so the largest area where the house can be constructed.
= [40 - 2(3)] [15 - 2(2)] = 34 {tex}\times{/tex} 11 = 374m2

Q 5

A field is in the shape of a trapezium having parallel sides 90 m and 30 m. These sides meet the third side at right angles. The length of the fourth side is 100 m. If it costs Rs.4 to plough 1 m2 of the field, find the total cost of ploughing the field.

Solution

The two-parallel sides are AB = 90 cm and CD = 30 m. DM {tex}\bot{/tex} AB

Now, MB = AB – AM = 90 m – 30 m = 60 m.
BD = 100 m

 

In right triangle DMB, we have {tex}\;\angle{/tex}M = 90°
By using Pythagoras theorem
DB2 = DM2 + MB2

DM2 = DB2 – MB2 
= (100)2 – (60)2

DM2 = 10,000 – 3600 = 6400

{tex}\Rightarrow D M = + \sqrt { 6400 } = 80 \mathrm { m }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} The area of the field ABDC which is trapezium in shape {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times{/tex} (Sum of the parallel sides) {tex}\times{/tex} height

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times ( 90 + 30 ) \times 80 m ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 120 \times 80 = 4800 m ^ { 2 }{/tex}

Total cost of ploughing the field at the rate of ₹ 4 per m2 = ₹ (4800 × 4) = ₹ 19,200.
Hence the total cost of ploughing the field is ₹ 19200.

Q 6

In Fig., {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC has sides AB = 7.5 cm, AC = 6.5 cm and BC = 7 cm. On base BC a parallelogram DBCE of same area as that of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is constructed. Find the height DF of the parallelogram.

Solution

Now, first determine the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC
The sides of a triangle are given as, 
AB = a = 7.5 cm, BC = b = 7 cm and CA = c = 6.5 cm
Now, semi-perimeter of a triangle,
{tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{7.5+7+6.5}{2}=\frac{21}{2}{/tex} = 10.5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} [by Heron's formula]
{tex}=\sqrt{10.5(10.5-7.5)(10.5-7)(10.5-65)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10.5 \times 3 \times 3.5 \times 4}{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{441}{/tex} = 21 cm2 ...(i)
Now, area of parallelogram BCED = Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= BC {tex}\times{/tex} DF = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} DF
According the question,
Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC  is equal to the Area of parallelogram BCED..
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 21 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} DF [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} DF {tex}=\frac {21} {7}{/tex} = 3 cm
Hence, the height of parallelogram is 3 cm.

Q 7

The dimensions of a rectangle ABCD are 51 cm{tex}\times{/tex}25 cm. A trapezium PBCQ with its parallel sides QC and PB in the ratio 9:8, is cut off from the rectangle as shown in the figure. If the area of the trapezium PBCQ is {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}th part of the area of the rectangle, find the lengths of QC and PB.

Solution

Area of rectangle ABCD = AB {tex}\times{/tex} BC = 51 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 = 1275 cm2
As per given condition,
If the area of the trapezium PBCQ is {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} part of the area of the rectangle
Area of trapezium PBCQ = {tex}\frac{5}{6} \times 1275=\frac{6375}{6} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Let QC = 9x cm and PB = 8x cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of trapezium PBCQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(Q C+P B) \times B C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{6375}{6}=\frac{1}{2}(9 x+8 x) \times 25{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{17 x \times 25}{2}=\frac{6375}{6} \quad {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{6375}{6} \times \frac{2}{17 \times 25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} QC = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm = 45 cm and PB = 8 {tex}\times{/tex}5 cm = 40 cm

Q 8

A design is made on a rectangular tile of dimensions 50 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 70 cm as shown in Figure. The design shows 8 triangles, each of sides 26 cm, 17 cm and 25 cm. Find the total area of the design and the remaining area of the tile.

Solution

Given, A design is made on a rectangular tile of dimensions 50 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 70 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Area of rectangular tile = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 70 = 3500 cm2.
The sides of a design of one triangle be a = 25 cm, b = 17 cm and c = 26 cm.
Now, semi-perimeter, {tex}s = \frac { a + b + c } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 25 + 17 + 26 } { 2 } = \frac { 68 } { 2 } = 34 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta A B C = \sqrt { s ( s - a ) ( s - b ) ( s - c ) }{/tex}
[By Heron’s formula]
{tex}= \sqrt { 34 ( 34 - 25 ) ( 34 - 17 ) ( 34 - 26 ) } = \sqrt { 34 \times 9 \times 17 \times 8 }{/tex}
 {tex}= \sqrt { 17 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 17 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 }{/tex}
= 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
= 204 cm2.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Total area of eight triangles = 204 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 1632 cm2
The design shows 8 triangles
So, area of the design = Total area of eight triangles  = 1632 cm2
And, remaining area of the tile = Area of the rectangle – Area of the design
= 3500 cm2 – 1632 cm2
= 1868 cm2
Hence, the total area of the design is 1632 cm2 and the remaining area of the tile is 1868 cm2.
